title: Enhanced Fluid Index Modulation for Integrated Data and Energy Transfer
summary: Integrated data and energy transfer (IDET) is a promising technique for supporting sustainable low-power wireless networks. To improve both communication reliability and energy transfer efficiency, this paper investigates a fluid index modulation (FIM) assisted IDET system, where the base station employs a two-dimensional fluid antenna system (FAS) and the receiver adopts a power-splitting architecture. In FIM, the information bits are delivered not only from the modulation symbols, but also the index of antenna position. Under finite-alphabet signaling, the average harvested power, bit error rate (BER), and achievable data rate are derived in closed form. A joint optimization problem is formulated to maximize the average harvested power subject to BER and achievable rate constraints by jointly optimizing the port selection, precoding vector, and power splitting ratio. An alternating optimization framework is developed, where the precoding vector and port selection are obtained via a Riemannian augmented Lagrangian method (RALM) and block coordinate descent (BCD) algorithm, respectively. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves a superior rate-energy trade-off over benchmark schemes, while the proposed algorithm attains near-optimal performance with significantly lower complexity than exhaustive search.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a fluid index modulation (FIM) assisted IDET system with a fluid antenna system (FAS) and power-splitting receiver, deriving closed-form expressions for harvested power, BER, and data rate, and developing an alternating optimization framework (RALM + BCD) to jointly optimize port selection, precoding, and power splitting for enhanced rate-energy trade-off.
AI.motivation: To improve both communication reliability and energy transfer efficiency in integrated data and energy transfer (IDET) systems for sustainable low-power wireless networks.
AI.method: The paper designs a FIM-assisted IDET system with a 2D fluid antenna system (FAS) and power-splitting receiver. Closed-form expressions for average harvested power, BER, and achievable data rate are derived. A joint optimization problem is formulated, solved via an alternating optimization framework using Riemannian augmented Lagrangian method (RALM) for precoding/port selection and block coordinate descent (BCD) for power splitting ratio.
AI.result: The proposed scheme achieves superior rate-energy trade-off compared to benchmark schemes. The proposed algorithm attains near-optimal performance with significantly lower complexity than exhaustive search.
AI.conclusion: FIM-assisted IDET with FAS effectively enhances both communication reliability and energy harvesting efficiency, and the proposed optimization framework offers a practical near-optimal solution with low complexity.

title: Co-optimization of Diffusive and Tomographic Blur in Computed Axial Lithography via Experimental Kernel Identification
summary: Computed Axial Lithography is a volumetric additive manufacturing method that selectively cures photosensitive resin through the 3D superposition of patterns of light, offering advantages over layer-based processes including rapid print times, reduced layer artifacts, and compatibility with high-viscosity materials. However, diffusive effects, primarily those of free-radical quenchers such as oxygen, blur the boundary between cured and uncured regions, limiting resolution and preventing the reproduction of sharp, high-spatial-frequency features. By comparing micro-CT data to computational dose models convolved with kernels across a range of diffusivities, we establish a framework for extracting a single diffusion kernel from any standard uncorrected print to account for all observed deviations from the target. In this work, we correct diffusion-induced blurring by co-optimizing for its effects alongside the inherent blur of the computed tomography reconstruction, demonstrating improved fidelity over previous approaches of pre-compensating the target geometry via deconvolution.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method to correct diffusion-induced blurring in Computed Axial Lithography by co-optimizing for diffusive effects alongside CT reconstruction blur, using a single diffusion kernel extracted from standard prints via micro-CT comparison with computational dose models.
AI.motivation: Diffusive effects, primarily from free-radical quenchers like oxygen, blur the boundary between cured and uncured regions in volumetric additive manufacturing, limiting resolution and preventing reproduction of sharp, high-frequency features.
AI.method: Compare micro-CT data to computational dose models convolved with kernels across a range of diffusivities to extract a single diffusion kernel from uncorrected prints, then co-optimize for diffusion-induced blurring alongside CT reconstruction blur.
AI.result: Improved fidelity over previous pre-compensation approaches (e.g., deconvolution of target geometry) for correcting diffusion blur.
AI.conclusion: The co-optimization framework effectively corrects diffusion blur in Computed Axial Lithography, enabling better reproduction of sharp features.

title: Dynamic FDD for Spectrum Sharing in Non-Terrestrial Networks
comment: Submitted to IEEE for possible publication. The associated code for reproducibility with DOI is available at: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20307458
summary: Future 6G networks are envisioned to integrate low Earth orbit satellite mega-constellations to enable seamless global connectivity, particularly in underserved and remote areas. However, the deployment of dense mega-constellations introduces interference among satellites operating over shared frequency bands. This represents a rather new setup for studying spectrum sharing, which exacerbates the limited flexibility of conventional FDD systems based on fixed bands for downlink and uplink transmissions. We address this spectrum-sharing problem and propose dynamic re-assignment of FDD bands for improved interference management in dense deployments, as well as evaluate the performance gain of this approach. To this end, we formulate a joint optimization problem that incorporates dynamic band assignment, user scheduling, and power allocation in both directions. This non-convex mixed integer problem is solved using a combination of equivalence transforms, alternating optimization, and state-of-the-art industrial-grade mixed integer solvers. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed approach of dynamic FDD band assignment significantly enhances system performance over conventional FDD, achieving up to 30\% improvement in throughput in dense deployments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes dynamic FDD band reassignment to manage interference in dense LEO satellite mega-constellations for 6G networks, achieving up to 30% throughput improvement over conventional FDD through joint optimization of band assignment, scheduling, and power allocation.
AI.motivation: Dense LEO satellite mega-constellations in 6G cause severe interference over shared spectrum, and conventional FDD with fixed downlink/uplink bands lacks flexibility to manage this interference effectively.
AI.method: Formulates a joint non-convex mixed integer optimization problem (dynamic band assignment, user scheduling, power allocation) solved via equivalence transforms, alternating optimization, and industrial-grade mixed integer solvers.
AI.result: Dynamic FDD band assignment achieves up to 30% throughput improvement over conventional FDD in dense deployments.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic reassignment of FDD bands is an effective approach for interference management in dense LEO satellite networks, significantly enhancing system performance.

title: FUSE-Flow: A Decoupled Framework for Calibration and Stateless Real-Time Multi-View Point Cloud Fusion
comment: 13pages,5figures, the version to submit IEEE TMM
summary: Real-time multi-camera 3D reconstruction is a key foundation for immersive media, remote interaction and spatial computing. While synchronized camera arrays are widely adopted, achieving geometrically consistent and scalable real-time reconstruction remains challenging. A key challenge is the close linkage among extrinsic calibration, multi-view fusion and global optimization, which causes fluctuating reconstruction results, cumulative errors and poor system expandability. We propose a decoupled framework for calibration and stateless real-time multi-view point cloud fusion (FUSE-Flow), a framework with two collaborative components: geometry-aligned multi-view extrinsic calibration (GMAC) and reliability-guided multi-view point cloud fusion (FUSE). This split design avoids conflicting optimization objectives for targeted improvement. The GMAC module refines camera extrinsics via geometric constraints and multi-view reconstruction transformers, enabling accurate sparse-view calibration without calibration targets, dense images or global bundle adjustment. The FUSE module integrates confidence weighting and adaptive spatial hashing for stateless fusion, ensuring linear time and memory consumption. The two modules mutually reinforce each other: accurate camera poses boost fusion accuracy, and confidence-aware fusion corrects calibration biases. Validated on public datasets and real camera setups, FUSE-Flow outperforms mainstream real-time reconstruction methods in visual effect, dynamic stability and scalability, providing a practical solution for large-scale real-time 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: FUSE-Flow is a decoupled framework for real-time multi-camera 3D reconstruction, combining a geometry-aligned extrinsic calibration module (GMAC) and a reliability-guided point cloud fusion module (FUSE), achieving state-of-the-art performance in visual quality, stability, and scalability.
AI.motivation: Existing real-time multi-camera 3D reconstruction methods suffer from tight coupling between calibration, fusion, and optimization, leading to fluctuating results, cumulative errors, and poor scalability. The authors aim to decouple these processes for targeted improvements.
AI.method: The framework splits reconstruction into two modules: (1) GMAC refines camera extrinsics using geometric constraints and multi-view reconstruction transformers for sparse-view calibration without targets or bundle adjustment; (2) FUSE performs stateless point cloud fusion via confidence weighting and adaptive spatial hashing for linear time/memory usage.
AI.result: Validated on public datasets and real camera setups, FUSE-Flow outperforms mainstream real-time reconstruction methods in visual effect, dynamic stability, and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The decoupled design resolves conflicting optimization objectives, and the mutual reinforcement between accurate calibration and confidence-aware fusion provides a practical solution for large-scale real-time 3D reconstruction.

title: TGSD: Topology-Guided State-Space Diffusion for EEG Spatial Super-Resolution
summary: Low-density EEG is more suitable for wearable and IoT-based brain sensing, but sparse electrode sampling often lacks sufficient spatial information to characterize cross-regional neural activity. EEG spatial super-resolution aims to recover dense-channel EEG from sparse recordings, yet remains challenging because channel missingness typically occurs at the whole-channel level, spatiotemporal dependencies over the full electrode layout are often underexplored, and the mapping from sparse to dense signals is inherently ambiguous. To address these issues, we propose TGSD, a topology-guided state-space diffusion framework for EEG spatial super-resolution. TGSD first employs a Hierarchical Spatial Prior Encoder to learn topology-aware priors over the complete electrode layout by integrating local geometric relationships with region-level contextual information. Based on these priors and sparse observations, a Conditional State-Space Diffusion Reconstructor progressively generates missing-channel signals through reverse diffusion, while alternating temporal and channel-wise state-space modeling captures long-range temporal dynamics and inter-channel dependencies in a unified framework. Experiments on the SEED and PhysioNet MM/I datasets show that TGSD consistently outperforms representative baselines under different super-resolution factors in both reconstruction fidelity and downstream classification performance. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining topology-aware spatial priors with conditional diffusion for enhancing practical low-density EEG sensing in wearable and IoT scenarios. The official implementation code is available at https://github.com/jtggz/TGSD.

AI.tldr: TGSD is a topology-guided state-space diffusion framework that enhances low-density EEG signals to dense-channel quality by learning spatial priors over electrode layouts and using conditional diffusion with alternating temporal/channel modeling, outperforming baselines on SEED and PhysioNet datasets.
AI.motivation: Low-density EEG is more practical for wearable/IoT use but suffers from sparse spatial sampling, missing whole-channel information, and underexplored spatiotemporal dependencies across the full electrode layout.
AI.method: Proposes TGSD with (1) Hierarchical Spatial Prior Encoder for topology-aware priors integrating local geometry and region-level context, and (2) Conditional State-Space Diffusion Reconstructor that progressively generates missing-channel signals via reverse diffusion with alternating temporal and channel-wise state-space modeling.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on SEED and PhysioNet MM/I datasets across multiple super-resolution factors in both reconstruction fidelity and downstream classification performance.
AI.conclusion: Combining topology-aware spatial priors with conditional diffusion effectively enhances practical low-density EEG sensing for wearable and IoT scenarios.

title: CADET: A Modular Platform for Evaluating Distributed Cooperative Autonomy in Connected Autonomous Vehicles
summary:
Deep learning models are increasingly central to autonomous vehicle (AV) pipelines, yet their integration has traditionally followed a monolithic design where perception, planning, and control execute on a single onboard computer. This design overlooks the emerging paradigm of cooperative autonomy, where vehicles interact with roadside units (RSUs), edge servers, and cloud-hosted intelligence through vehicle-to-everything (V2X) connectivity. Cooperative perception and control improve safety and efficiency, but also introduce systems-level challenges: network latency, compute heterogeneity, and multi-tenant contention, all critically affect real-time decision-making. These challenges are further amplified by the increasing reliance on large foundation models, whose scale necessitates cloud deployment.
  We present CADET (Cooperative Autonomy through Distributed Experimentation Toolkit), a modular platform for systematic and reproducible evaluation of distributed cooperative autonomy systems under realistic deployment conditions. CADET decouples the AV stack into composable modules that can be flexibly deployed across vehicles, infrastructure, and edge/cloud tiers. The framework integrates state-of-the-art models, incorporates trace-driven network and workload emulation, and provides synchronized model-, system-, and task-level instrumentation. Through V2V and V2I experiments, we show that distributed deployment choices fundamentally shape safety, with V2V intent packets outperforming cloud-based perception and RSU-assisted perception sustaining safety until overloaded by concurrent requests. Although designed for AV pipelines, CADET also supports dataset-driven experimentation, enabling systems and ML researchers to benchmark distributed inference workloads independently of full vehicle simulation. CADET is open source, with code and demo available at https://nesl.github.io/cadet-web.

AI.tldr: CADET is a modular, open-source platform for evaluating distributed cooperative autonomy systems under realistic conditions, showing that deployment choices (V2V, V2I, cloud) significantly affect safety and performance in autonomous vehicle pipelines.
AI.motivation: Traditional AV pipelines use monolithic onboard designs that overlook cooperative autonomy via V2X connectivity. Emerging cooperative perception/control introduces challenges (latency, heterogeneity, contention) amplified by large foundation models needing cloud deployment, motivating a need for systematic evaluation.
AI.method: CADET decouples the AV stack into composable modules deployable across vehicles, infrastructure, and edge/cloud tiers, integrating trace-driven network/workload emulation and synchronized instrumentation across model, system, and task levels.
AI.result: V2V intent packets outperform cloud-based perception; RSU-assisted perception sustains safety until overloaded by concurrent requests. Distributed deployment choices fundamentally shape safety outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Distributed deployment is critical for AV safety; CADET enables reproducible benchmarking for both ML and systems researchers. Open-source release facilitates further study.

title: Early Detection of Alzheimer's Disease Using Explainable Machine Learning on Clinical Biomarkers: A Multi-Class Classification Study Using the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) Dataset
summary: Background: Alzheimer's disease (AD) affects over 55 million people worldwide. Accurate, interpretable detection of normal cognition (NC), mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and AD from routine clinical assessments remains a critical unmet need. Methods: An XGBoost classifier was developed for three-class detection using eight clinical features from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI): MMSE, CDR Global, CDR Sum of Boxes (CDR-SB), MoCA, FAQ, age, sex, and education. Hyperparameters were optimised using Optuna (50 trials); class imbalance was addressed with SMOTE. Performance was evaluated by macro AUC-ROC with 1,000-iteration bootstrap 95% confidence intervals, macro F1, balanced accuracy, and Cohen's kappa. SHAP values provided feature-level explainability. Results: The dataset comprised 1,641 baseline subjects (608 NC, 767 MCI, 266 AD). On five-fold cross-validation, mean macro AUC was 0.983 (SD 0.007), accuracy 0.944 (SD 0.006), and macro F1 0.929 (SD 0.008). On the held-out test set (n = 247), macro AUC was 0.982 (95% CI: 0.965--0.995), accuracy 0.943, balanced accuracy 0.932, macro F1 0.927, and Cohen's kappa 0.909. SHAP analysis identified CDR Global as the dominant predictor for NC and MCI, while CDR-SB and MMSE together drove AD classification. Conclusion: An explainable machine learning model trained on routine clinical assessments achieves near-perfect three-class Alzheimer's detection. SHAP analysis reveals clinically plausible, class-specific feature importance patterns supporting clinical validity. Future work will extend this framework with speech biomarkers for multimodal detection.

AI.tldr: An XGBoost model using 8 routine clinical features (e.g., MMSE, CDR) achieves near-perfect three-class classification (NC, MCI, AD) with macro AUC 0.982 on held-out test data. SHAP analysis shows CDR Global drives NC/MCI detection, while CDR-SB and MMSE drive AD classification.
AI.motivation: Over 55 million people worldwide have Alzheimer's disease, and there is an unmet need for accurate, interpretable detection of NC, MCI, and AD from routine clinical assessments.
AI.method: XGBoost classifier with 8 clinical features (MMSE, CDR Global, CDR-SB, MoCA, FAQ, age, sex, education) from ADNI; hyperparameter optimization via Optuna (50 trials); SMOTE for class imbalance; SHAP for explainability.
AI.result: On held-out test set (n=247): macro AUC 0.982 (95% CI: 0.965–0.995), accuracy 0.943, balanced accuracy 0.932, macro F1 0.927, Cohen's kappa 0.909. SHAP identified CDR Global as key for NC/MCI, CDR-SB and MMSE for AD.
AI.conclusion: An explainable ML model using routine clinical assessments achieves near-perfect three-class Alzheimer's detection with clinically plausible feature importance. Future work will incorporate speech biomarkers for multimodal detection.

title: Dive into the Scene: Breaking the Perceptual Bottleneck in Vision-Language Decision Making via Focus Plan Generation
summary: In embodied vision-language decision making tasks such as robotic manipulation and navigation, Vision-Language and Vision-Language-Action Models (VLMs & VLAs) are powerful tools with different benefits: VLMs are better at long-term planning, while VLAs are better at reactive control. However, their performance is limited by the same perceptual bottleneck: visual hallucinations arise due to the models' inability to distinguish task-relevant objects from distractors. In principle, accurate identification and focus on critical objects while filtering out irrelevant ones is the key to break this limitation. A straightforward solution is one-step focus: directly attending to essential objects. However, this approach proves ineffective because effective focus inherently requires deep scene understanding. To this end, we propose SceneDiver, a coarse-to-fine focus plan generation method for VLMs leveraging their long-term planning abilities, that first constructs a holistic scene graph to establish initial comprehension, then progressively decomposes the task into simpler sub-problems through an iterative cycle of recognition, understanding, and analysis. To enable reactive control, we also design a lightweight adapter for distilling the deliberate focus ability into VLAs. Evaluations on standard embodied AI benchmarks confirm that our method substantially reduces visual hallucinations for both VLMs and VLAs, while preserving computational efficiency in tasks requiring fast execution. Our code and data are released at: https://future-item.github.io/SceneDiver.

AI.tldr: SceneDiver proposes a coarse-to-fine focus plan generation method that reduces visual hallucinations in embodied vision-language tasks by first building a holistic scene graph, then iteratively decomposing tasks into simpler sub-problems for VLMs, and distilling this focus ability into VLAs via a lightweight adapter.
AI.motivation: VLMs and VLAs suffer from visual hallucinations due to their inability to distinguish task-relevant objects from distractors, limiting their performance in embodied decision-making tasks like robotic manipulation and navigation.
AI.method: SceneDiver uses a coarse-to-fine focus plan generation: (1) constructs a holistic scene graph for initial scene comprehension, (2) progressively decomposes tasks into simpler sub-problems through iterative recognition-understanding-analysis cycles, and (3) distills the deliberate focus ability into VLAs via a lightweight adapter for reactive control.
AI.result: Substantially reduces visual hallucinations for both VLMs and VLAs on standard embodied AI benchmarks, while preserving computational efficiency in tasks requiring fast execution.
AI.conclusion: Accurate identification and focus on critical objects while filtering out distractors via structured coarse-to-fine planning effectively breaks the perceptual bottleneck in embodied vision-language decision making.

title: Toward Pre-Deployment Assurance for Enterprise AI Agents: Ontology-Grounded Simulation and Trust Certification
summary: Pre-deployment verification of enterprise artificial intelligence (AI) agents remains a critical gap between large language model (LLM) capability benchmarking and production deployment. Post-deployment monitoring, human-in-the-loop controls, and prompt-level guardrails offer limited assurance once an agent is operating in production. We propose an ontology-grounded verification framework combining three components: an Agent Operational Envelope formalizing the certification space across permissions, domain constraints, safety properties, governance rules, and autonomy levels; an ontology-to-scenario generation pipeline that derives regulatory, operational, and adversarial test scenarios automatically; and a Trust Certificate carrying a machine-verifiable attestation with graduated deployment verdicts (Approved, Conditional, Rejected). A controlled pilot across four regulated industries (Fintech, Banking, Insurance, and Healthcare), instantiated as five industry-by-regulatory-regime cells across the United States and Vietnam, generated 1,800 scenarios evaluated against 125 primary-source regulatory requirements and 25 injected faults. Ontology-grounded generation (G4) achieved 48.3% regulatory coverage versus 33.1% for the persona-based baseline (corrected p = .0006) and the highest domain specificity (4.77/5.0; p = 2e-6). The coverage advantage over baseline and retrieval-augmented prompting was not robust after Bonferroni correction. Cross-validation across three LLM families (Claude Sonnet 4, Qwen 2.5 72B, Gemma 4 26B; 5,400 total scenarios) replicated the persona-versus-ontology pattern. The results establish ontology-grounded scenario generation as a credible complement to persona-based test suites for regulatory-intensive domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an ontology-grounded verification framework for pre-deployment AI agent certification, combining an Agent Operational Envelope, automated scenario generation, and Trust Certificates. Evaluated across four regulated industries (5,400 scenarios), ontology-grounded generation achieved 48.3% regulatory coverage vs 33.1% baseline (p=.0006) with highest domain specificity (4.77/5.0, p=2e-6), though the advantage over baseline and RAP was not robust after Bonferroni correction.
AI.motivation: Pre-deployment verification of enterprise AI agents remains a critical gap between LLM benchmarking and production deployment, as post-deployment monitoring, human-in-the-loop controls, and prompt-level guardrails offer limited assurance once an agent is in production.
AI.method: Three-component ontology-grounded verification framework: (1) Agent Operational Envelope formalizing certification space across permissions, domain constraints, safety, governance, and autonomy; (2) ontology-to-scenario generation pipeline for regulatory, operational, and adversarial tests; (3) Trust Certificate with machine-verifiable attestation (Approved/Conditional/Rejected). Evaluated across 5 industry-by-regulatory cells (Fintech, Banking, Insurance, Healthcare) in US and Vietnam.
AI.result: 1,800 scenarios evaluated against 125 regulatory requirements and 25 injected faults. Ontology-grounded generation (G4) achieved 48.3% regulatory coverage (vs 33.1% baseline, p=.0006) and highest domain specificity (4.77/5.0, p=2e-6). Cross-validation across 3 LLM families (5,400 total scenarios) replicated the pattern, but coverage advantage over baseline and RAP was not robust after Bonferroni correction.
AI.conclusion: Ontology-grounded scenario generation is established as a credible complement to persona-based test suites for regulatory-intensive domains, though the coverage advantage requires further investigation given Bonferroni correction results.

title: Airy Beam Dispersion in Near-Field Wideband Terahertz Communications
comment: 5 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
summary: This letter investigates Airy beam dispersion in near-field wideband terahertz communications. Unlike conventional focusing beams, whose dispersion mainly appears as focal-point migration, Airy beams exhibit frequency-dependent shifts of both the reference focusing point and the self-bending main-lobe trajectory. Based on the Fresnel diffraction integral, a closed-form trajectory expression is derived to characterize the dispersion behavior across subcarriers. Furthermore, a true-time-delay (TTD)-assisted Airy beamforming structure is developed to actively control the trajectory dispersion. By properly designing the time delay parameters, the proposed scheme can either generate frequency-dependent curved trajectory clusters for sensing-oriented scanning or suppress trajectory drift for reliable communication.

AI.tldr: This letter analyzes Airy beam dispersion in near-field wideband terahertz communications, deriving a closed-form trajectory expression and proposing a TTD-assisted beamforming structure to control frequency-dependent trajectory shifts for sensing or communication.
AI.motivation: Conventional focusing beams exhibit simple focal-point migration dispersion, but Airy beams show more complex frequency-dependent shifts in both reference focusing point and self-bending main-lobe trajectory, which need to be characterized and controlled.
AI.method: The authors derive a closed-form trajectory expression based on the Fresnel diffraction integral and develop a true-time-delay (TTD)-assisted Airy beamforming structure to actively control trajectory dispersion.
AI.result: The proposed TTD-assisted scheme can either generate frequency-dependent curved trajectory clusters for sensing-oriented scanning or suppress trajectory drift for reliable communication.
AI.conclusion: Airy beam dispersion in wideband terahertz communications can be effectively characterized and controlled via the proposed TTD-assisted beamforming approach, enabling dual functionality for sensing and communication.

title: L-TGVN: Leveraging Longitudinal Priors for Personalized Rapid MRI
summary: MRI provides excellent soft-tissue contrast without ionizing radiation, but long acquisition times increase patient discomfort while also raising exam costs and limiting scanner throughput. A common approach to reduce scan time is to acquire fewer measurements, which yields an ill-posed linear inverse problem; recovering diagnostic-quality images therefore requires incorporating prior knowledge beyond the measured data. In follow-up exams, the most recent prior scan of a patient can provide a highly informative subject-specific context, but practical use is complicated by temporal changes (including pathology progression), misalignment between scans, and protocol drift across acquisitions. In this work, we introduce L-TGVN, a Longitudinal Trust-Guided Variational Network that leverages prior scans as side information to reconstruct the current scan from heavily undersampled measurements. Crucially, L-TGVN constrains the influence of prior scans to be consistent with the acquired measurements. Unlike many existing longitudinal reconstruction methods, it does not require explicit pre-registration between prior and current scans. It further accommodates differences in acquisition protocols across visits (e.g., changes in sequence parameters). We evaluate L-TGVN against matched-capacity baselines, including prior-guided methods and methods that do not use longitudinal priors, and observe consistent improvements in standard quantitative metrics together with better preservation of fine structures at challenging accelerations. Source code is available at github.com/sodicksonlab/L-TGVN.

AI.tldr: L-TGVN is a longitudinal trust-guided variational network that leverages prior patient scans as side information to reconstruct MRI images from heavily undersampled measurements, without requiring explicit registration or consistent acquisition protocols across visits.
AI.motivation: MRI acquisition times are long, causing patient discomfort, higher costs, and limited throughput. Undersampling leads to ill-posed inverse problems. Prior scans contain rich subject-specific context but are underutilized due to temporal changes, misalignment, and protocol drift.
AI.method: L-TGVN (Longitudinal Trust-Guided Variational Network) incorporates prior scans as side information in a variational network, constraining the prior's influence to be consistent with current measurements. It does not require explicit pre-registration and handles inter-scan protocol differences.
AI.result: L-TGVN outperforms matched-capacity baselines (both prior-guided and non-prior methods) in standard quantitative metrics and better preserves fine structures under challenging accelerations.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging longitudinal priors with trust-guided constraints improves MRI reconstruction quality under heavy undersampling, offering practical advantages by removing requirements for registration and protocol consistency.

title: AgenticDiffusion: Agentic Diffusion-based Path Planning for Vision-Based UAV Navigation
summary: Indoor UAV navigation requires efficient exploration, scene understanding, and reliable trajectory execution under limited field-of-view observations. Existing vision-based navigation frameworks typically rely on single-view observations, limiting their ability to reason about occlusions, target visibility, and global scene structure. In this work, we propose AgenticDiffusion, a multi-view UAV navigation framework that coordinates language-guided reasoning, open-vocabulary target grounding, vision-based diffusion planning, and NMPC within a unified aerial navigation pipeline. Given a natural language instruction and synchronized first-person-view (FPV) and top-view observations, the framework determines the most informative viewpoint for navigation and generates a mission plan prior to trajectory execution. The targets are localized using an open-vocabulary grounding model, after which viewpoint-specific diffusion planners generate navigation trajectories for UAV execution. Using complementary viewpoints, the proposed framework reduces repeated target exploration and improves navigation efficiency in cluttered indoor environments. The framework was validated in four real-world UAV navigation scenarios involving adaptive viewpoint selection, multi-stage mission execution, long-horizon navigation, and safe landing-site selection. The experimental results demonstrated an overall mission success rate of 80% in 40 real-world trials, while the diffusion planners achieved a trajectory generation success rate of 100%.

AI.tldr: AgenticDiffusion is a multi-view UAV navigation framework that combines language-guided reasoning, open-vocabulary target grounding, diffusion-based planning, and NMPC control, using both FPV and top-view observations to improve exploration efficiency and mission success in cluttered indoor environments, achieving 80% success rate in 40 real-world trials.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-based UAV navigation frameworks rely on single-view observations, which limits their ability to reason about occlusions, target visibility, and global scene structure in indoor environments.
AI.method: The framework coordinates four key components: (1) language-guided reasoning to select the most informative viewpoint, (2) open-vocabulary target grounding for flexible object detection, (3) viewpoint-specific diffusion planners for trajectory generation, and (4) NMPC for trajectory execution, using synchronized FPV and top-view camera inputs.
AI.result: Validated in four real-world scenarios (adaptive viewpoint selection, multi-stage mission, long-horizon navigation, safe landing-site selection). Achieved 80% overall mission success rate in 40 real-world trials, and 100% trajectory generation success rate from diffusion planners.
AI.conclusion: AgenticDiffusion demonstrates that multi-view coordination with diffusion-based planning effectively reduces repeated target exploration and improves navigation efficiency in cluttered indoor environments, offering a robust unified pipeline for autonomous UAV navigation.

title: Novel Aspects of IEEE SA P3109 Arithmetic Formats for Machine Learning
summary: The IEEE P3109 draft standard defines a parameterized family of binary floating-point formats and associated operations, with a focus on facilitating machine learning. These formats allow efficient and consistent representation of values in a small number of bits. The defined formats are parameterized over width and precision in bits, signedness, and the presence of infinities. Operations are defined by decoding floating-point values to the set of closed extended reals: the reals augmented with positive and negative infinity and NaN (Not a Number). Explicit treatment of NaN and infinite operands ensures that only real arithmetic is invoked in operation definitions. Extensive rounding and saturation modes are defined; stochastic rounding is included. Operations are exception-free, accelerating throughput, with exceptional situations communicated through return values, e.g., NaN. Operations on blocks of values sharing a common scale factor are defined in terms of the underlying operations in a uniform manner. System vendors may describe approximate implementations via a novel scale-invariant measure, akin to units in the last place, called kappa-approximation. Standard function definitions and various other properties are mechanically verified and generated using formal specifications.

AI.tldr: IEEE P3109 defines a parameterized family of binary floating-point formats for machine learning, with extensive rounding/saturation modes, exception-free operations, and formally verified specifications including a novel kappa-approximation measure for approximate implementations.
AI.motivation: To create a standardized, efficient binary floating-point format family tailored for machine learning workloads, enabling consistent representation in few bits with flexible parameterization and exception-free throughput.
AI.method: Defines formats parameterized over width, precision, signedness, and infinity presence; decodes values to closed extended reals; uses stochastic rounding and extensive rounding/saturation modes; communicates exceptions via return values (e.g., NaN); introduces kappa-approximation for approximate implementations; mechanically verified via formal specifications.
AI.result: A draft standard with mechanically verified and formally specified operations, enabling efficient ML-oriented floating-point arithmetic with consistent semantics across parameterized formats.
AI.conclusion: IEEE P3109 provides a rigorous, formally verified standard for low-bit-width floating-point formats optimized for machine learning, balancing flexibility, efficiency, and mathematical soundness.

title: Weakly Supervised Incremental Segmentation via Semantic Anchors and Spatial Arbitration
summary: Weakly Incremental Learning for Semantic Segmentation (WILSS) suffers from the continuous introduction of noisy supervision, which progressively corrupts class-level representations, leading to severe feature drift and semantic corruption, thereby causing newly learned classes to overwrite old ones. To address these issues, we propose a drift-resilient WILSS approach, named SASA, designed to stabilize semantic learning via Semantic Anchors and Spatial Arbitration. Specifically, at the representation level, we introduce semantic anchors of learnable tokens as rigid class-level references to preserve long-term semantic identity. Complementary to this, an elastic residual adaptation facilitates controlled, instance-specific refinement, ensuring a stable yet flexible learning trajectory. At the supervision level, we develop a Spatial Label Arbitration mechanism that performs geometry-aware decisions to directly filter unreliable signals and enforce a strict "one object, one class" constraint. By synergistically stabilizing representations and improving supervision reliability, SASA effectively mitigates feature drift under weak supervision. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly in challenging multi-step incremental settings. The code is available at https://github.com/ZhonggaiWang/SASA.

AI.tldr: SASA introduces semantic anchors (learnable tokens) and spatial label arbitration to combat feature drift and noisy supervision in Weakly Incremental Learning for Semantic Segmentation.
AI.motivation: Weakly Incremental Learning for Semantic Segmentation suffers from progressively corrupting class-level representations due to noisy supervision, causing feature drift and new classes overwriting old ones.
AI.method: (1) Semantic anchors (learnable tokens) as rigid class-level references with elastic residual adaptation for instance-specific refinement. (2) Spatial Label Arbitration for geometry-aware filtering of unreliable signals and enforcing "one object, one class" constraint.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on standard benchmarks, especially in challenging multi-step incremental settings.
AI.conclusion: SASA effectively mitigates feature drift under weak supervision by synergistically stabilizing representations and improving supervision reliability.

title: Stumbling Into AI Emotional Dependence: How Routine AI Interactions Reshape Human Connection
summary: Public discourse and emerging policy typically assume that AI emotional support is a deliberate act: a lonely user consciously seeking comfort from a dedicated companion chatbot. In this paper, we draw on emerging empirical evidence and argue that this picture is inaccurate on two accounts, both in how AI emotional support arises and how it shapes future behavior. First, AI emotional support commonly emerges incidentally within task-oriented interactions on general-purpose platforms, much as workplace friendships deepen through collaboration. Second, these incidental encounters are path-dependent: positive experiences of AI emotional support update people's beliefs about AI's emotional capabilities and redirect their choices for future emotional support, increasing preference for AI and decreasing preference for humans. We review recent evidence, including a large-scale longitudinal study conducted in collaboration with OpenAI, showing that daily five-minute conversations with an AI about personal issues over 28 days led to a 10.3% decrease in the preference for seeking support from humans and an 11.6% increase in the preference for AI. These findings suggest that current policy, focused on companion apps and isolated interactions, cannot adequately protect human connection. Instead, effective regulations should extend to general-purpose AI systems and address cumulative, trajectory-level changes in how people seek support. Recognizing how people stumble into AI emotional support and how those encounters redirect human connections over time is essential to safeguarding human well-being.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that AI emotional support often emerges incidentally during task-oriented interactions, not from deliberate chatbot use, and that these encounters create a path-dependent shift where people increasingly prefer AI over humans for emotional support, citing a 28-day study showing 10.3% decreased preference for humans and 11.6% increased preference for AI.
AI.motivation: To challenge the prevailing assumption that AI emotional support is a deliberate, conscious act (like using a companion chatbot), and to reveal how it actually emerges incidentally during general-purpose AI interactions, with significant implications for human connection and well-being.
AI.method: The authors review emerging empirical evidence, including a large-scale longitudinal study in collaboration with OpenAI, where participants engaged in daily five-minute conversations with an AI about personal issues over 28 days, measuring changes in preferences for seeking emotional support from humans versus AI.
AI.result: After 28 days of daily AI conversations about personal issues, there was a 10.3% decrease in preference for seeking support from humans and an 11.6% increase in preference for AI, demonstrating path-dependent shifts in support-seeking behavior.
AI.conclusion: Current policies focused on companion apps and isolated interactions are insufficient. Effective regulation must extend to general-purpose AI systems and address cumulative, trajectory-level changes in how people seek emotional support to protect human connection.

title: Geometry-Structured Channel Reconstruction for Conventional and Fluid Antenna Systems: Bayesian Inference and Fundamental Limits
summary: Accurate channel state information (CSI) acquisition is critical for exploiting the spatial flexibility of fluid antenna systems (FASs). However, port selection and transmission optimization require CSI over a large number of candidate port positions, making direct port-wise estimation prohibitively costly in terms of pilot overhead. This paper addresses this challenge through geometry-structured channel reconstruction, which exploits the fact that the port-domain CSI can be parameterized by a small number of dominant propagation paths. We first establish fundamental mean square error (MSE) and normalized MSE (NMSE) benchmarks for both geometry-structured and unstructured channel reconstruction, providing analytical references for evaluating the intrinsic benefit of geometric modeling in conventional antenna systems and FASs. Motivated by the strong spatial correlation induced by densely distributed fluid antenna ports, we further propose a Bayesian reconstruction framework, termed geometry-structured expectation-maximization approximate message passing (GS-EM-AMP). The proposed algorithm incorporates geometric channel structure into the EM-AMP procedure and adaptively learns unknown statistical parameters from noisy observations. Numerical results demonstrate that GS-EM-AMP achieves near-bound reconstruction accuracy while maintaining strong robustness against steering-domain correlation, thereby offering an efficient and reliable solution for large-scale CSI acquisition in FASs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a geometry-structured Bayesian reconstruction framework (GS-EM-AMP) for efficient CSI acquisition in fluid antenna systems, leveraging spatial correlation to reduce pilot overhead while achieving near-bound accuracy.
AI.motivation: Accurate CSI acquisition in FASs is costly due to the large number of candidate port positions; direct port-wise estimation incurs prohibitively high pilot overhead.
AI.method: Proposes GS-EM-AMP, a Bayesian reconstruction framework that integrates geometric channel structure into the EM-AMP procedure and adaptively learns unknown statistical parameters from noisy observations.
AI.result: GS-EM-AMP achieves near-bound reconstruction accuracy and exhibits strong robustness against steering-domain correlation.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-structured reconstruction offers an efficient and reliable solution for large-scale CSI acquisition in FASs.

title: Scaling Datasets for Multi-Sensor, Multi-Agent, and Multi-Domain Learning in Autonomous Systems
summary: Existing datasets cannot support large-scale learning in multi-agent, multi-sensor, or multi-domain autonomy, where diversity and coordination are essential. We present a modular dataset generation pipeline that creates terabyte-scale, ground-truth-labeled data for ground, aerial, and infrastructure-based systems using the AVstack framework and CARLA simulator. Supporting single- and multi-agent configurations with flexible sensor suites, the pipeline enables controllable experimentation across challenging conditions. Representative perception and fusion studies show how generated data can support application-specific training and collaborative autonomy.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a modular pipeline for generating large-scale, labeled multi-agent multi-sensor datasets using CARLA and AVstack to support collaborative autonomy research.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets lack the scale and diversity needed for multi-agent, multi-sensor, or multi-domain autonomy learning, where coordination and variety are critical.
AI.method: A modular dataset generation pipeline built on AVstack and CARLA simulator, producing terabyte-scale ground-truth-labeled data for ground, aerial, and infrastructure systems, with flexible sensor suites and controllable conditions.
AI.result: Demonstrated through representative perception and fusion studies that the generated data supports application-specific training and collaborative autonomy.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline enables large-scale, diverse data generation to advance multi-agent collaborative autonomy research.

title: CLAW: Learning Continuous Latent Action World Models via Adversarial Latent Regularization
summary: We introduce CLAW, a fully end-to-end self-supervised framework for learning a world model jointly with continuous latent action representations directly from action-free videos. Our approach leverages adversarial latent regularization and diffusion-based video generation to capture structured and semantically meaningful action representations while modeling rich, predictive environment dynamics, without relying on any action labels or annotations. By simultaneously training the Latent Action Model and world model, CLAW learns to reason about how inferred actions induce environment transitions from visual observations alone. We show that the resulting latent action world model supports both imitation learning from observation and goal-directed planning. In imitation learning, latent actions extracted from raw videos enable behavior cloning. For planning, CLAW generates sequences of latent actions and maps them to executable actions to reach desired goals. Extensive experiments across diverse tasks and embodiments demonstrate that CLAW produces semantically meaningful latent action representations, supports effective action transfer, and enables planning and imitation from observation, outperforming existing methods.

AI.tldr: CLAW is a self-supervised framework that learns a world model with continuous latent action representations directly from action-free videos, enabling imitation learning and goal-directed planning without action labels.
AI.motivation: To learn structured action representations and world models from videos without requiring action labels or annotations, enabling broader applicability of imitation learning and planning from observation.
AI.method: Uses adversarial latent regularization and diffusion-based video generation to learn a Latent Action Model jointly with a world model in an end-to-end self-supervised manner from action-free videos.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods in producing semantically meaningful latent action representations, supporting action transfer, and enabling planning and imitation from observation across diverse tasks and embodiments.
AI.conclusion: CLAW demonstrates that fully self-supervised learning of latent action world models from raw videos is feasible and effective for both imitation learning and goal-directed planning.

title: Position: Deployed Reinforcement Learning should be Continual
comment: Accepted to the ICML 2026 Position Paper Track. See https://icml.cc/virtual/2026/poster/67195
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has received increasing attention and adoption in real-world use cases. Most of these systems follow a train-then-fix paradigm, where trained agents do not learn while interacting with the world until performance degrades and retraining becomes necessary. In this position paper, we argue that deploying an agent that is incapable of optimality, but receives an evaluative reward signal, is inherently a continual RL problem. We identify four sources of non-stationarity after deployment that necessitate never-ending learning, and highlight why the best deployed agents never stop adapting. We analyze successful examples of continual RL in the real world, and present the community with the advantages and measures to move away from the current train-then-fix paradigm.

AI.tldr: This position paper argues that deploying RL agents in real-world settings is inherently a continual learning problem due to four sources of non-stationarity, advocating for a shift away from the train-then-fix paradigm toward never-ending adaptation.
AI.motivation: The current train-then-fix paradigm for RL agents is inefficient and suboptimal for real-world deployment, as agents do not continuously learn and adapt to changes in their environment.
AI.method: The paper identifies four sources of non-stationarity after deployment that necessitate continual RL, analyzes successful real-world examples of continual RL, and presents advantages and measures for moving beyond the train-then-fix paradigm.
AI.result: The paper identifies four sources of non-stationarity that necessitate never-ending learning, and highlights why the best deployed agents never stop adapting.
AI.conclusion: The paper advocates that real-world RL deployment should be viewed as a continual RL problem, and provides the community with reasons and measures to transition from the train-then-fix paradigm.

title: Intra-Modal Neighbors Never Lie: Rectifying Inter-Modal Noisy Correspondence via Graph-Based Intra-Modal Reasoning
summary: Large-scale web-harvested datasets have fueled the progress of cross-modal retrieval but inevitably suffer from noisy correspondence, which severely degrades model generalization. Existing methods primarily address this by filtering out noise or seeking a substitute label, yet they predominantly remain bound by a "Discrete Selection" paradigm. We argue that relying on a single discrete proxy induces Single-Point Fragility and Discretization Error. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel framework, Intra-modal Neighbor-aware Noise Rectification (IN2R), which shifts the paradigm from searching for a substitute to synthesizing a reliable supervision target. Leveraging the intrinsic geometric stability of intra-modal data, IN2R employs a Graph Refiner to perform relational reasoning over neighbors retrieved from a dynamic Cross-Model Memory. Instead of propagating discrete labels, our method synthesizes a continuous, soft prototype that reflects the consensus of the local semantic neighborhood, effectively rectifying inter-modal misalignment. Extensive experiments on Flickr30K, MS-COCO, and CC152K demonstrate that IN2R significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Our code and pre-trained models are publicly available at https://github.com/liuyyy111/IN2R.

AI.tldr: IN2R is a new cross-modal retrieval framework that replaces discrete label selection with continuous soft prototype synthesis via intra-modal graph refinement, achieving SOTA on Flickr30K, MS-COCO, and CC152K.
AI.motivation: Existing noisy correspondence methods rely on a "Discrete Selection" paradigm (filtering or substituting a single label), which suffers from Single-Point Fragility and Discretization Error.
AI.method: IN2R uses a Graph Refiner to perform relational reasoning over neighbors from a dynamic Cross-Model Memory, synthesizing a continuous soft prototype that reflects local semantic neighborhood consensus, rather than propagating discrete labels.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on Flickr30K, MS-COCO, and CC152K datasets.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from discrete selection to continuous soft prototype synthesis via intra-modal geometric stability effectively rectifies inter-modal misalignment from noisy correspondence.

title: Thinking Through Signs: PEEL as a Semiotic Scaffolding for Epistemically Accountable AI-Enabled Research
summary: Large language models are reshaping research practice while quietly eroding researchers epistemic accountability. This commentary introduces PEEL - Protocols for Epistemically Engaged Literacy in AI, a working scaffolding that combines deterministic distant reading via Voyant Tools with LLM interpretation via Claude, grounded in Peircean semiotics and abductive reasoning. Applied to AI-generated condensations of three source texts, PEEL reveals systematic distortions in quantity, term frequency, and epistemic voice that are invisible without non-AI measurement -- and yields three design implications: deterministic instruments must accompany AI tools; fluency is not fidelity; epistemic authority must be designed in, not assumed.

AI.tldr: Introduces PEEL, a framework combining deterministic distant reading tools with LLM interpretation to reveal systematic distortions in AI-generated text condensations, arguing that fluency does not equal fidelity and epistemic authority must be deliberately designed.
AI.motivation: To address the erosion of epistemic accountability caused by LLMs reshaping research practice, by developing a method to detect hidden distortions in AI-generated text.
AI.method: PEEL (Protocols for Epistemically Engaged Literacy in AI) combines Voyant Tools (deterministic distant reading) with Claude (LLM interpretation), grounded in Peircean semiotics and abductive reasoning, applied to AI-generated condensations of three source texts.
AI.result: PEEL reveals systematic distortions in quantity, term frequency, and epistemic voice that remain invisible without non-AI measurement tools.
AI.conclusion: Three design implications: deterministic instruments must accompany AI tools; fluency is not fidelity; epistemic authority must be designed in, not assumed.

title: A sharp analysis of Root-MUSIC: locations of correct and extraneous roots
summary: Root-MUSIC is a spectral estimation algorithm that approximates the unknown signal frequencies by constructing a high-degree polynomial and finding a subset of roots which are closest to the complex unit circle. Previous works found asymptotic expectation formulas for the performance of Root-MUSIC under the implicit assumption that the aforementioned root selection criterion does not select extraneous roots -- those which are unrelated to the correct parameters. This paper removes the need for this assumption by showing all extraneous roots lie outside an annulus of a certain thickness and therefore are not selected by the algorithm. This paper also provides sharp, non-asymptotic, and explicit error bounds for the correct roots in terms of fundamental model parameters. All results hold under a natural separation condition on the correct signal frequencies and are applicable in both the single- and multi-snapshot models. More specifically, in the multi-snapshot model, we prove that Root-MUSIC estimates the frequencies with error at most $O(σ/(m \sqrt n))$, where $σ^2$ is the noise variance, $m$ is the number of sensors, and $n$ is the number of snapshots. A novelty of this non-asymptotic bound is the explicit $1/m$ decay, which indicates that there is a significant advantage in utilizing additional sensors. Numerical simulations confirm our theory. The main mathematical insight of this paper is a geometric property of the Root-MUSIC polynomial: its correct roots are highly stable to noise while its extraneous roots must lie outside of an annulus.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that extraneous roots of the Root-MUSIC polynomial lie outside a specific annulus and thus are never selected by the algorithm, removing a key implicit assumption. It provides sharp, non-asymptotic error bounds showing Root-MUSIC estimates frequencies with error O(σ/(m√n)), highlighting a 1/m decay advantage from additional sensors.
AI.motivation: Previous Root-MUSIC analyses assumed the root selection criterion does not pick extraneous roots (unrelated to correct signal parameters). This paper removes that assumption by proving extraneous roots are never selected, enabling rigorous performance guarantees.
AI.method: The paper establishes a geometric property of the Root-MUSIC polynomial: correct roots are highly stable to noise, while extraneous roots must lie outside an annulus of a certain thickness, preventing their selection. Non-asymptotic error bounds are derived under a natural separation condition on signal frequencies.
AI.result: Root-MUSIC estimates frequencies with error at most O(σ/(m√n)), where σ² is noise variance, m is number of sensors, and n is number of snapshots. The explicit 1/m decay demonstrates significant advantage from using additional sensors.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the first rigorous non-asymptotic analysis of Root-MUSIC without assuming extraneous root exclusion, applicable to both single- and multi-snapshot models. Numerical simulations confirm the theoretical findings.

title: KD-NVC: A Search-and-Distill Framework to Accelerate Neural Video Coding
comment: This manuscript is submitted to IEEE Transactions
summary: While neural video coding (NVC) has achieved remarkable rate-distortion performance, real-time decoding on edge devices has become an important demand but remains limited by high complexity. Knowledge distillation (KD) is widely used for model acceleration, yet its application to NVC faces critical challenges. Specifically, the heterogeneity of NVC sub-modules renders uniform architectural reduction suboptimal, necessitating a per-module design for better rate-distortion-speed trade-off. However, searching for diverse architectures via existing neural architecture search (NAS) algorithms is unaffordable due to the expensive training cost of neural video codecs. Moreover, after the lightweight architecture is determined, existing distillation methods overlook the feature-energy sparsity induced by the rate-constraint, which is essential for maintaining compression performance. To address these issues, we propose a two-stage distillation framework KD-NVC. In the first stage, we introduce an acceleration-efficiency-based neural architecture search (AE-NAS) algorithm. It explores the module-wise Pareto frontier to adaptively allocate the acceleration budget across heterogeneous modules. Also, it introduces the acceleration-efficiency metric to determine the final student architecture without practically training all architecture-level candidates. In the second stage, we design an energy-aware feature distillation (EFD) loss that aligns the spatially-aggregated feature-energy signatures between the teacher and student codecs, transferring the rate-induced sparsity patterns for better compression efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms existing codec-oriented distillation methods, and achieves 69 FPS decoding at 1080p on RTX 5060 while maintaining comparable RD performance to VTM-LDB.

AI.tldr: KD-NVC is a two-stage distillation framework for neural video coding that uses acceleration-efficiency-based NAS for module-wise lightweight architecture design and energy-aware feature distillation to maintain compression performance, achieving 69 FPS 1080p decoding on RTX 5060 with VTM-LDB-comparable RD performance.
AI.motivation: Real-time decoding of neural video codecs on edge devices is limited by high complexity. Existing KD approaches fail due to heterogeneous sub-modules requiring per-module design, unaffordable NAS training costs, and overlook of rate-induced feature-energy sparsity in distillation.
AI.method: Two-stage distillation: (1) AE-NAS explores module-wise Pareto frontiers to allocate acceleration budgets and selects student architecture via acceleration-efficiency metric without full training; (2) Energy-aware feature distillation (EFD) loss aligns spatially-aggregated feature-energy signatures between teacher and student to transfer rate-induced sparsity patterns.
AI.result: Outperforms existing codec-oriented distillation methods; achieves 69 FPS decoding at 1080p on RTX 5060 with comparable RD performance to VTM-LDB.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the heterogeneity, training cost, and sparsity-awareness challenges in NVC distillation, enabling practical real-time decoding on edge devices.

title: CoPark: Learning Reactive Parking via Self-Play
summary: Learning a single policy that reaches a goal with high geometric precision while interacting safely with nearby agents poses conflicting objectives. Precision favors commitment to a fixed geometric plan, whereas interaction requires immediate deviation when another agent intrudes, causing policies optimized for one objective to often fail at the other. We study this problem in the context of reactive autonomous parking, where multiple vehicles must reach assigned slots with sub-meter terminal accuracy while remaining responsive to neighboring vehicles throughout the maneuver. We propose CoPark, a multi-agent self-play RL approach built on a residual-policy architecture. A precomputed offline plan provides a fixed action prior, while a residual head learns the reactive corrections. The residual policy learns behaviors under self-play, where data and scripting fall short, while the fixed prior holds the slot-frame geometry that pure policies struggle to reach reliably. The key design is a partner-threat-modulated, channel-asymmetric release of the prior. A continuous threat signal shifts authority of the longitudinal channel to the residual head to enable yielding, while the lateral channel remains anchored to the precomputed reference to preserve sub-meter slot alignment. A closed-loop refinement layer corrects residual terminal error from action-grid discretization. We train our policy on six parking lots and evaluate zero-shot on our new reactive-parking benchmark spanning Dragon Lake Parking (DLP) and DeepScenario Open 3D (DSC3D). CoPark achieves ~70-85% success with only 3-6% collision rate, substantially outperforming classical, imitation-learning, and large-scale RL baselines. Importantly, the results demonstrate emergent interaction behaviors such as reverse-yielding, mid-maneuver yielding, tight-corridor passing, and queuing.

AI.tldr: CoPark is a multi-agent self-play RL approach for reactive autonomous parking that uses a residual-policy architecture with a fixed offline plan prior and a learned residual head. A partner-threat-modulated, channel-asymmetric release mechanism allows longitudinal yielding while preserving lateral geometric precision, achieving ~70-85% success with only 3-6% collision rate, outperforming classical, IL, and large-scale RL baselines.
AI.motivation: Learning a single policy that achieves high geometric precision for goal reaching while safely interacting with nearby agents poses conflicting objectives (precision vs. reactivity), causing policies optimized for one objective to often fail at the other. The paper addresses this in reactive autonomous parking where multiple vehicles must reach assigned slots with sub-meter accuracy while remaining responsive to neighbors.
AI.method: CoPark uses a residual-policy architecture with: (1) a precomputed offline plan as fixed action prior, (2) a residual head learning reactive corrections via self-play RL, (3) partner-threat-modulated channel-asymmetric release (longitudinal channel shifts to residual for yielding, lateral stays anchored for precision), and (4) a closed-loop refinement layer correcting terminal error from action-grid discretization.
AI.result: Achieves ~70-85% success with only 3-6% collision rate on six parking lots (zero-shot on DLP and DSC3D benchmarks), substantially outperforming classical, imitation-learning, and large-scale RL baselines. Demonstrates emergent interaction behaviors: reverse-yielding, mid-maneuver yielding, tight-corridor passing, and queuing.
AI.conclusion: The channel-asymmetric release mechanism successfully resolves the precision vs. reactivity conflict in multi-agent parking. The residual policy learns emergent cooperative behaviors under self-play where data and scripting fall short, while the fixed prior maintains geometric accuracy that pure policies struggle to achieve reliably.

title: Pseudospectral Bounds for Transient Amplification in Coupled Gradient Descent
comment: 11 pages, 3 tables. Accepted as poster at HiLD 2026 (4th Workshop on High-dimensional Learning Dynamics, ICML 2026)
summary: Coupled gradient descent--where the update of one parameter block depends on another--underlies bilevel optimization, two-time-scale stochastic approximation, and adversarial training. When the coupled Jacobian is block-triangular, asymptotic stability is governed by the spectral radii of the diagonal blocks, yet transient amplification before convergence can be arbitrarily large due to non-normality. We develop a sharp pseudospectral theory for such block-triangular Jacobians, proving that the Kreiss constant satisfies $K(J) \leq 2/(1-γ) + \|C\|/(4(1-γ))$ when the diagonal blocks are symmetric with spectral radii at most $γ< 1$, and we establish matching minimax lower bounds. We characterize the critical coupling threshold for spectral instability and extend the analysis to nearly self-referential systems via a Neumann-series perturbation framework. As a consequence, we obtain a finite-horizon iteration-complexity bound of $O(K(J)^2 \log(1/δ))$ for stochastic coupled descent. Framed as scaling laws for non-stationary two-time-scale optimization, our results expose a non-asymptotic, instance-dependent regime of high-dimensional learning dynamics that is invisible to spectral-radius analysis. Experiments on linear-quadratic problems, IQC-based comparisons, and neural-network training confirm the theory.

AI.tldr: This paper develops sharp pseudospectral theory for block-triangular Jacobians in coupled gradient descent, proving Kreiss constant bounds and matching minimax lower bounds, with applications to finite-horizon iteration complexity and scaling laws in two-time-scale optimization.
AI.motivation: Coupled gradient descent methods (bilevel optimization, two-time-scale SA, adversarial training) can suffer from large transient amplification due to non-normality, even when asymptotically stable. Standard spectral radius analysis fails to capture this phenomenon, motivating a sharper pseudospectral theory for block-triangular Jacobians.
AI.method: Develops a pseudospectral theory for block-triangular Jacobians; proves Kreiss constant bound K(J) ≤ 2/(1-γ) + ‖C‖/(4(1-γ)) for symmetric diagonal blocks with spectral radius γ<1; establishes minimax lower bounds; extends to nearly self-referential systems via Neumann-series perturbation framework; derives O(K(J)² log(1/δ)) iteration-complexity bound.
AI.result: Sharp bounds on the Kreiss constant for block-triangular Jacobians; matching minimax lower bounds; characterization of critical coupling threshold for spectral instability; finite-horizon iteration-complexity bound; identification of a non-asymptotic, instance-dependent regime invisible to spectral-radius analysis.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pseudospectral theory exposes transient amplification effects in coupled gradient descent that spectral radius misses, with confirmed validity via linear-quadratic problems, IQC comparisons, and neural-network training experiments.

title: Optimal Transport Flow Matching by Design
summary: Flow matching models learn to transport samples from a simple prior distribution to a complex data distribution. When prior-data pairs are coupled via optimal transport (OT), the learned trajectories are straight and non-crossing, enabling fast, even single-step, generation. However, computing the OT coupling in high dimensions is intractable, and existing methods attempt to solve the OT problem, at the cost of persistent bias or significant overhead. Rather than solving for the OT coupling, we reformulate the problem. Once the prior is treated as a design choice rather than a fixed input, the OT coupling between prior and data is no longer unique. Many priors admit an OT-optimal identity coupling to the data, leaving us free to choose one that is also tractable to sample. We identify low-frequency projection of natural images as such a choice. The identity coupling between data and its low-frequency representation is empirically OT-optimal, the prior is structured enough to be sampled by a lightweight model at inference, and the remaining flow-matching task reduces to synthesizing high-frequency detail. Interpolating the prior with Gaussian noise further improves generation quality while preserving the OT coupling. The approach requires no modifications to the flow model itself, and integrates naturally with latent-space models, classifier-free guidance, and one-step generation frameworks. Across all benchmarks, our method reduces trajectory curvature by more than $2\times$ compared to existing flow matching methods, yielding better generation quality in the few-step regime.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using low-frequency projections of images as a prior for flow matching, which achieves OT-optimal identity coupling, reduces trajectory curvature by 2x, and improves few-step generation quality without modifying the flow model.
AI.motivation: Existing flow matching methods rely on optimal transport (OT) coupling for straight, non-crossing trajectories, but computing OT in high dimensions is intractable and existing solutions suffer from bias or high overhead.
AI.method: Treat the prior as a design choice rather than fixed input; use low-frequency projection of natural images as the prior (which admits OT-optimal identity coupling to data), interpolate it with Gaussian noise, and train a lightweight model to sample this prior at inference.
AI.result: Reduces trajectory curvature by more than 2x compared to existing flow matching methods, yielding better generation quality in the few-step regime across all benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The approach requires no modifications to the flow model itself, integrates naturally with latent-space models, classifier-free guidance, and one-step generation frameworks, and provides a tractable alternative to solving OT coupling directly.

title: SMAC-Talk: A Natural Language Extension of the StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge for Large Language Models
summary: As LLMs become more widely deployed, they are increasingly expected to work alongside other AI agents rather than operating in isolation. Effective coordination in these settings requires agents to communicate, share information and make decisions under uncertainty. We introduce SMAC-Talk, a natural language extension of the StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge for evaluating LLM-based agents in cooperative multi-agent environments. The environment has several key features such as decentralized control, partial observability and long-horizon decision making. SMAC-Talk includes a natural language communication channel which is used to probe agent coordination and trust. We use this communication channel to construct different evaluation scenarios, including settings with an embedded deceptive communicator that tries to disrupt and deceive allies through communication alone. We provide three agents for benchmarking using 4 models from the Qwen3.5 family and study how reasoning structure, memory and model scale affect coordination between agents. We release SMAC-Talk as an open benchmark to support the research community in developing and evaluating LLM agents in cooperative multi-agent settings.

AI.tldr: SMAC-Talk extends StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge with natural language communication for evaluating LLM-based agents in cooperative multi-agent settings, including deceptive communication scenarios, and benchmarks Qwen3.5 models.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are deployed alongside other AI agents, effective coordination through communication and shared decision-making under uncertainty becomes critical, but existing benchmarks lack natural language channels for studying these dynamics.
AI.method: SMAC-Talk extends SMAC with a natural language communication channel, enabling decentralized control, partial observability, and long-horizon tasks. It introduces evaluation scenarios including a deceptive communicator agent. Three benchmarking agents are built using Qwen3.5 models to study reasoning, memory, and scale effects.
AI.result: Provides benchmarking results using 4 Qwen3.5 models, analyzing how reasoning structure, memory mechanisms, and model scale influence cooperative coordination performance.
AI.conclusion: SMAC-Talk is released as an open benchmark to support the community in developing and evaluating LLM-based agents in cooperative multi-agent environments with natural language communication.

title: Adaptive Patching Is Harder Than It Looks For Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Adaptive patching is a recent and compelling proposal for time-series Transformers: allocate finer patches where the sequence looks locally informative. This paper asks under what conditions a content-adaptive patching operator should outperform a tuned uniform one. Local heterogeneity alone is not enough: under pointwise forecasting losses, a complex-looking region is not automatically one where finer patching reduces the loss. We model patching as a budgeted bitrate allocation and derive an explicit threshold that a dynamic patching rule must satisfy to beat a well-tuned uniform baseline, then bound the achievable improvement both locally (a quadratic surrogate) and globally (a strong-convexity bound under the model's assumptions). Two structural results follow: without a coupling constraint, scalar local complexity cannot produce a non-uniform optimum under a common loss landscape; and once the backbone is trained to its representation-aware optimum, the alignment gain collapses around a well-tuned uniform patch size. To test these predictions, we run a controlled isolation study on three representative architectures, replacing each adaptive mechanism with a uniform patch-size sweep while keeping the backbone, data, and training protocol fixed. On standard long-horizon forecasting benchmarks, the validation-selected uniform baseline is competitive with the dynamic counterpart, with per-setting effects concentrated near zero and no consistent directional advantage once results are aggregated by dataset. The larger gains we do observe are method- and dataset-specific. Adaptive patching should therefore be evaluated against a tuned uniform baseline; its value depends on whether a cheap and reliable routing signal can identify where finer patches actually reduce forecasting loss.

AI.tldr: Adaptive patching for time-series Transformers is not inherently superior to a well-tuned uniform baseline; theoretical analysis shows specific conditions are needed for gains, and empirical isolation studies confirm that uniform baselines are competitive, with adaptive benefits being method- and dataset-specific.
AI.motivation: To determine under what conditions adaptive patching outperforms uniform patching in time-series Transformers, challenging the assumption that local heterogeneity alone justifies adaptive allocation.
AI.method: Models patching as budgeted bitrate allocation, derives a threshold rule for adaptive superiority, uses quadratic surrogate and strong-convexity bounds; conducts controlled isolation study with three architectures comparing adaptive vs. uniform patch-size sweeps.
AI.result: Uniform baselines selected via validation are competitive with adaptive methods; per-setting effects are near zero; no consistent directional advantage across datasets; larger gains are method- and dataset-specific.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive patching should be evaluated against tuned uniform baselines; its value depends on whether cheap, reliable routing signals can identify where finer patches actually reduce forecasting loss.

title: 3D-GlioPREDICT: 3D Latent Diffusion for Post-Radiotherapy Brain MRI Prediction in Patients with Glioma
summary: Radiotherapy is a cornerstone of glioma treatment inducing complex structural changes in brain tissue that are difficult to anticipate. Predicting these changes from pretreatment data could improve understanding of treatment-related effects and support the development of image-based outcome prediction methods.
