Recent studies have shown that follow-up brain magnetic resonance imaging can be synthesized from baseline imaging and treatment information, but most existing approaches operate on single 2D slices and represent treatment as a global parameter, rather than a spatially dynamic variable. In this work, we address both limitations with a 3D latent diffusion framework that conditions image generation on the spatially resolved voxel-wise dose distribution, alongside a pretreatment image and follow-up time. To make volumetric synthesis computationally feasible, the model combines latent-space compression with ControlNet-based spatial conditioning. The method was trained and evaluated on a public dataset comprising 257 scans from 25 glioma patients. Prediction quality was assessed using mean squared error, peak signal-to-noise ratio, and structural similarity index. Anatomical consistency was further evaluated using Dice scores for cerebrospinal fluid, gray matter, and white matter segmentations, together with hippocampus volume prediction error and deformation analysis based on log Jacobian determinant maps. Compared with our previously proposed 2D approach, the 3D model achieved improved image similarity while maintaining good agreement with ground truth anatomy and deformation patterns. Overall, these results support the feasibility of 3D treatment-aware generative modeling for predicting post-radiotherapy brain MRI using only pretreatment information. Code is available at https://github.com/nordinbelkacemi/fu-pred-3d

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a 3D latent diffusion framework that predicts post-radiotherapy brain MRI by conditioning on spatially-resolved dose distributions, achieving better image similarity and anatomical consistency than prior 2D methods.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches predict treatment-related brain changes from single 2D slices and treat treatment as a global parameter rather than a spatially dynamic variable, limiting their accuracy and clinical utility.
AI.method: A 3D latent diffusion framework combining latent-space compression with ControlNet-based spatial conditioning, using voxel-wise dose distribution, pretreatment imaging, and follow-up time as conditioning signals for volumetric synthesis.
AI.result: The 3D model outperformed a prior 2D approach in image similarity metrics (MSE, PSNR, SSIM) and maintained good anatomical consistency across CSF, gray matter, and white matter segmentations (Dice scores), hippocampus volume prediction, and deformation analysis.
AI.conclusion: 3D treatment-aware generative modeling is feasible for predicting post-radiotherapy brain MRI from pretreatment data, offering improved spatial accuracy over 2D methods.

title: Selecting haptic guidance models in teleoperation: guidelines from a comparative user study
comment: EUROHAPTICS 2026 - EuroHaptics International Conference, Jul 2026, Sienna, Italy
summary: Haptic guidance in teleoperation enhances operator performance through force feedback. This paper presents guidelines to select the most appropriate model considering the task, the environment and the operator. We define a unified formulation expressing most common models (spring-damper, potential field, and guiding tube) as variations of a stiffness-damping system with model-specific guiding functions. We conducted a user study comparing the three classical models across six scenarios with varying environmental conditions in a vertical farming task. Results show no universally superior model: spring-damper excels in cluttered environments, potential field in free spaces (but it shows risks near obstacles), and guiding tube offers a balanced compromise. We propose novel objective metrics to evaluate the interaction, and show that guiding force magnitude correlates with comfort and trust scores. These findings provide practical model selection guidelines through environmental characteristics and real-time evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: This paper presents guidelines for selecting haptic guidance models (spring-damper, potential field, guiding tube) in teleoperation based on task, environment, and operator, with a user study in vertical farming showing no universally superior model.
AI.motivation: To provide practical guidelines for selecting the most appropriate haptic guidance model in teleoperation, considering that different models perform differently depending on environmental conditions and task contexts.
AI.method: The authors propose a unified formulation expressing spring-damper, potential field, and guiding tube models as variations of a stiffness-damping system with model-specific guiding functions, then conduct a user study comparing these three models across six scenarios with varying environmental conditions in a vertical farming task.
AI.result: No universally superior model was found: spring-damper performs best in cluttered environments, potential field excels in free spaces but risks near obstacles, and guiding tube offers a balanced compromise. Novel objective metrics correlate guiding force magnitude with comfort and trust scores.
AI.conclusion: Practical model selection guidelines are provided through environmental characteristics and real-time evaluation metrics, enabling operators to choose the optimal haptic guidance model for their specific teleoperation context.

title: Do Transformers Need Three Projections? Systematic Study of QKV Variants
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 (PMLR vol. 306). 26 pages, 12 figures, 16 tables. Code: https://github.com/anushamadan02/Do-Transformers-Need-3-Projections
summary: Transformers have become the standard solution for various AI tasks, with the query, key, and value (QKV) attention formulation playing a central role. However, the individual contribution of these three projections and the impact of omitting some remain poorly understood. We systematically evaluate three projection sharing constraints: a) Q-K=V (shared key-value), b) Q=K-V (shared query-key), and c) Q=K=V (single projection). The last two variants produce symmetric attention maps; to address this, we also explore asymmetric attention via 2D positional encodings. Through experiments spanning synthetic tasks, vision (MNIST, CIFAR, TinyImageNet, anomaly), and language modeling (300M and 1.2B parameter models on 10B tokens), we discovered that our transformers perform on par or occasionally better than the QKV transformer. In language modeling, Q-K=V projection sharing achieves 50% KV cache reduction with only 3.1% perplexity degradation. Crucially, projection sharing is complementary to head sharing (GQA/MQA): combining Q-K=V with GQA-4 yields 87.5% cache reduction, while Q-K=V + MQA achieves 96.9%, enabling practical on-device inference. We show that Q-K=V preserves quality because keys and values can occupy similar representational spaces and attention operates in a low-rank regime, whereas Q=K-V breaks attention directionality. Our results systematically characterize projection sharing as an underexplored instance of weight tying in attention, with direct, quantifiable inference memory benefits, particularly valuable for edge deployment. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/anushamadan02/Do-Transformers-Need-3-Projections

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates sharing query, key, and value projections in Transformers (Q-K=V, Q=K-V, Q=K=V), finding that Q-K=V performs on par with standard QKV while reducing KV cache by 50%, and combining with GQA/MQA yields up to 96.9% cache reduction for on-device inference.
AI.motivation: The individual contributions of Q, K, V projections in Transformers are poorly understood, and the impact of omitting some projections remains unclear despite their widespread use.
AI.method: The authors systematically evaluate three projection sharing constraints: Q-K=V (shared key-value), Q=K-V (shared query-key), and Q=K=V (single projection). Asymmetric attention via 2D positional encodings is also explored for symmetric variants.
AI.result: Q-K=V achieves 50% KV cache reduction with only 3.1% perplexity degradation in language modeling. Combined with GQA-4 yields 87.5% cache reduction, and with MQA achieves 96.9% cache reduction, enabling practical on-device inference.
AI.conclusion: Q-K=V preserves quality because keys and values can occupy similar representational spaces and attention operates in a low-rank regime, while Q=K-V breaks attention directionality. Projection sharing is an underexplored weight tying instance with direct inference memory benefits for edge deployment.

title: When Seeing Is Not Believing -- A Benchmark for Search-Grounded Video Misinformation Detection
summary: Video misinformation increasingly operates at the semantic and evidential level: authentic footage may be selectively edited, temporally reordered, spliced across sources, or augmented with AI-generated content to construct false narratives. Such evidence-dependent manipulations cannot be reliably verified from the input video alone, because the missing, reordered, replaced, or recontextualized evidence lies outside the video itself. We introduce \textbf{EVID-Bench}, a benchmark for search-grounded video misinformation detection, where a system must search the open web for related videos and identify what information is false through cross-video comparison. EVID-Bench comprises 222 videos spanning 9 manipulation types across 3 categories: AI generation, single-source editing, and multi-source editing. All samples are verified to be undetectable by frontier models through visual inspection alone. We evaluate nine frontier multimodal models using a retrieval-augmented verification baseline. The best system achieves only 61.43\% point-level accuracy and 43.24\% video-level accuracy, while AI-generated manipulations remain especially challenging. Error analysis reveals recurring challenges: models fixate on irrelevant anchors, misattribute synthetic content to editorial splicing, and terminate search prematurely before fully explaining the manipulation.

AI.tldr: EVID-Bench is a new benchmark for search-grounded video misinformation detection where systems must search the web and compare videos across sources. It includes 222 videos with 9 manipulation types; best models achieve only 61.43% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current video misinformation detection methods fail for evidence-dependent manipulations (e.g., selective editing, temporal reordering, splicing) because the missing or altered evidence exists outside the input video. Frontier models cannot detect these via visual inspection alone.
AI.method: The authors introduce EVID-Bench with 222 videos spanning 9 manipulation types across 3 categories: AI generation, single-source editing, and multi-source editing. They evaluate 9 frontier multimodal models using a retrieval-augmented verification baseline requiring open-web search and cross-video comparison.
AI.result: Best system achieves 61.43% point-level accuracy and 43.24% video-level accuracy. AI-generated manipulations remain especially challenging. Error analysis shows models fixate on irrelevant anchors, misattribute synthetic content to splicing, and terminate search prematurely.
AI.conclusion: Current frontier models significantly struggle with semantically manipulated video misinformation when verification requires external evidence. The benchmark highlights the need for improved search-grounded, cross-video verification capabilities.

title: Consensus is Strategically Insufficient: Reasoning-Trace Disagreement as a Knowledge-Representation Signal
comment: Accepted to LAMAS&SR workshop at FLoC 2026 (KR + ICPL + LICS + CP + FSCD)
summary: Multi-agent systems are commonly designed to reduce disagreement through voting, consensus protocols, debate, or fault-tolerant aggregation. We argue that this objective is insufficient for value-laden tasks, where disagreement may reflect genuine normative uncertainty rather than agent error. Building on prior work on reasoning-trace disagreement in human-AI collaborative moderation, we propose a knowledge-representation layer in which reasoning traces and agent decisions are abstracted into symbolic disagreement states. Given agents producing explicit reasoning traces and binary decisions, we distinguish four states according to reasoning similarity and conclusion agreement: convergent agreement, divergent agreement, convergent disagreement and divergent disagreement. These states support defeasible strategic routing rules. We instantiate the framework in content moderation and argue that disagreement-aware routing provides a bridge between sub-symbolic LLM deliberation and symbolic knowledge representation for multi-agent strategic reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a knowledge-representation layer that captures reasoning-trace disagreement in multi-agent systems, distinguishing four symbolic disagreement states (convergent/divergent agreement/disagreement) to enable strategic routing in value-laden tasks like content moderation.
AI.motivation: Traditional multi-agent systems aim to reduce disagreement, but this is insufficient for value-laden tasks where disagreement reflects genuine normative uncertainty, not just agent error.
AI.method: The authors introduce a symbolic representation layer that abstracts agent reasoning traces and binary decisions into four disagreement states (convergent/divergent agreement/disagreement), which support defeasible strategic routing rules.
AI.result: The framework is instantiated in the content moderation domain, showing how disagreement-aware routing bridges sub-symbolic LLM deliberation and symbolic knowledge representation.
AI.conclusion: Disagreement-aware routing provides a bridge between sub-symbolic LLM deliberation and symbolic knowledge representation for multi-agent strategic reasoning in value-laden contexts.

title: KODA: Contrastive Representation Comparison and Alignment for Vision-Language Foundation Models
summary: Vision-language foundation models such as CLIP and SigLIP provide widely used representations for multimodal learning systems. While these models are typically compared through downstream performance, such evaluations often do not explain how their representations differ structurally. In this work, we study this problem through the task of Contrastive Embedding Clustering: identifying sample subsets that are weakly clustered under one representation but strongly clustered under another. We propose \emph{Kernel Optimization for Discrepancy Analysis (KODA)}, a kernel-based framework for contrastive representation comparison and alignment. KODA constructs unified multimodal kernels through modality-wise kernel composition and formulates discrepancy discovery as a constrained optimization problem that searches for coherent structures in one representation while suppressing coherence in a reference representation. This yields interpretable discrepancy directions associated with specific sample subsets and modality interactions. To scale KODA to large vision-language datasets, we develop randomized low-dimensional approximations of joint kernels using random projections, including Random Fourier Features for shift-invariant kernels. Empirically, KODA identifies consistent and interpretable discrepancy structures across vision-language representations and provides sample subsets for representation alignment. The code is available at https://github.com/yokiwuuu/KODA.

AI.tldr: KODA is a kernel-based framework that systematically identifies and quantifies structural differences between vision-language representations (e.g., CLIP vs. SigLIP) by finding sample subsets where one representation clusters more strongly than another, enabling interpretable discrepancy analysis and representation alignment.
AI.motivation: Vision-language foundation models (CLIP, SigLIP) are commonly compared via downstream task performance, but such evaluations fail to explain how their representations differ structurally. The authors aim to fill this gap by developing a method to systematically uncover structural discrepancies between representations.
AI.method: KODA (Kernel Optimization for Discrepancy Analysis) constructs unified multimodal kernels via modality-wise kernel composition, then formulates discrepancy discovery as a constrained optimization problem that maximizes coherence in one representation while suppressing it in a reference representation. To scale to large datasets, it uses randomized low-dimensional approximations (e.g., Random Fourier Features for shift-invariant kernels).
AI.result: KODA identifies consistent and interpretable discrepancy structures across vision-language representations (e.g., CLIP vs. SigLIP) and provides sample subsets useful for representation alignment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables systematic, interpretable comparison of multimodal representations beyond simple performance metrics, offering both analytical insights and practical utility for alignment.

title: Prospective Dynamic 3D MRI Reconstruction via Latent-Space Motion Tracking from Single Measurement
summary: Prospective reconstruction is crucial in many clinical applications such as MRI-guided radiotherapy, which demands accurate image reconstruction and fast motion estimation from currently acquired measurements. However, prospective reconstruction remains challenging due to ultra-sparse sampling and stringent latency requirements. In this work, we propose PDMR, a Prospective Dynamic 3D MRI Reconstruction framework with latent-space motion tracking. Our core idea is to learn an efficient and generalizable latent manifold of motion fields offline, enabling rapid online adaptation for prospective reconstruction. Specifically, we parameterize the deformation vector fields (DVFs) on a low-dimensional manifold, effectively reducing the search space for fast online adaptation, and employ a tri-plane representation to achieve geometry-aware and memory-efficient encoding of 3D motion. Experiments on both XCAT digital phantoms and in-house abdominal MRI datasets demonstrate that PDMR achieves high-fidelity and temporally consistent reconstruction across multiple prospective scenarios (Immediate and After-2min), outperforming state-of-the-art retrospective and online methods. Our results suggest a promising pathway toward ultra-fast, motion-aware prospective MRI reconstruction in clinical practice.

AI.tldr: PDMR is a framework for prospective dynamic 3D MRI reconstruction that learns a latent manifold of motion fields offline, enabling fast online adaptation with ultra-sparse sampling for real-time clinical applications like MRI-guided radiotherapy.
AI.motivation: Prospective MRI reconstruction is critical for applications like MRI-guided radiotherapy but faces challenges from ultra-sparse sampling and strict latency requirements. Existing methods struggle to balance reconstruction accuracy with fast motion estimation.
AI.method: PDMR learns a low-dimensional latent manifold of deformation vector fields (DVFs) offline, uses tri-plane representation for geometry-aware and memory-efficient 3D motion encoding, and rapidly adapts online for prospective reconstruction.
AI.result: PDMR achieves high-fidelity, temporally consistent reconstruction on XCAT digital phantoms and in-house abdominal MRI datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art retrospective and online methods across multiple prospective scenarios (Immediate and After-2min).
AI.conclusion: PDMR offers a promising pathway toward ultra-fast, motion-aware prospective MRI reconstruction for clinical practice.

title: Distortion-Aware UAV Placement for Aerial Semantic Relay Communications: An Analytical Approach
summary: Aerial semantic relay communications (SRC) employs an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) equipped with a semantic encoder as a relay, which not only extends the data acquisition coverage of the base station (BS) from resource-limited sensing device (SD) but also enhances communication efficiency through semantic feature transmission over the UAV-BS link. Existing works mainly focus on sum-rate maximization, overlooking the end-to-end reconstruction distortion of sensory data in UAV-assisted SRC systems. Optimizing the UAV placement is crucial for minimizing the end-to-end reconstruction distortion, as it fundamentally trades off the input perturbation at the UAV-side encoder against that at the BS-side decoder through the two-hop wireless channel conditions. In this paper, we propose an interpretable and efficient UAV placement policy by minimizing end-to-end reconstruction distortion in aerial SRC. This is a challenging task since the black-box nature of the DNN-based codecs and the intricate coupling between the heterogeneous codec sensitivities, along with two-hop channel impairments, render the end-to-end distortion analytically intractable to characterize. We first derive an analytical expression of the end-to-end distortion, explicitly revealing the impact of cross-hop perturbation coupling, wireless channel and radio resource on the reconstruction error. Based on that, we develop a closed-form UAV placement strategy with fast adaptability across various aerial SRC system configurations. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed distortion-aware UAV deployment closely tracks the empirical exhaustive-search optimum, while achieving lower distortion compared to representative capacity-based and curve-fitting benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an interpretable UAV placement policy that minimizes end-to-end reconstruction distortion in aerial semantic relay communications, deriving closed-form solutions that outperform capacity-based and curve-fitting benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing works focus on sum-rate maximization in aerial SRC, overlooking end-to-end reconstruction distortion of sensory data. Optimizing UAV placement to minimize this distortion is crucial but challenging due to black-box DNN codecs and complex coupling between codec sensitivities and two-hop channel impairments.
AI.method: The authors first derive an analytical expression for end-to-end distortion that reveals cross-hop perturbation coupling and wireless channel impacts. Based on this, they develop a closed-form UAV placement strategy with fast adaptability across different system configurations.
AI.result: The proposed distortion-aware UAV deployment closely tracks the empirical exhaustive-search optimum and achieves lower distortion compared to representative capacity-based and curve-fitting benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: An interpretable closed-form UAV placement policy minimizing end-to-end reconstruction distortion in aerial SRC is feasible and outperforms existing approaches that ignore distortion or rely on curve-fitting.

title: Multi-Agent Next-Best-View Optimization for Risk-Averse Planning
summary: Multi-agent Next-Best-View (NBV) selection for safe path planning in uncertain and unknown environments requires informative, safety-aware, and efficient coordination. Centralized approaches rely on sharing raw sensor data or significant communication overhead, resulting in limited scalability. We propose a distributed, risk-aware multi-agent NBV framework in which each robot maintains a private local 3D Gaussian Splatting map and the team jointly maximizes expected information gain (EIG) restricted to masked zones along planned trajectories. The resulting distributed objective is solved by Consensus ADMM (C-ADMM) over a communication graph, with each robot exchanging only candidate viewpoints, planned trajectory descriptors, and scalar EIG contributions. Collision risk along each trajectory is modeled via Average Value-at-Risk (AV@R) over the local 3DGS map and used both to shape the masking radius and to score planned paths. Experiments in Gibson environments at multiple team sizes show that the distributed formulation approaches the centralized baseline in mapping quality and trajectory safety while reducing communication by orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: A distributed, risk-aware multi-agent Next-Best-View framework using local 3D Gaussian Splatting maps and Consensus ADMM, achieving centralized-level performance with drastically reduced communication.
AI.motivation: Centralized multi-agent NBV approaches rely on raw sensor data sharing or high communication overhead, limiting scalability. A distributed, communication-efficient solution is needed for safe path planning in uncertain environments.
AI.method: Each robot maintains a private local 3DGS map; the team jointly maximizes expected information gain (EIG) over masked zones via Consensus ADMM; robots exchange only viewpoints, trajectory descriptors, and scalar EIG values. Collision risk (AV@R) shapes masking and scores paths.
AI.result: In Gibson environments across multiple team sizes, the distributed formulation matches centralized baseline in mapping quality and trajectory safety while reducing communication by orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Distributed C-ADMM with local 3DGS maps is a scalable, communication-efficient alternative to centralized multi-agent NBV, achieving comparable performance with minimal data exchange.

title: Inverse Critical Experiment Design via Gradient Optimization and a Multigroup Attention-Based Neural Network Architecture
summary:
The validation of advanced nuclear reactor designs and fuel concepts requires critical experiments with high neutronic similarity to the target technology. Neutronic similarity is quantified by the correlation coefficient $c_k$, which captures the shared bias in $k_\text{eff}$ induced by uncertainties in nuclear data. Generally, a $c_k\geq0.9$ is needed for an experiment to be sufficiently similar to a target technology. This work presents a methodology for the inverse design of critical experiments. Deep neural network surrogate modeling and nonparametric gradient optimization are used to generate experiment geometries that maximize $c_k$.
  A deep neural network is trained on OpenMC-calculated sensitivity vectors for grid-based critical experiment geometries. The model architecture combines a U-Net convolutional encoder-decoder with a novel multigroup attention pooling layer, introduced to capture the differing spatial dependencies of sensitivities. Multigroup attention pooling is shown to achieve better performance than traditional pooling, as well as interpretable internal behavior. The differentiability of the surrogate enables gradient-based optimization of the full combinatorial design space, allowing $c_k$ to be maximized by directly changing the material assignment of each position in the geometry grid.
  The method is applied to the validation of the TN-Americas TN-LC transportation cask with HALEU fuel, for which existing critical experiment coverage is limited. The optimization procedure is shown to produce experiment geometries achieving $c_k$ scores of 0.97757, 0.81324, and 0.93276 for three configurations of interest. This approach demonstrates the potential of deep learning and gradient optimization to accelerate the development of advanced nuclear technology.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a deep learning methodology for inverse design of critical nuclear experiments. A U-Net with multigroup attention pooling surrogate model enables gradient-based optimization to maximize neutronic similarity correlation coefficient ($c_k$) with target technologies. Applied to TN-LC transportation cask validation, the method achieves high $c_k$ scores (up to 0.9776), demonstrating deep learning's potential for accelerating advanced nuclear reactor validation.
AI.motivation: Validating advanced nuclear reactor designs and fuel concepts requires critical experiments with high neutronic similarity (quantified by $c_k$) to target technologies. Existing critical experiment coverage is limited for technologies like HALEU fuel transportation casks, necessitating a methodology to design experiments that maximize $c_k$.
AI.method: A deep neural network surrogate (U-Net convolutional encoder-decoder with multigroup attention pooling) is trained on OpenMC-calculated sensitivity vectors for grid-based geometries. The differentiable surrogate enables nonparametric gradient optimization to directly modify material assignments in the geometry grid, maximizing the correlation coefficient $c_k$.
AI.result: The method achieved $c_k$ scores of 0.97757, 0.81324, and 0.93276 for three configurations of the TN-LC transportation cask with HALEU fuel. Multigroup attention pooling outperformed traditional pooling methods and provided interpretable internal behavior.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deep learning and gradient optimization approach can generate experiment geometries with high neutronic similarity, demonstrating significant potential to accelerate the development of advanced nuclear technology.

title: Reflection Separation from a Single Image via Joint Latent Diffusion
summary: Single-image reflection separation is highly challenging under extreme conditions like glare or weak reflections. Existing methods often struggle to recover both layers in glare or weak-reflection scenarios because of insufficient information. This paper presents a diffusion model explicitly fine-tuned for this task, leveraging generative diffusion priors for robust separation. Our method simultaneously generates transmission and reflection layers through a unified diffusion model, incorporating a novel cross-layer self-attention mechanism for better feature disentanglement. We further introduce a disjoint sampling strategy to iteratively reduce interference between the layers during diffusion and a latent optimization step with a learned composition function for improved results in complex real-world scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach surpasses state-of-the-art methods on multiple real-world benchmarks. Project page: https://brian90709.github.io/diff-reflection-separation/

AI.tldr: A diffusion model with cross-layer self-attention, disjoint sampling, and latent optimization for robust single-image reflection separation under extreme conditions like glare and weak reflections.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle to recover both transmission and reflection layers under glare or weak-reflection scenarios due to insufficient information.
AI.method: Proposes a unified diffusion model with (1) cross-layer self-attention for feature disentanglement, (2) disjoint sampling strategy to reduce inter-layer interference, and (3) latent optimization with a learned composition function.
AI.result: Surpasses state-of-the-art methods on multiple real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The generative diffusion priors combined with the proposed attention and sampling mechanisms enable robust reflection separation even under extreme conditions.

title: VAMPS: Visual-Assisted Mathematical Problem Solving Benchmark
summary: Multimodal large language models are increasingly capable of complex reasoning, yet their performance often degrades when they must externalize a problem through a tool and then reason over the tool's output, specifically when they rely on visual aids. This gap is especially important because real engineering and scientific workflows often rely on visualization tools for analysis, validation, and decision-making. To study this discrepancy, we introduce VAMPS (Visual-Assisted Mathematical Problem Solving), a benchmark for graph-assisted mathematics. VAMPS contains 1,168 multimodal, bilingual multiple-choice question-answer pairs drawn from Iranian University Entrance Exam algebra and calculus problems and expanded with human-reviewed LLM-generated synthetic variants, all selected so that plotting provides a natural solution strategy by revealing intersections, extrema, asymptotes, etc. Designed for both benchmarking and diagnosis, VAMPS goes beyond prior multimodal benchmarks that primarily evaluate reasoning over fixed visual inputs by testing whether a model can benefit from constructing a useful graph and grounding its answer in the resulting visualization. Overall, we found that across a diverse set of models, direct analytical solving surprisingly outperforms tool-enabled visual solving, even on problems where plotting is a natural strategy.

AI.tldr: VAMPS is a new benchmark of 1,168 bilingual math problems where plotting is a natural solution strategy, revealing that LLMs perform worse when using visual tools than solving directly analytically.
AI.motivation: LLMs' performance degrades when they must use external visualization tools before reasoning, which is problematic since real engineering/scientific workflows rely on visual aids.
AI.method: Created VAMPS benchmark with 1,168 multimodal bilingual multiple-choice questions from Iranian University Entrance Exam problems, enhanced with human-reviewed LLM-generated variants, designed so plotting reveals solution strategies (intersections, extrema, asymptotes).
AI.result: Across diverse models, direct analytical solving outperformed tool-enabled visual solving, even on problems where plotting is a natural strategy.
AI.conclusion: Current multimodal LLMs fail to benefit from constructing and grounding reasoning on visualizations, despite plots being a natural solution approach for these problems.

title: An Open-Source Two-Stage Computer Vision Pipeline for Fine-Grained Vehicle Classification using Vision Transformers
summary: Vehicle body type is a significant determinant of cyclist injury severity in overtaking crashes, yet automated tools for classifying vehicles into injury-risk-relevant categories from naturalistic roadway video do not exist in the open literature. Standard object detection benchmarks provide only coarse vehicle labels (car, truck, bus, motorcycle), while existing fine-grained recognition systems are trained on controlled imagery and lack evaluation for deployment robustness across recording sites. This paper presents an open-source two-stage computer vision pipeline combining a pre-trained RT-DETR detector for coarse vehicle localization with a fine-tuned Vision Transformer (ViT-Base/16) for six-category body-type classification: passenger car, SUV, pickup truck, minivan, large van, and commercial truck. A confidence-based abstention mechanism withholds Stage 2 predictions when softmax output falls below 0.60, producing unknown labels rather than silent misclassifications. Evaluated on 3,805 annotated overtaking events from a bicycle-lane corridor in Ann Arbor, Michigan (in-distribution), the pipeline achieved 0.94 accuracy with per-class F1 scores from 0.91 (minivan) to 0.97 (SUV). On an independent out-of-distribution evaluation of 311 events from an open cycling dataset without retraining, accuracy was 0.89. Three of four well-represented categories maintained F1 at or above 0.90 under domain shift. The largest degradation was observed for minivan (F1 = 0.72), driven by abstention rate rising from 2.4% to 25.0% rather than active misclassification, consistent with the mechanism propagating genuine model uncertainty. The full pipeline, including inference scripts, training code, evaluation utilities, and model weights, is released as open-source software to support reproducibility and reuse across roadside video archives and cycling safety research.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an open-source two-stage computer vision pipeline combining RT-DETR detection with a Vision Transformer (ViT-Base/16) to classify vehicles into six body types relevant to cyclist injury severity. It achieves 0.94 accuracy in-distribution and 0.89 out-of-distribution, with a confidence-based abstention mechanism that gracefully handles uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Vehicle body type is a key determinant of cyclist injury severity in overtaking crashes, but no open-source automated tool exists for classifying vehicles into injury-risk-relevant categories from naturalistic roadway video footage.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) RT-DETR detector for coarse vehicle localization, (2) fine-tuned ViT-Base/16 for six-category classification (passenger car, SUV, pickup truck, minivan, large van, commercial truck). A confidence-based abstention mechanism (threshold=0.60) outputs "unknown" instead of silent misclassifications.
AI.result: In-distribution: 0.94 accuracy, per-class F1 0.91–0.97 (3,805 events). Out-of-distribution: 0.89 accuracy (311 events), three of four well-represented categories maintained F1≥0.90; minivan dropped to 0.72 F1 due to increased abstention (2.4%→25.0%), not active misclassification.
AI.conclusion: The open-source pipeline reliably classifies vehicle body types for cycling safety, gracefully handles domain shift via abstention, and is released for reproducibility and reuse across roadside video archives.

title: GenED-SC: Generative Editing Semantic Communication with Integrated Multi-Modal LLMs
summary: Deep learning-based joint source-channel coding has recently demonstrated strong potential for semantic communication (SemComm). However, most existing approaches focus on optimizing visual-fidelity metrics, which can lead to reduced perceptual quality. Generative model-based SemComm leverages rich prior knowledge from large-scale pre-training to enhance perceptual quality, but often at the cost of increased distortion and unreliability. This paper addresses the above issues by proposing a two-stage semantic image transmission framework, integrating a multimodal large language model (MLLM) for generative editing. In the first stage, a JSCC-based discriminative transmission selectively prioritizes semantically important regions, preserving scene layout and object integrity under limited bandwidth. In the second phase, MLLM-driven generative editing refines missing details based on the textual descriptions, enhancing semantic fidelity and perceptual quality. Extensive experiments show that the proposed framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in semantic preservation, perceptual quality, and visual fidelity across a wide range of channel conditions, especially in low-SNR regimes.

AI.tldr: A two-stage semantic image transmission framework combining JSCC-based discriminative transmission (preserving layout/objects under limited bandwidth) with MLLM-driven generative editing (refining details via text descriptions) to achieve superior semantic preservation, perceptual quality, and visual fidelity across channel conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing deep JSCC methods optimize visual-fidelity metrics but reduce perceptual quality; generative model-based SemComm improves perceptual quality but introduces distortion and unreliability. This paper addresses the trade-off between visual fidelity and perceptual quality in semantic communication.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) JSCC-based discriminative transmission prioritizes semantically important regions to preserve layout/object integrity under limited bandwidth; (2) MLLM-driven generative editing refines missing details using textual descriptions to enhance semantic fidelity and perceptual quality.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in semantic preservation, perceptual quality, and visual fidelity across various channel conditions, especially in low-SNR regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage framework effectively balances visual fidelity and perceptual quality by combining discriminative JSCC transmission with MLLM-based generative editing, outperforming existing methods particularly under challenging channel conditions.

title: Affordance2Action: Task-Conditioned Scene-level Affordance Grounding for Real-Time Manipulation
summary: Task-conditioned manipulation requires grounding instructions to task-relevant functional parts rather than object categories. This setting is scene-dependent and often one-to-many in cluttered scenes: the same object may afford different interactions across tasks, while a single task may correspond to either one functional region or multiple valid functional regions, depending on the scene layout. Existing affordance datasets and benchmarks remain misaligned with this setting, as they typically focus on grasping or object-level affordances, rely on synthetic scenes, or assume a single instruction-region correspondence. We present Affordance2Action (A2A), a benchmark-centered learning framework for scene-level, task-conditioned part affordance grounding. At its core is A2A-Bench, a manipulation-oriented benchmark that covers both single-region and multi-region instruction correspondences in everyday scenes, with the latter highlighting the ambiguity and diversity of affordance grounding in realistic multi-object environments. To construct it at scale, we build A2A-AffordGen, an agent-assisted annotation pipeline that combines language-model filtering, interactive part segmentation, instance-level mask-out refinement, task-reasoning instruction generation, and human verification. A2A-Bench's supervision further supports diverse downstream applications, with real-time affordance grounding and affordance-conditioned manipulation policies as two representative examples. Experiments show that A2A exposes substantial gaps in generic segmentation, VLM-based grounding, and affordance distillation baselines, while improving task-level localization and providing useful spatial priors for downstream manipulation. All datasets and code will be publicly released to promote open research.

AI.tldr: A2A is a benchmark-centered framework for scene-level, task-conditioned part affordance grounding that addresses one-to-many instruction-region correspondences in cluttered environments, featuring a large-scale benchmark (A2A-Bench), an agent-assisted annotation pipeline (A2A-AffordGen), and downstream applications including real-time affordance grounding and manipulation policies.
AI.motivation: Existing affordance datasets focus on grasping or object-level affordances, rely on synthetic scenes, or assume a single instruction-region correspondence, failing to capture the scene-dependent, one-to-many nature of task-conditioned part affordance grounding in realistic cluttered environments.
AI.method: The authors propose A2A with (1) A2A-AffordGen: an agent-assisted annotation pipeline combining language-model filtering, interactive part segmentation, instance-level mask-out refinement, task-reasoning instruction generation, and human verification; (2) A2A-Bench: a benchmark covering both single-region and multi-region instruction correspondences; (3) downstream applications for real-time affordance grounding and affordance-conditioned manipulation policies.
AI.result: A2A reveals substantial gaps in generic segmentation, VLM-based grounding, and affordance distillation baselines, while demonstrating improved task-level localization and useful spatial priors for downstream manipulation.
AI.conclusion: The A2A framework better aligns affordance learning with realistic task-conditioned manipulation needs, exposing limitations of existing methods and enabling more effective task-level grounding and manipulation policy learning. Datasets and code will be publicly released.

title: Self-Distilled Policy Gradient
summary: On-policy self-distillation, where a language model conditions on privileged context to supervise its own generations, is a promising source of dense supervision for sparse-reward reinforcement learning. Actually, it can be instantiated as an auxiliary full-vocabulary student-to-teacher reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence loss. We therefore propose SDPG, a self-distilled policy-gradient framework that combines group-relative verifier advantages with normalized standard deviation, exact full-vocabulary on-policy self-distillation, as well as reference-policy KL regularization. Empirically, SDPG improves stability and performance over RLVR and self-distillation baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/lauyikfung/SDPG.

AI.tldr: SDPG is a self-distilled policy-gradient framework that combines group-relative verifier advantages, full-vocabulary on-policy self-distillation, and reference-policy KL regularization for improved stability and performance in sparse-reward RL.
AI.motivation: To leverage on-policy self-distillation as dense supervision for sparse-reward reinforcement learning in language models.
AI.method: SDPG combines three components: (1) group-relative verifier advantages, (2) exact full-vocabulary on-policy self-distillation (as reverse KL divergence from student to teacher), and (3) reference-policy KL regularization.
AI.result: SDPG improves stability and performance over RLVR and self-distillation baselines.
AI.conclusion: On-policy self-distillation, instantiated as a student-to-teacher reverse KL divergence loss, is effective for dense supervision in sparse-reward RL for language models.

title: Pinpoint: Grounded Worldwide Image Geolocation via Cross-Source Retrieval and Reranking
summary: Image geolocation aims to estimate where a photograph was taken from its visual content. At worldwide scale, this remains challenging because visual evidence is often ambiguous, diverse, and unevenly distributed. Prior work has typically treated geolocation of ordinary internet photos and street-view imagery as separate tasks, despite their complementary strengths: internet photos better match the appearance distribution of user-captured queries, while street-view imagery provides denser, geographically grounded coverage. We present Pinpoint, a retrieve-and-rerank architecture that combines both sources in a coarse-to-fine pipeline. A contrastive image-GPS embedder is trained on both user-uploaded Flickr photos and street-view imagery, learning a shared image-GPS embedding space that is used to retrieve candidate locations. An attention-based reranker then rescores retrieved candidates by combining candidate-level visual and GPS features with cross-source evidence from nearby locations to ground the prediction. Unlike recent prior work, Pinpoint does not rely on multimodal large-language models, making inference faster and more reproducible. Pinpoint achieves state-of-the-art results across all metrics on standard benchmarks for internet photos (IM2GPS3k and YFCC4k) and street-view imagery (OSV-5M).

AI.tldr: Pinpoint is a retrieve-and-rerank architecture for image geolocation that combines internet photos (Flickr) and street-view imagery using a contrastive image-GPS embedder and an attention-based reranker, achieving state-of-the-art results without relying on multimodal LLMs.
AI.motivation: Prior work treated geolocation of internet photos and street-view imagery as separate tasks, missing complementary strengths: internet photos match user-captured query appearance, while street-view provides denser, geographically grounded coverage.
AI.method: A coarse-to-fine pipeline: (1) contrastive image-GPS embedder trained on both Flickr and street-view data to retrieve candidate locations, (2) attention-based reranker that rescored candidates using visual/GPS features and cross-source evidence from nearby locations.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results across all metrics on IM2GPS3k, YFCC4k (internet photos), and OSV-5M (street-view imagery) benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Combining internet photos and street-view imagery in a unified pipeline outperforms separate approaches, and competitive geolocation is achievable without expensive multimodal LLMs, enabling faster and more reproducible inference.

title: StepPRM-RTL: Stepwise Process-Reward Guided LLM Fine-Tuning for Enhanced RTL Synthesis
summary: Automatic generation of RTL code for digital hardware designs remains challenging due to long-horizon reasoning, multi-step dependencies, and strict correctness constraints in Verilog and VHDL. We present StepPRM-RTL, a novel framework that combines stepwise trajectory modeling, process-reward modeling (PRM), and retrieval-augmented fine-tuning (RAFT) to enhance both the functional correctness and reasoning fidelity of LLM-based RTL code generation. StepPRM-RTL constructs stepwise reasoning trajectories from canonical solutions, where each step contains a rationale and incremental code modification. A Process Reward Model (PRM) evaluates intermediate steps, providing dense feedback that guides reinforcement-style updates during RAFT fine-tuning. Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) explores alternative reasoning paths, enriching the training dataset with high-quality trajectories. This integration of stepwise and outcome-aware rewards allows the model to learn both how and why to construct correct RTL, improving long-horizon reasoning beyond standard supervised or outcome-based training. Experimental evaluation on benchmark Verilog and VHDL datasets demonstrates that StepPRM-RTL outperforms the best prior methods by over 10\% in functional correctness and reasoning fidelity metrics. Ablation studies confirm that the combination of PRM-guided rewards and stepwise trajectory exploration is key to its performance. StepPRM-RTL generalizes across RTL languages and provides a scalable framework for high-fidelity, interpretable code generation, establishing a new standard for LLM-assisted hardware design automation.

AI.tldr: StepPRM-RTL is a framework combining stepwise trajectory modeling, process-reward modeling, and retrieval-augmented fine-tuning to improve LLM-based RTL code generation, achieving over 10% improvement in functional correctness and reasoning fidelity over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Automatic RTL code generation for digital hardware remains challenging due to long-horizon reasoning, multi-step dependencies, and strict correctness constraints in Verilog and VHDL. Existing supervised or outcome-based training methods are insufficient for these requirements.
AI.method: StepPRM-RTL constructs stepwise reasoning trajectories from canonical solutions with rationales and incremental code modifications. It uses a Process Reward Model (PRM) to evaluate intermediate steps with dense feedback, Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for exploring alternative reasoning paths, and RAFT fine-tuning guided by reinforcement-style updates.
AI.result: Outperforms best prior methods by over 10% in functional correctness and reasoning fidelity on benchmark Verilog and VHDL datasets. Ablation studies confirm PRM-guided rewards and stepwise trajectory exploration are key to performance.
AI.conclusion: StepPRM-RTL generalizes across RTL languages, provides a scalable framework for high-fidelity interpretable code generation, and establishes a new standard for LLM-assisted hardware design automation.

title: Gravity-Aware Hierarchical Routing for Lightweight SensorLLM on Human Activity Recognition
summary: Recent studies on sensor-language alignment have shown that two-stage frameworks can improve the semantic modeling ability of wearable-sensor human activity recognition (HAR), where SensorLLM-style methods first perform motion-to-language alignment and then fine-tune the model for downstream tasks. However, our experiments reveal a consistent failure mode when the Stage 2 backbone is compressed to a compact model such as TinyLlama: recognition of dynamic activities remains relatively strong, while the discrimination of low-motion static classes such as standing, sitting, and lying degrades substantially. To address this issue, we propose a gravity-aware hierarchical routing head as a lightweight post-alignment adaptation built on top of an already aligned model, rather than a new large-scale pretraining framework. The method uses the per-channel mean and std from the Chronos tokenizer state to extract statistical cues related to posture and gravity direction, and adaptively combines a static expert and a full expert through soft routing, together with a load-balancing loss for stable training. On the MHealth dataset, this design significantly improves macro-F1 with minimal parameter overhead, and the gains are concentrated mainly on static classes while preserving strong performance on dynamic activities. As a first arXiv disclosure, the current paper reports results on a single dataset only, with the goal of highlighting the core method and laying the groundwork for broader evaluation in future work.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a gravity-aware hierarchical routing head to improve static activity recognition (standing, sitting, lying) in compact SensorLLM-style HAR models, without requiring large-scale retraining.
AI.motivation: Two-stage sensor-language alignment frameworks (SensorLLM) degrade on static activity recognition when the backbone is compressed to a compact model like TinyLlama, while dynamic activities remain strong. The paper aims to fix this failure mode.
AI.method: A lightweight gravity-aware hierarchical routing head is added post-alignment. It extracts statistical cues (per-channel mean/std from Chronos tokenizer state) related to posture/gravity direction, then uses soft routing to combine a static expert and a full expert, with a load-balancing loss for stable training.
AI.result: On MHealth dataset, the method significantly improves macro-F1 with minimal parameter overhead. Gains concentrate on static classes while dynamic activity performance is preserved.
AI.conclusion: The proposed head effectively addresses the static-dynamic performance imbalance in compact SensorLLM models. Results are reported on a single dataset as an initial disclosure, with broader evaluation planned for future work.

title: Distribution-Free Risk-Aware Planning and Control Under Uncertainty Using Conformal Spectral Risk Control
comment: Submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
summary: Safe navigation in dynamic and uncertain environments often relies on accurate estimation of, or assumptions about, the true underlying uncertainty. However, accurately characterizing the true uncertainty distribution is often difficult due to limited data or imperfect information. An incorrect understanding of the uncertainty and its associated risk may lead to dangerous decisions even under high levels of risk aversion. To address this issue, we propose a risk-aware model predictive control (RA-MPC) framework that incorporates prediction sets to guarantee risk control below a user-specified threshold without requiring assumptions about the underlying uncertainty distribution. To generate the prediction sets, we develop a distribution-free risk quantification framework that extends conformal risk control (CRC) to general spectral risk measures. We then show that incorporating the prediction sets into the MPC framework provides statistical safety guarantees in terms of spectral risk constraint satisfaction even under uncertainty misspecification. We validate the proposed framework in simulated vehicle obstacle avoidance scenarios, demonstrating improved safety and reduced solve time compared to a baseline RA-MPC framework.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a risk-aware model predictive control (RA-MPC) framework using conformal prediction sets to guarantee spectral risk control below a user-specified threshold without distributional assumptions, validated in vehicle obstacle avoidance.
AI.motivation: Accurate uncertainty estimation is difficult in dynamic environments; incorrect risk characterization can lead to dangerous decisions even under high risk aversion.
AI.method: Extends conformal risk control (CRC) to general spectral risk measures for distribution-free risk quantification, then integrates prediction sets into MPC framework.
AI.result: Demonstrated improved safety and reduced solve time in simulated vehicle obstacle avoidance compared to baseline RA-MPC.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides statistical safety guarantees for spectral risk constraints even under uncertainty misspecification, without requiring distributional assumptions.

title: Bayes-Sufficient Representations in Supervised Learning
summary: Representation learning is often described as preserving the information in an input that is relevant for prediction. This work asks what relevance means for a fixed supervised decision problem. A representation is defined to be Bayes-sufficient for a joint distribution and loss if some prediction head can use it to implement a Bayes-optimal action rule. This makes the target information loss-dependent. In the almost-surely unique Bayes-action case, the relevant object is a Bayes quotient, which identifies inputs that require the same Bayes-optimal action. A representation is sufficient when it refines this quotient, and Bayes-minimal when it is informationally equivalent to it. The framework connects naturally to property elicitation: zero-one loss requires the Bayes class, squared loss the conditional mean, Brier loss the conditional probability in binary prediction, and log loss or strictly proper scoring rules the predictive distribution. Controlled finite experiments, learned neural bottleneck experiments, and a real-data iNaturalist taxonomic refinement experiment illustrate the distinction between sufficiency, minimality, and retained non-required information. For a fixed supervised problem, the distribution and the loss determine the Bayes action, the Bayes action determines the quotient, and the quotient determines the minimal information required for Bayes-optimal prediction.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Bayes-sufficient representations, defining when a representation retains enough information for Bayes-optimal decision-making under a specific loss function. It formalizes the Bayes quotient as the minimal sufficient statistic for optimal prediction and connects the framework to property elicitation and various loss functions.
AI.motivation: To formally define what information in a representation is "relevant" for a fixed supervised decision problem, particularly showing that relevance is loss-dependent and characterizing minimal sufficient information.
AI.method: The paper proposes Bayes-sufficiency based on whether a representation can implement a Bayes-optimal action rule. It introduces the Bayes quotient (equivalence classes of inputs with identical Bayes-optimal actions) and defines sufficiency as refining this quotient, with Bayes-minimality being informationally equivalent to it.
AI.result: In the almost-surely unique Bayes-action case, the relevant object is a Bayes quotient. Zero-one loss requires the Bayes class, squared loss the conditional mean, Brier loss the conditional probability, and log loss the predictive distribution. Controlled experiments, neural bottlenecks, and iNaturalist taxonomic refinement illustrate sufficiency vs. minimality.
AI.conclusion: For a fixed supervised problem, the distribution and loss determine the Bayes action, which determines the quotient, which determines the minimal information required for Bayes-optimal prediction.

title: End-to-End Text Line Detection and Ordering
summary: Practical text-recognition pipelines for historical documents typically decompose layout analysis into line detection followed by a separate reading-order step, with the latter most often handled by a hand-coded geometric heuristic that struggles with marginalia, multiple columns, tables, and source-specific editorial conventions. This article introduces Orli (Ordered Regression of Lines), an end-to-end model that casts both sub-tasks as a single image-to-sequence problem: from a page image, Orli autoregressively generates text-line baselines directly in reading order. Baselines are represented in a chord-frame parameterization that anchors a line's position, orientation, and extent while encoding local geometry through perpendicular offsets; an iterative refinement head and a local visual refiner produce the final curve. Trained on a heterogeneous corpus of 196,691 pages spanning ten writing systems, Orli marginally exceeds the previously reported state of the art for cBAD line detection without dataset-specific training, reaches near perfect coverage and ordering on multiple reading-order benchmarks zero-shot, and adapts to more specialized out-of-domain layouts with limited fine-tuning. The method's source code and model weights are available under an open license at https://github.com/mittagessen/orli.

AI.tldr: Orli is an end-to-end model that unifies text-line detection and reading-order prediction in historical documents as a single image-to-sequence task, using chord-frame parameterization and autoregressive generation, achieving state-of-the-art or near-perfect results across diverse benchmarks zero-shot or with minimal fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Traditional text-recognition pipelines for historical documents split layout analysis into separate line detection and reading-order steps, where reading order is typically handled by hand-coded geometric heuristics that fail on complex layouts like marginalia, multiple columns, tables, and editorial conventions.
AI.method: Orli casts both line detection and reading order as a single image-to-sequence problem, autoregressively generating text-line baselines directly in reading order using a chord-frame parameterization (anchoring position, orientation, extent with perpendicular offsets), an iterative refinement head, and a local visual refiner.
AI.result: Trained on 196,691 pages across ten writing systems, Orli slightly exceeds the previous SOTA for cBAD line detection without dataset-specific training, achieves near-perfect coverage and ordering on multiple reading-order benchmarks zero-shot, and adapts to out-of-domain layouts with limited fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Orli demonstrates that end-to-end joint modeling of line detection and reading order is effective and practical, eliminating the need for hand-coded heuristics, with open-source code and weights available for further use.

title: Can Generalist Agents Automate Data Curation?
summary: Curating training data is among the most consequential yet labor-intensive parts of modern AI development: practitioners iteratively propose, implement, evaluate, and revise data policies against noisy benchmark feedback. We ask whether generalist coding agents can automate this data-curation loop. We introduce *Curation-Bench*, an agent-centric benchmark that fixes the model, training recipe, and evaluation suite while giving agents command-line access to inspect data, implement policies, submit them to a fixed training/evaluation pipeline, and revise. In a vision-language instruction-tuning instantiation, out-of-the-box agents reach strong published data-selection baselines within ten iterations. However, trajectory analysis reveals a persistent *execution-research gap*: agents mainly tune local policy variants rather than explore new policy families, even when given strategy guides and paper references. Scaffolds requiring each iteration to cite, instantiate, and adapt a prior method shift agents toward method-guided exploration. The scaffolded agent autonomously composes -- without human design input -- a data-selection policy that outperforms strong published baselines at one-tenth their data budget. Overall, current agents can run the curation loop, but reliable data research requires scaffolded method adaptation, not open-ended prompting alone. Code and benchmark are open-sourced.

AI.tldr: Coding agents can automate AI data curation loops, autonomously composing a data-selection policy that outperforms published baselines at 1/10th the data budget, though scaffolded method adaptation is needed for reliable data research.
AI.motivation: Data curation is labor-intensive in modern AI development; practitioners must iteratively propose and evaluate data policies against noisy benchmark feedback. The goal is to automate this process using generalist coding agents.
AI.method: Introduces *Curation-Bench*, an agent-centric benchmark giving agents command-line access to inspect data, implement policies, submit to a fixed training/evaluation pipeline, and revise. Tests out-of-the-box agents and scaffolded agents (requiring citation, instantiation, and adaptation of prior methods) in a vision-language instruction-tuning setting.
AI.result: Out-of-the-box agents reach strong published data-selection baselines within ten iterations but exhibit an *execution-research gap* (tuning local variants vs. exploring new policy families). Scaffolded agents shift toward method-guided exploration and autonomously compose a data-selection policy outperforming strong published baselines at one-tenth the data budget.
AI.conclusion: Current agents can run the curation loop, but reliable data research requires scaffolded method adaptation, not open-ended prompting alone.

title: SkySense: A Semi-Supervised Generative Framework for UAV Localization in ISAC Networks
summary: Extreme data scarcity and inherent multipath spatial ambiguity severely limit existing deep learning-based channel state information (CSI) fingerprinting localization schemes for target unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). To overcome these challenges, we propose an end-to-end semi-supervised generative localization framework. First, by exploiting the temporal correlations inherent in continuous flight trajectories, a self-supervised encoder extracts robust spatial features from massive unlabeled CSI sequences to establish structured latent representations. Following this, we utilize a consistency model, a powerful derivative of diffusion architectures, as the core generative backbone to map the learned latent space to physical coordinates, jointly fine-tuning the pre-trained encoder with a strictly limited set of labeled CSI. This consistency formulation models the conditional distribution to resolve the mean collapse problem of discriminative models, while compressing the inference trajectory to 1-2 steps to avoid the latency bottleneck of traditional diffusion models. Furthermore, a lightweight distributed fusion mechanism is designed to aggregate spatial predictions across multiple base stations (BS) from a multi-view geometry perspective. Comprehensive evaluations on a real-world measurement dataset demonstrate that our framework achieves low latency and suppresses the mean localization error to 9.77 cm under a 3-BS fusion setup with only a 1\% label fraction, significantly outperforming existing fully supervised and semi-supervised discriminative baselines.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised generative localization framework for UAVs that uses a consistency model (diffusion derivative) and self-supervised encoder to achieve 9.77 cm mean error with only 1% labeled data in 3-BS fusion setup.
AI.motivation: Extreme data scarcity and multipath spatial ambiguity severely limit existing deep learning-based CSI fingerprinting localization for target UAVs.
AI.method: 1) Self-supervised encoder extracts features from unlabeled CSI via temporal correlations. 2) Consistency model (diffusion derivative) maps latent space to coordinates, fine-tuned with limited labeled data, solving mean collapse and reducing inference to 1-2 steps. 3) Lightweight distributed fusion mechanism aggregates predictions across multiple base stations via multi-view geometry.
AI.result: Achieves 9.77 cm mean localization error with 3-BS fusion and only 1% label fraction, outperforming fully supervised and semi-supervised discriminative baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses data scarcity and multipath ambiguity in UAV CSI fingerprinting localization, achieving high accuracy with low latency.

title: DLO-Lab: Benchmarking Deformable Linear Object Manipulations with Differentiable Physics
summary: We address the challenge of enabling robots to manipulate deformable linear objects (DLOs), such as ropes, cables, and rubber bands. Prior work has primarily focused on narrow, task-specific problems, often relying on real-world demonstrations or handcrafted heuristics. Such approaches, however, struggle to scale to the wide variety of materials and tasks encountered in practice, and collecting sufficiently diverse real-world data is often impractical. Additionally, existing simulation environments offer limited support for the broad spectrum of material behaviors necessary for generalizable DLO manipulation. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a differentiable simulator explicitly designed for versatile DLO manipulation. Our simulator models a wide range of material properties-including (in)extensibility, elasticity, bending plasticity, and complex interactions with other objects-providing a robust foundation for learning and evaluating manipulation skills. Building on this simulator, we propose a benchmark suite of representative tasks that highlight the unique challenges of DLO manipulation. The successful execution of these tasks is often hindered by the topological complexity and grasp sensitivity inherent to DLOs. Therefore, we introduce a specialized DLO agent that explicitly manages these challenges by proposing strategic grasping points and decomposing long-horizon tasks to maximize control authority. Finally, we evaluate various policy-learning algorithms using our framework, alongside sim-to-real transfer experiments, demonstrating our platform's potential to advance DLO manipulation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a differentiable simulator for deformable linear objects (DLOs) like ropes and cables, a benchmark suite of manipulation tasks, and a specialized DLO agent that handles topological complexity and grasp sensitivity, with evaluations of policy-learning algorithms and sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Prior work on DLO manipulation is limited to narrow, task-specific problems relying on real-world demos or handcrafted heuristics, which struggle to scale across diverse materials and tasks. Collecting sufficient real-world data is impractical, and existing simulators lack support for the wide range of material behaviors needed for generalizable DLO manipulation.
AI.method: The authors develop a differentiable simulator modeling diverse material properties (extensibility, elasticity, bending plasticity, complex interactions). They propose a benchmark suite of representative DLO tasks and design a specialized DLO agent that strategically selects grasping points and decomposes long-horizon tasks to manage topological complexity and grasp sensitivity.
AI.result: The framework is used to evaluate various policy-learning algorithms, and sim-to-real transfer experiments are conducted, demonstrating the platform's potential to advance DLO manipulation.
AI.conclusion: The differentiable simulator, benchmark suite, and specialized agent collectively address key limitations in DLO manipulation, offering a scalable foundation for learning generalizable manipulation skills that can transfer to real-world scenarios.

title: Unlocking Feature Learning in Gated Delta Networks at Scale
summary: Training and scaling Large Language Models demand enormous computational resources, motivating both efficient sub-quadratic architectures and principled hyperparameter tuning methods. While the Maximal Update Parametrization ($μ$P) has enabled zero-shot hyperparameter transfer for standard Transformers, its extension to linear models, particularly those with structured state transitions and complicated architectures, remains largely unexplored. By rigorously propagating coordinate-size estimates through the forward pass, gating mechanisms, and recurrent state dynamics, we derive the scaling rules for Gated Delta Network. Experiments on language-model pre-training confirm that our configurations enable stable learning-rate transfer across model widths under both AdamW and SGD, whereas standard parametrization fails to transfer, validating the correctness and practical utility of our analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Maximal Update Parametrization (μP) to Gated Delta Networks, deriving scaling rules that enable stable hyperparameter transfer across model widths, validated through language-model pre-training experiments.
AI.motivation: While μP works for standard Transformers, its extension to linear models with structured state transitions and gating mechanisms (like Gated Delta Networks) is unexplored, and existing methods fail to transfer hyperparameters for such architectures.
AI.method: The authors rigorously propagate coordinate-size estimates through forward pass, gating mechanisms, and recurrent state dynamics to derive scaling rules specifically for Gated Delta Network architectures.
AI.result: Experiments on language-model pre-training show that the derived scaling rules enable stable learning-rate transfer across model widths under both AdamW and SGD optimizers, while standard parametrization fails.
AI.conclusion: The derived μP extension for Gated Delta Networks is correct and practically useful for enabling zero-shot hyperparameter transfer in sub-quadratic architectures.

title: GroupToM-Bench: Benchmarking Group Theory of Mind and Nonlinear Social Emergence in MLLMs
summary: True general intelligence requires not only a model of the physical world but also a social world model: the capacity to infer how individual mental states interact and crystallize into group-level outcomes. Despite notable progress in individual-level Theory of Mind (ToM) reasoning, existing multimodal large language models fail at this broader task. Collective behavior emerges non-linearly from social tensions, conformity dynamics, and structural constraints, meaning it cannot be recovered by merely summing individual intentions. We present GroupToM-Bench, the first multimodal benchmark for group-level ToM, built around a causal chain spanning micro-level BDI states (belief, desire, intention), meso-level group tension and structural constraints, and macro-level outcome prediction and mechanistic attribution. To probe this full arc, we develop a seven-level cognitive audit framework. Experiments reveal a gap between current models and human baselines, highlighting a failure to process social structures and non-linear collective dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GroupToM-Bench, the first multimodal benchmark for group-level Theory of Mind reasoning, revealing that current multimodal LLMs fail at understanding non-linear collective social dynamics despite progress in individual-level ToM.
AI.motivation: True general intelligence requires understanding not just the physical world but also social group dynamics (collective behavior emerging non-linearly from social tensions, conformity, and structural constraints), which existing models cannot handle.
AI.method: The authors built GroupToM-Bench, a multimodal benchmark with a causal chain spanning micro-level BDI states (belief, desire, intention), meso-level group tension and structural constraints, and macro-level outcome prediction. A seven-level cognitive audit framework was used for evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments show a significant gap between current multimodal LLMs and human baselines, with models failing to process social structures and non-linear collective dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Group-level Theory of Mind reasoning remains a major challenge for current AI systems, highlighting the need for models that can handle emergent social phenomena beyond individual mental state inference.

title: Characterizing initial human-AI proof formalization workflows
summary: For centuries, human mathematicians have written proofs to substantiate their mathematical arguments; yet, the ability to automatically verify the validity of proofs has long been a challenge. Advances in AI systems' ability to generate code and engage in increasingly high-level mathematical reasoning promise to transform people's ability to formalize and thereby verify proofs. While many works focus on benchmarking the current frontier, we instead study how people use these tools. We conduct a mixed-methods analysis into the initial impact of AI on people's formalization workflows: what people claim they want, what they see as the barriers to those visions, and how they actually use and adapt AI in practice. A qualitative survey shows that people's preferences are diverse, but with a general desire for AI assistance in formalization that preserves high-level human control over the proof discovery process. To assess how people actually engage with AI for formalization under such limitations, we conduct a controlled user study in which participants formalize informal math problems and their proofs, with and without AI, across a range of mathematical problems at varying levels of difficulty and domains. Despite limitations of the tools at the time for autoformalization, participants tend to attain higher formalization accuracy when allowed access to AI tools than when formalizing on their own, with most participants flexibly choosing to use multiple different AI tools. Taken together, our work sheds light on the early stages of AI integration into formalization workflows, involving an intimate interplay of human and AI engagement.

AI.tldr: This mixed-methods study examines how mathematicians use AI tools for formalizing proofs, finding that users desire AI assistance with human oversight, and that AI access improves formalization accuracy despite tool limitations.
AI.motivation: To understand how people actually use AI tools for mathematical proof formalization, as opposed to merely benchmarking AI capabilities, given the transformative potential of AI in automating verification workflows.
AI.method: Mixed-methods approach combining a qualitative survey on user preferences and a controlled user study where participants formalize informal math problems with and without AI assistance across varying difficulty levels and domains.
AI.result: Users have diverse preferences but generally want AI assistance that preserves human control over proof discovery. Participants achieved higher formalization accuracy with AI tools than without, and most flexibly used multiple AI tools.
AI.conclusion: AI integration into formalization workflows is in early stages, characterized by intimate human-AI interplay where users strategically combine tools to overcome current limitations.

title: Spatial-Spectral Modeling of the Array Pattern of a Two-Element Dynamic Antenna Array with Differential Amplitude Modulation
summary: We present a theoretical model for a two-element dynamic phased array and characterize the transfer of information as a function of angle. The array is based on a two-state switched structure with phase shifting to support beamsteering. Dynamic motion of the phase center of antenna arrays generates time-varying radiation patterns that, when appropriately designed, support directional modulation, or the transfer of information to regions of space that are narrower than that covered by the energy radiated by the array. We evaluate the impact of switching frequency and steering on the spatial width of the information beam, which is the region of space where information is recoverable. The concepts are evaluated through simulation and experiment using a 0.75$λ$ two-element array operating at 2.5 GHz.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a theoretical model for a two-element dynamic phased array using two-state switched structures with phase shifting, enabling directional modulation where information is transferred to narrower spatial regions than the radiated energy. The concepts are validated through simulation and experiment at 2.5 GHz.
AI.motivation: To characterize and model how dynamic phased arrays with switched structures can achieve directional modulation, where information is confined to narrower angular regions than the radiated beam width.
AI.method: Theoretical modeling of a two-element dynamic phased array with two-state switching and phase shifting for beamsteering. Evaluation through simulation and experimental validation using a 0.75λ two-element array at 2.5 GHz.
