The full leaderboard shows a tiered structure rather than a smooth total order: a small frontier tier lies above 48%, a dense strong-model tier spans roughly 38-45%, and low-performing models remain only modestly above the 10% chance baseline. Tool augmentation adds up to 5.17 points across the five tool-use evaluations, with gains varying substantially across models. We report bootstrap ranking-stability statistics to make bounded-budget variance explicit and to discourage over-interpretation of adjacent ranks.

AI.tldr: KINA is an 899-item knowledge benchmark across 261 disciplines, featuring formal guarantees for disciplinary representativeness (via a greedy proxy) and incentive-compatible bonus-on-review payment. Evaluation of 42 models shows large headroom (top score 53.17%), a tiered leaderboard, and tool-use gains of up to 5.17 points, with bootstrap stability statistics to caution against over-interpreting rank differences.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks face three issues: (1) scaling-driven designs that lack disciplinary representativeness, (2) flat-payment annotation that incentivizes lazy consensus, and (3) unaudited ranking instability under bounded test budgets. The paper aims to address these gaps with principled solutions.
AI.method: The authors construct KINA, an 899-item benchmark across 261 fine-grained disciplines. They formalize representativeness as a coverage-style objective over expert-elicited anchors, approximated greedily with a (1-1/e) guarantee (Proposition 1). They prove a bonus-on-tournament mechanism weakly FOSD-dominates flat payment, with an incentive-compatibility threshold (Theorem 1). They evaluate 42 models from 13 labs and report bootstrap ranking-stability statistics.
AI.result: The top model (Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview) scores 53.17%, followed by Claude-Opus-4.6 (49.92%) and GPT-5.4 (48.55%). The leaderboard is tiered: frontier (>48%), strong (38-45%), and low (just above 10% chance). Tool augmentation adds up to 5.17 points, varying across models. Bootstrap statistics quantify ranking instability under bounded budgets.
AI.conclusion: KINA provides a more rigorous benchmark with formal guarantees on representativeness and annotation incentives. There remains substantial headroom, and the tiered structure suggests meaningful capability gaps. The ranking-stability analysis encourages cautious interpretation of fine-grained rank differences.

title: PE-MHL: Physics-Encoded Modular Hybrid Layers for Scalable Learning of Complex Systems
summary: Hybrid models that combine physics-based and data-driven components have shown strong potential for achieving accuracy and interpretability in control applications. While recent methods have made progress in incorporating physical consistency, challenges remain in scalability, robustness to noise, and control of model complexity. This paper proposes a Physics-Encoded Modular Hybrid Layer (PE-MHL) framework, in which a baseline physics-based model is incrementally refined through the addition of new sub-models, where each new component adds complexity while preserving what previous components have already learned. We establish a theoretical guarantee for this construction: with a least-squares initialization of each new sub-model, the training error is monotonically non-increasing in the number of sub-models and provably converges. Empirical evaluations on a nonlinear NARX benchmark and the Quanser Aero 2 platform demonstrate that PE-MHL outperforms equivalently sized monolithic networks in both accuracy and generalization, while also providing more stable training dynamics and better preservation of underlying data structures.

AI.tldr: PE-MHL is a modular hybrid framework that incrementally adds physics-encoded sub-models to refine a baseline model, with theoretical guarantees of monotonically non-increasing training error and proven convergence. It outperforms monolithic networks in accuracy, generalization, and training stability on NARX and Quanser Aero 2 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Hybrid physics-data models offer accuracy and interpretability but face challenges in scalability, noise robustness, and complexity control. The paper aims to address these limitations.
AI.method: Proposes Physics-Encoded Modular Hybrid Layer (PE-MHL) framework that incrementally adds physics-based sub-models to a baseline model, preserving prior learned knowledge. Each new sub-model is initialized via least-squares, with a theoretical guarantee of monotonic error reduction and convergence.
AI.result: PE-MHL outperforms monolithic networks of equivalent size in accuracy and generalization on nonlinear NARX benchmark and Quanser Aero 2 platform, with more stable training and better preservation of data structures.
AI.conclusion: The modular hybrid approach with theoretical convergence guarantees effectively balances complexity, accuracy, and interpretability, offering a scalable solution for control applications.

title: IMPose: Interactive Multi-person Pose Estimation with Dynamic Correction Propagation
summary: High-quality dynamic human pose annotation equips AI with precise motion kinematics to enable human behavior mastery, yet remains labor-intensive and time-consuming. Current annotation tools either lack temporal correction propagation or fail in multi-person scenarios, necessitating excessive manual intervention. In this paper, we introduce IMPose, an interactive tool for multi-person dynamic pose annotation. It features a dual-level tracking mechanism that propagates one-frame multi-person pose corrections from annotators across entire videos. The keypoint-level ensures corrections temporal propagation via sequential modeling, while the instance-level employs keypoint-aware embedding with relative positional encoding to maintain multi-person cross-frame consistency. To further improve robustness, IMPose maintains historical pose and instance cues in a trajectory bank, which enhances long-range temporal association and stabilizes annotation in challenging cases such as occlusion and motion blur. By converting sparse human corrections into dense and coherent pose trajectories, our framework significantly reduces repeated manual refinement across frames. Extensive experiments show that IMPose consistently achieves a strong accuracy efficiency trade off under different interaction budgets, demonstrating particular advantages in low click annotation settings. IMPose achieves high precision annotation with high efficiency, requiring only 27 clicks per 1,050 frame video on 3DPW and 3 clicks per tracklet per 84-frame on PoseTrack21. We further expand PoseTrack21 with 188K pose instances (3.55M keypoints) at a minimal cost of 10 annotators in 10 hours. The annotation tool, codes, and extended dataset will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: IMPose is an interactive tool for multi-person dynamic pose annotation that uses a dual-level tracking mechanism (keypoint-level and instance-level) to propagate sparse corrections across entire videos, achieving high accuracy with minimal clicks (e.g., 27 clicks per 1,050 frames).
AI.motivation: High-quality dynamic human pose annotation is labor-intensive and time-consuming; existing tools lack temporal correction propagation or fail in multi-person scenarios, requiring excessive manual intervention.
AI.method: IMPose features a dual-level tracking mechanism: (1) keypoint-level for temporal correction propagation via sequential modeling, and (2) instance-level using keypoint-aware embedding with relative positional encoding for multi-person cross-frame consistency. A trajectory bank maintains historical pose and instance cues to enhance long-range temporal association.
AI.result: Achieves 27 clicks per 1,050-frame video on 3DPW and 3 clicks per tracklet per 84-frame on PoseTrack21. Expanded PoseTrack21 with 188K pose instances (3.55M keypoints) at minimal cost (10 annotators in 10 hours).
AI.conclusion: IMPose converts sparse human corrections into dense, coherent pose trajectories, significantly reducing manual refinement across frames while maintaining a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off, especially in low-click settings.

title: DiffAero: A GPU-Accelerated Differentiable Simulation Framework for Efficient Quadrotor Policy Learning
summary: This letter introduces DiffAero, a lightweight, GPU-accelerated, and fully differentiable simulation framework designed for efficient quadrotor control policy learning. DiffAero supports both environment-level and agent-level parallelism and integrates multiple dynamics models, customizable sensor stacks (IMU, depth camera, and LiDAR), and diverse flight tasks within a unified, GPU-native training interface. By fully parallelizing both physics and rendering on the GPU, DiffAero eliminates CPU-GPU data transfer bottlenecks and delivers orders-of-magnitude improvements in simulation throughput. In contrast to existing simulators, DiffAero not only provides high-performance simulation but also serves as a research platform for exploring differentiable and hybrid learning algorithms. Extensive benchmarks and real-world flight experiments demonstrate that DiffAero and hybrid learning algorithms combined can learn robust flight policies in hours on consumer-grade hardware. The code is available at https://github.com/flyingbitac/diffaero.

AI.tldr: DiffAero is a lightweight, GPU-accelerated, fully differentiable quadrotor simulation framework that enables efficient policy learning through massive parallelism, eliminating CPU-GPU bottlenecks and enabling robust flight policy training in hours on consumer hardware.
AI.motivation: Existing quadrotor simulators suffer from CPU-GPU data transfer bottlenecks and lack full differentiability, limiting the speed and efficiency of control policy learning.
AI.method: DiffAero achieves end-to-end GPU acceleration by fully parallelizing both physics simulation and rendering on the GPU, supporting environment-level and agent-level parallelism, multiple dynamics models, and customizable sensor stacks.
AI.result: Orders-of-magnitude improvement in simulation throughput; robust flight policies can be learned in hours on consumer-grade hardware.
AI.conclusion: DiffAero is not only a high-performance simulator but also a research platform for exploring differentiable and hybrid learning algorithms for quadrotor control.

title: Offline-to-Online Learning in Linear Bandits
summary: We study online learning with an additional offline dataset in the stochastic linear bandit setting. Although this problem arises frequently in practice, the offline-to-online tradeoff remains poorly understood in structured environments. We propose a linear bandit algorithm that balances this tradeoff: it relies on offline data during early rounds, and increasingly favors exploration as the horizon grows. We establish regret bounds showing that our method is simultaneously competitive with both purely online and purely offline solutions. In particular, it achieves sublinear regret relative to the optimal action in the number of online interactions, while its regret relative to an offline reference decreases as the number of offline samples grows. Empirical results further demonstrate its effectiveness across various problem parameters.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a linear bandit algorithm that effectively combines offline data with online learning, achieving sublinear regret relative to the optimal action while leveraging offline samples to reduce regret early in the learning process.
AI.motivation: The offline-to-online tradeoff in stochastic linear bandits is poorly understood despite its practical relevance. The authors aim to design an algorithm that can leverage pre-collected offline data while still benefiting from online exploration.
AI.method: A linear bandit algorithm that balances offline data usage and online exploration by relying on offline data in early rounds and increasingly favoring exploration as the horizon grows.
AI.result: Sublinear regret relative to the optimal action in the number of online interactions; regret relative to an offline reference decreases as offline sample size increases; empirical validation across various problem parameters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is simultaneously competitive with both purely online and purely offline solutions, effectively bridging the gap between the two settings.

title: SFMambaNet: Spectral-Frequency Enhanced Selective State Space Model for Correspondence Pruning
summary: Correspondence pruning aims to identify inliers from an initial set of correspondences. Most existing Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based methods rely on geometric features mapped from coarse Euclidean coordinates, which struggle to capture the subtle geometric consistencies presented by inliers. While Mamba-based methods possess global receptive fields and long sequence modeling capabilities, they tend to accumulate substantial inconsistent features within the hidden state space, making it difficult to distinguish inliers from outliers. In this paper, we integrate frequency domain perception into this task for the first time and propose SFMambaNet, a novel Spectral-Frequency enhanced Mamba-based two-view correspondence pruning network. Our method is collaboratively composed of two components: First, we design a Local Spectral-Geometric Attention (LSGA) block. LSGA incorporates spectral positional encoding into local graph interactions and introduces multi-scale Mamba processing to enhance the capture of subtle geometric consistencies and improve local feature discriminability. Building upon this, we design a Spectral-Integrated Global Mamba (SIGM) block. SIGM embeds a frequency gating mechanism within the state space, utilizing the frequency information provided by LSGA to explicitly suppress high-frequency noise accumulation within hidden states and mitigate the propagation of inconsistent features. This enhances inlier-outlier separability and achieves robust global context modeling capabilities with nearly linear complexity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SFMambaNet outperforms current state-of-the-art methods on several challenging tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/Kirito14IT/SFMambaNet.

AI.tldr: SFMambaNet integrates frequency domain perception with Mamba and spectral-geometric attention for two-view correspondence pruning, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Existing GNN-based methods using coarse Euclidean coordinates fail to capture subtle geometric consistencies of inliers, while Mamba-based methods accumulate inconsistent features in hidden states, making inlier/outlier distinction difficult.
AI.method: Two components: (1) Local Spectral-Geometric Attention (LSGA) block with spectral positional encoding and multi-scale Mamba for local feature discriminability; (2) Spectral-Integrated Global Mamba (SIGM) block with frequency gating to suppress high-frequency noise accumulation in hidden states.
AI.result: Outperforms current state-of-the-art methods on several challenging tasks.
AI.conclusion: Frequency domain perception effectively enhances correspondence pruning by improving inlier-outlier separability with near-linear complexity.

title: SpurAudio: A Benchmark for Studying Shortcut Learning in Few-Shot Audio Classification
summary: Few-shot classification (FSC) is widely used for learning from limited labeled data, yet most evaluations implicitly assume that target concepts are independent of contextual cues. In real-world settings, however, examples often appear within rich contexts, allowing models to exploit spurious correlations between foreground content and background signals. While such effects have been studied in few-shot image classification, their role in few-shot audio classification remains largely unexplored, and existing audio benchmarks offer limited control over contextual structure. We introduce SpurAudio, a benchmark that leverages the natural separability of foreground events and background environments in audio to enable controlled, multi-level evaluation of contextual shifts across support and query sets. Using this benchmark, we show that many state-of-the-art few-shot methods suffer severe performance degradation when background correlations are disrupted, despite achieving similar accuracy under standard evaluation protocols. Crucially, this vulnerability persists even in large pretrained audio foundation models, ruling out limited backbone capacity as an explanation. Moreover, methods that appear comparable under conventional benchmarks can exhibit markedly different sensitivity to spurious correlations, revealing systematic algorithmic strengths and vulnerabilities tied to how feature representations interact with classifier heads at inference time. These findings provide new insight into the behavior of few-shot methods in audio and highlight the need for benchmarks that explicitly probe context dependence when evaluating FSC models.

AI.tldr: Introduces SpurAudio, a benchmark for evaluating spurious correlations in few-shot audio classification, revealing that state-of-the-art methods (including large pretrained audio models) suffer significant performance degradation when background-context correlations are disrupted.
AI.motivation: Few-shot classification evaluations assume target concepts are independent of contextual cues, but real-world settings contain rich contexts where models can exploit spurious correlations between foreground content and background signals. While studied in image classification, such effects remain unexplored in few-shot audio classification.
AI.method: Proposes SpurAudio, a benchmark that leverages the natural separability of foreground events and background environments in audio to enable controlled, multi-level evaluation of contextual shifts across support and query sets.
AI.result: Many state-of-the-art few-shot audio methods suffer severe performance degradation when background correlations are disrupted, even large pretrained audio foundation models. Methods with similar conventional benchmark accuracy exhibit markedly different sensitivity to spurious correlations.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the need for benchmarks that explicitly probe context dependence when evaluating FSC models, and reveals systematic algorithmic strengths/vulnerabilities tied to how feature representations interact with classifier heads at inference time.

title: Folded Transport MCMC: Certifiable Quotient Posterior Computation for Symmetric Bayesian Models
comment: 48 pages (including supplementary material), 5 figures, 6 tables. Submitted to Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B
summary: Bayesian models with finite symmetry - mixture models with exchangeable components, structural identification with closely-spaced modes - define posteriors that are invariant under a group of label permutations, creating redundant multimodality that degrades MCMC convergence diagnostics. We introduce Folded Transport MCMC (FolT-MCMC), which performs inference directly on the quotient posterior by constructing an independence sampler on the fundamental domain of the symmetry group. The quotient proposal is formed by symmetrising a learned normalising flow over the group orbits. We prove that the LCNF oscillation-based certification framework transfers to the quotient metric with a stabiliser-corrected ball-mass bound and improved covering radius, and that the quantile-core certified lower bound improves whenever the unfolded flow exhibits cross-mode proposal deficiency. On Gaussian mixtures (d = 2 - 20), label-switching targets (up to 24 equivalent modes), and a standard Bayesian three-component mixture posterior, the quantile-core certified improvement ratio ranges from 2x to 145x, with the folded certificate empirically nearly dimension-free. On real accelerometer data from a supertall building during Typhoon Mangkhut, FolT-MCMC yields a non-vacuous quantile-core certificate where the unfolded certificate is vacuous.

AI.tldr: FolT-MCMC performs inference on the quotient posterior by symmetrizing normalizing flows, achieving 2x-145x certified improvement in MCMC convergence on symmetric Bayesian models.
AI.motivation: Bayesian models with finite symmetry (e.g., mixture models with exchangeable components) produce redundant multimodality due to label permutation invariance, which degrades MCMC convergence diagnostics.
AI.method: FolT-MCMC constructs an independence sampler on the fundamental domain of the symmetry group by symmetrizing a learned normalizing flow over group orbits, enabling direct inference on the quotient posterior.
AI.result: On Gaussian mixtures (d=2-20), label-switching targets (up to 24 modes), and Bayesian 3-component mixtures, quantile-core certified improvement ratio ranges from 2x to 145x, with nearly dimension-free folded certificates. On real accelerometer data from a supertall building during Typhoon Mangkhut, FolT-MCMC yields non-vacuous certificates where unfolded is vacuous.
AI.conclusion: FolT-MCMC effectively addresses label-switching multimodality by working on the quotient space, with theoretical guarantees showing improved certified bounds whenever the unfolded flow exhibits cross-mode proposal deficiency.

title: Optical-Guided Neural Collapse for SAR Few-Shot Class Incremental Learning
summary: Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) in synthetic aperture radar imagery presents unique challenges due to severe data scarcity and SAR-specific variability. In particular, strong azimuth sensitivity in SAR induces large intra-class variation and inter-class confusion, and FSCIL sequential updates further lead to catastrophic forgetting of previously learned classes. Inspired by neural collapse, we propose an optical-guided SAR FSCIL framework, which derives orthogonal feature subspaces from a data-rich optical ATR dataset and uses them as geometric priors to guide SAR feature learning. SAR features are projected onto these orthogonal subspaces via principal angle constraints, effectively transferring discriminative structure from the optical to the SAR domain. Specifically, our projection loss and the classifier loss optimized with a frozen simplex-ETF geometry jointly induce neural collapse by concentrating features around class means while maintaining large inter-class angles. We evaluate the approach on a benchmark comprising an optical ATR dataset and a SAR ATR dataset with 24 target classes, organized into a base training session and seven incremental sessions. Compared with recent FSCIL methods including NCFSCIL and so on, our method achieves the highest final accuracy and a favorable trade-off between final performance and performance degradation. Moreover, neural collapse metrics show improved intra-class compactness and inter-class separability, indicating that the learned features more closely approximate the ideal simplex-ETF geometry.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an optical-guided SAR FSCIL framework that uses neural collapse principles and orthogonal feature subspaces from optical data to improve few-shot incremental learning in SAR imagery, achieving better accuracy and reduced forgetting.
AI.motivation: Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) in SAR imagery suffers from severe data scarcity, strong azimuth sensitivity causing intra-class variation and inter-class confusion, and catastrophic forgetting during sequential updates.
AI.method: An optical-guided SAR FSCIL framework that derives orthogonal feature subspaces from a data-rich optical ATR dataset, uses them as geometric priors to guide SAR feature learning via principal angle constraints, and jointly optimizes projection loss and classifier loss with frozen simplex-ETF geometry to induce neural collapse.
AI.result: The method achieves the highest final accuracy and favorable trade-off between final performance and performance degradation compared to recent FSCIL methods (e.g., NCFSCIL) on a benchmark with 24 target classes across 8 sessions. Neural collapse metrics show improved intra-class compactness and inter-class separability.
AI.conclusion: The optical-guided approach effectively transfers discriminative structure to SAR domain, and neural collapse principles help mitigate catastrophic forgetting in SAR FSCIL, with learned features closely approximating the ideal simplex-ETF geometry.

title: Latent Anchor-Driven Test Generation for Deep Neural Networks
summary: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are increasingly being deployed in security-critical and safety-sensitive applications, which makes rigorous testing essential to identify and mitigate model weaknesses. Existing DNN testing approaches explore either the input space or a learned latent space. While latent-space generation can better maintain plausibility than direct input-space mutation, current methods still face a trade-off among exploration controllability, failure diversity, and seed-relative semantic drift. To overcome these limitations, we propose Latte, a black-box testing framework that generates semantically proximate, diverse, and fault-revealing test cases by leveraging the latent space. Specifically, Latte encodes each input seed with a pre-trained VQ-VAE and performs a seed-centered, one-step latent mutation along directions defined by anchors sampled from alternative classes, followed by quantization and decoding back to the input space. This explores local neighborhoods around each seed within the learned latent manifold, resulting in a larger number and broader diversity of oracle-triggering prediction discrepancies under the same budget. We evaluated Latte on 5 datasets and 10 DNN models in single-model and multi-model testing scenarios. Across the evaluated datasets and models, Latte improves fault exposure and behavioral diversity under matched testing budgets. Under the single-model setting, it also maintains low seed-relative semantic drift with respect to the source seeds.

AI.tldr: Latte is a black-box DNN testing framework that uses VQ-VAE latent space mutation to generate semantically proximate, diverse, and fault-revealing test cases, outperforming existing methods across 5 datasets and 10 models.
AI.motivation: Existing DNN testing methods face a trade-off among exploration controllability, failure diversity, and semantic drift; latent-space approaches maintain plausibility better than input-space mutation, but still lack in balancing these factors.
AI.method: Latte encodes input seeds via a pre-trained VQ-VAE, performs seed-centered one-step latent mutation along directions defined by anchor samples from alternative classes, quantizes, and decodes back to input space to explore local neighborhoods on the latent manifold.
AI.result: On 5 datasets and 10 DNN models under single-model and multi-model settings, Latte improves fault exposure and behavioral diversity under matched budgets, while maintaining low semantic drift relative to source seeds.
AI.conclusion: Latte effectively overcomes the trade-off in existing DNN testing by leveraging latent-space mutation, producing more diverse and fault-revealing test cases with controlled semantic proximity.

title: Impostor: An Agent-Curated Benchmark for Realistic AIGC Manipulation Localization
summary: Recent advances in generative image editing have improved the realism and controllability of localized image manipulation, raising new challenges for image manipulation detection and localization (IMDL). However, existing IMDL benchmarks still have limitations in visual realism, manipulation diversity, and generator coverage, making it difficult to reflect recent trends in image manipulation. To address these limitations, we introduce Impostor, a high-quality AI-edited image manipulation localization dataset containing 100K manipulated images. Impostor is constructed by CraftAgent, a closed-loop agent framework that integrates scene perception, editing planning, manipulation execution, quality validation, and iterative reflection to automatically generate diverse and visually realistic manipulated images. Moreover, Impostor contains images generated by seven recent AIGC models across three manipulation types and includes multiple manipulated regions, providing a more comprehensive benchmark for AIGC-based IMDL. Furthermore, we propose PhaseAware-Net (PANet), a semantic-forensic framework that introduces local phase modeling and semantic-forensic consistency learning to better localize semantically plausible yet forensically disrupted manipulated regions. Extensive experiments show that Impostor poses significant challenges to existing large vision-language models (LVLMs) and specialized IMDL methods, while PANet achieves superior performance on Impostor and multiple public benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Impostor, a large-scale AI-edited image manipulation localization dataset (100K images) built via an automated closed-loop agent framework (CraftAgent), and proposes PANet, a semantic-forensic framework using local phase modeling and consistency learning to detect localized manipulations.
AI.motivation: Existing IMDL benchmarks lack visual realism, manipulation diversity, and generator coverage, failing to reflect recent advances in generative image editing.
AI.method: (1) CraftAgent: a closed-loop agent framework with scene perception, editing planning, manipulation execution, quality validation, and iterative reflection. (2) Impostor dataset: 100K manipulated images from 7 AIGC models across 3 manipulation types. (3) PANet: a semantic-forensic framework with local phase modeling and semantic-forensic consistency learning.
AI.result: Impostor poses significant challenges to existing LVLMs and specialized IMDL methods; PANet achieves superior performance on Impostor and multiple public benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset and framework advance the state of the art in AIGC-based image manipulation detection and localization, addressing limitations in existing benchmarks and methods.

title: Neural Radiated-Noise Fields for Unmanned Underwater Vehicle Noise Spectrum Prediction in Three-Dimensional Scenes
summary: Radiated noise in unmanned underwater vehicles (UUVs) is an important indicator for characterizing acoustic signatures and evaluating platform performance. To address the strong dependence of traditional physics-based modeling and numerical simulation methods on target structural information and environmental boundary conditions, and their inability to achieve continuous spatial spectrum-response modeling in three-dimensional scenes, this paper proposes a neural radiated-noise field (NRNF). An NRNF represents the UUV radiated-noise spectrum as a continuous function of the three-dimensional UUV position, the three-dimensional hydrophone position, the UUV yaw angle, and the frequency, enabling query-based prediction at arbitrary spatial locations. The proposed method employs sinusoidal encoding for position and frequency, and introduces a learnable three-dimensional scene feature grid to explicitly represent environmental structure and propagation effects. A spectrum-prediction dataset is constructed from lake trials, and the proposed model is evaluated under three settings: horizontal extrapolation, depth extrapolation, and cross-run generalization. Results show that the NRNF achieves an average prediction error of 3.5 dB in the 50 to 5000 Hz band. Horizontal extrapolation is easiest, depth extrapolation is the most challenging, and cross-run generalization is of intermediate difficulty. Further ablation results demonstrate that the scene feature grid significantly improves the prediction stability and spatial generalization of the model.

AI.tldr: Proposes a neural radiated-noise field (NRNF) for UUVs, enabling continuous 3D spatial spectrum prediction using sinusoidal encoding and learnable scene feature grids, achieving ~3.5 dB error across 50-5000 Hz.
AI.motivation: Traditional physics-based methods for UUV radiated noise depend heavily on detailed structural info and boundary conditions, and cannot do continuous 3D spatial spectrum-response modeling.
AI.method: NRNF represents radiated-noise spectrum as a continuous function of 3D UUV position, 3D hydrophone position, yaw angle, and frequency, using sinusoidal encoding + learnable 3D scene feature grid.
AI.result: Average prediction error 3.5 dB in 50-5000 Hz. Horizontal extrapolation easiest, depth extrapolation hardest, cross-run intermediate. Scene feature grid improves stability and generalization.
AI.conclusion: NRNF is effective for continuous spatial spectrum prediction of UUV radiated noise, overcoming limitations of traditional methods.

title: Testing Neural Networks via Bayesian-Guided Exploration of Decision Landscapes
summary: As neural networks are increasingly deployed in safety-critical domains, testing is essential to evaluate and improve their reliability. Existing testing methods, whether black-box or white-box, primarily use global mutation or coverage-guided strategies, both of which struggle to efficiently uncover diverse model failures while remaining proximate to the original data distribution and semantics. We propose BayesWarp, a testing framework that addresses this limitation by mutating decision-critical input regions identified via interpretable saliency techniques and adaptively guiding the testing process using an uncertainty-aware Bayesian Optimization strategy, enabling the discovery of diverse failures while preserving distributional and semantic proximity to the original data. Evaluation on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and ImageNet across six neural network models shows that BayesWarp improves failure discovery, failure diversity, test case quality, and critical neuron coverage under a fixed mutation budget. These results demonstrate that BayesWarp improves testing effectiveness. Moreover, fine-tuning with the generated failure cases leads to improvements in model performance.

AI.tldr: BayesWarp is a neural network testing framework that uses interpretable saliency techniques to identify decision-critical input regions and an uncertainty-aware Bayesian Optimization strategy to guide mutation, efficiently discovering diverse failures while preserving data distribution and semantics.
AI.motivation: Existing testing methods (global mutation or coverage-guided) struggle to efficiently uncover diverse model failures while staying close to the original data distribution and semantics.
AI.method: BayesWarp mutates decision-critical input regions identified via saliency techniques and adaptively guides testing using uncertainty-aware Bayesian Optimization.
AI.result: On MNIST, CIFAR-10, and ImageNet across six models, BayesWarp improves failure discovery, failure diversity, test case quality, and critical neuron coverage under a fixed mutation budget.
AI.conclusion: BayesWarp improves testing effectiveness, and fine-tuning with generated failure cases improves model performance.

title: 4D Reconstruction from Sparse Dynamic Cameras
summary: Although dynamic 3D (i.e., 4D) reconstruction from a monocular dynamic camera has recently advanced, it remains fundamentally limited by depth ambiguity. In this paper, we focus on an alternative practical way, i.e., sparse dynamic camera setup, where a handful of independently moving cameras capture the same subjects. While keeping capture costs low, this setup introduces multi-view constraints and remains practical for real-world video production such as sports, concerts, and TV shows. Despite its potential, our experiments show that naive extensions of existing monocular or dense-fixed camera-based methods are insufficient since they fail to resolve the complex spatiotemporal inconsistencies across views and time. To fill this gap, we propose a simple yet effective 3D track initialization method designed to ensure spatiotemporal consistency by integrating inter-camera feature matching with intra-camera point tracking. Additionally, we incorporate a noise-robust depth-ordering regularization loss and a spatiotemporally diverse batch sampling strategy to enhance optimization stability and cross-view generalization. Furthermore, to address the lack of standardized benchmarks for this task, we introduce LetCamsGo, a new real-world video dataset with 5 sequences across 4 diverse environments, recorded by three independently moving cameras and one fixed camera. Comprehensive benchmarking on LetCamsGo demonstrated that our proposed framework improves 4D reconstruction quality in dynamic regions compared with baselines, paving the way for a low-cost 4D reconstruction paradigm in the wild.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a 4D reconstruction framework for sparse dynamic camera setups (independently moving cameras), introducing a 3D track initialization method with spatiotemporal consistency, noise-robust regularization, and a new benchmark dataset (LetCamsGo) to improve reconstruction quality in dynamic scenes.
AI.motivation: Monocular dynamic 3D reconstruction suffers from depth ambiguity, while dense fixed-camera setups are costly and impractical. The authors aim to enable low-cost, practical 4D reconstruction using a sparse dynamic camera setup (e.g., sports, concerts, TV shows) where only a few independently moving cameras capture the same subjects.
AI.method: 1) A 3D track initialization method integrating inter-camera feature matching with intra-camera point tracking for spatiotemporal consistency. 2) A noise-robust depth-ordering regularization loss. 3) A spatiotemporally diverse batch sampling strategy. 4) Introduction of LetCamsGo dataset (5 sequences, 4 environments, 3 moving + 1 fixed camera).
AI.result: The proposed framework improves 4D reconstruction quality in dynamic regions compared to baselines, as demonstrated through comprehensive benchmarking on the LetCamsGo dataset.
AI.conclusion: The work paves the way for a low-cost 4D reconstruction paradigm in real-world, in-the-wild scenarios using sparse dynamic camera setups.

title: OpenRFM: Dissecting Relational In-Context Learning
summary: Relational Foundation Models (RFMs) promise a single pre-trained predictor that, given any relational database, returns predictions in one forward pass via relational in-context learning (ICL). Yet a substantial gap separates open RFMs from their commercial counterparts, and the origin of this gap has not been systematically understood. We dissect a representative framework, the Relational Transformer (RT), from two perspectives. Model side: we show that RT performs relation-level ICL, and a kernel regression view shows it fails when sparse label-cell coverage yields an underdetermined regression. Data side: we ablate RT's pre-training source and find that existing synthetic-only pre-training and in-distribution pre-training drive the same architecture into different regimes, lazy vs. feature-learning. Probing this gap reveals that the missing ingredient is a support-identifiable relational latent in the label-generation process. These two diagnoses translate into (1) a dual-stage ICL architecture that combines the relational backbone with a batch-level ICL layer lifted from a pre-trained tabular foundation model to overcome relation-level label scarcity, and (2) a homophily-aware synthetic plus continual real-data pre-training mixture, augmented with a prototype-based regularization. These choices define OpenRFM, a simple yet effective RFM that improves average task performance by approximately 30% over the RT backbone and surpasses the commercial model KumoRFMv1 on a large set of evaluation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies why open-source Relational Foundation Models (RFMs) underperform commercial ones, finding two root causes: (1) relation-level in-context learning fails under sparse label coverage, and (2) synthetic-only pre-training leads to lazy vs. feature-learning regimes. The authors propose OpenRFM, combining a dual-stage ICL architecture and a homophily-aware pre-training mixture, achieving ~30% improvement over the RT backbone and surpassing KumoRFMv1.
AI.motivation: To systematically understand why open-source Relational Foundation Models (RFMs) significantly underperform their commercial counterparts, and to bridge this performance gap through principled architectural and data-level improvements.
AI.method: The authors dissect the Relational Transformer (RT) from two perspectives: (1) model side—showing RT performs relation-level ICL, with a kernel regression view revealing failure under sparse label-cell coverage; (2) data side—ablating pre-training source to show synthetic-only vs. in-distribution pre-training drive different learning regimes (lazy vs. feature-learning). They propose OpenRFM with (a) a dual-stage ICL architecture combining relational backbone + batch-level ICL from a tabular foundation model, and (b) homophily-aware synthetic + continual real-data pre-training with prototype-based regularization.
AI.result: OpenRFM improves average task performance by approximately 30% over the RT backbone and surpasses the commercial model KumoRFMv1 on a large set of evaluation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The gap between open and commercial RFMs stems from both architectural limitations (relation-level ICL failure under sparse labels) and pre-training data issues (synthetic-only causing lazy learning). The proposed fixes—dual-stage ICL and homophily-aware pre-training mixture—effectively close this gap.

title: COMBINER: Composed Image Retrieval Guided by Attribute-based Neighbor Relations
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) represents a challenging retrieval task that targets locating specific images through multimodal inputs. Despite recent progress in CIR techniques, prior approaches often overlook cases where images appear visually alike yet differ in attributes, potentially undermining both multimodal feature fusion and similarity modeling. To mitigate this limitation, we design a unified representation of cross-modal features based on attribute prototypes. Nevertheless, the task is far from straightforward, owing to three core issues: (1) entanglement in attribute-level semantics, (2) inconsistency across modalities, and (3) supervised signal missing. To tackle the above obstacles, we introduce a COMposed image retrieval network guided By attrIbute-based NEighbor Relations (COMBINER). Specifically, we first design an Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement module, which is capable of disentangling attribute features based on multimodal primitive features. Secondly, we propose a Unified Prototype-based Composition module, which can construct cross-modal unified prototypes (CUP) and facilitate multimodal feature composition. Finally, we introduce a Dual Relations Modeling module, which can mine pairwise and neighbor relations based on attribute similarity. Compared to traditional neighbor relations modeling CIR methods, COMBINER represents the first study addressing the phenomenon of visually similar but attribute-unrelated samples. It achieves a more accurate understanding of the semantic relations among samples by employing an attribute prototype-based similarity metric. Comprehensive experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets confirm the effectiveness of our proposed COMBINER. The implementation of our method will be accessed at https://github.com/Lee-zixu/COMBINER

AI.tldr: COMBINER is a new Composed Image Retrieval method that tackles the problem of visually similar but attribute-different images by using attribute prototypes to disentangle features, create unified cross-modal representations, and model pairwise/neighbor relations, achieving state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing CIR methods fail when images look visually alike but differ in attributes, leading to poor multimodal fusion and similarity modeling. The paper identifies three core issues: attribute-level semantic entanglement, cross-modal inconsistency, and lack of supervised signals.
AI.method: Three modules: (1) Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement module to separate attribute features from multimodal primitives; (2) Unified Prototype-based Composition module to build cross-modal unified prototypes (CUP) for feature composition; (3) Dual Relations Modeling module to mine pairwise and neighbor relations via attribute-based similarity.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on three benchmark datasets confirm the effectiveness of COMBINER, particularly in handling visually similar but attribute-unrelated samples. Code is available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: COMBINER is the first CIR method addressing visually similar but attribute-unrelated samples, achieving more accurate semantic relation understanding through attribute prototype-based similarity.

title: Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flows for Bayesian Inference of Diffusions with Inaccessible Boundaries
summary:
One of the primary challenges in Bayesian inference on the parameters of a diffusion model from discrete observations is the unavailability of an analytical expression for the transition density function between consecutive observation times, which is needed to derive the likelihood function.
  Extending previous studies that solve Fokker-Planck (FP) type partial differential equations with Normalizing Flows, we propose a new Normalizing Flow architecture to learn the transition density function of the diffusion process between two observation times. We do so by solving in a Neural Galerkin framework the associated FP equation with a Dirac mass as initial condition, over a specified training distribution of the initial datum and the coefficients of the diffusion. We specifically focus on processes whose diffusion matrix vanishes in certain inaccessible boundary regions, such as Stochastic Volatility models that satisfy a Feller condition.
  The product of the obtained transition densities evaluated along the observed trajectory approximates the likelihood function, thereby enabling cheap posterior sampling via Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC). After the offline training phase, inference becomes significantly more efficient, as it avoids the need to solve the FP equation in real time for each parameter proposed by the MCMC sampler or to rely on other likelihood-free methods for Bayesian inference that involve repeated simulation of diffusion bridges.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Normalizing Flow architecture within a Neural Galerkin framework to learn the transition density of diffusion processes by solving the Fokker-Planck equation, enabling efficient Bayesian inference via MCMC after offline training.
AI.motivation: The lack of an analytical transition density function for diffusion models makes Bayesian inference difficult, as the likelihood function cannot be directly computed from discrete observations.
AI.method: The proposed method uses a Normalizing Flow architecture to solve the Fokker-Planck equation with a Dirac mass initial condition in a Neural Galerkin framework, specifically designed for processes with vanishing diffusion at inaccessible boundaries (e.g., Stochastic Volatility models satisfying a Feller condition).
AI.result: The product of learned transition densities approximates the likelihood function, enabling cheap posterior sampling via MCMC without solving the FP equation in real-time or relying on simulation-based inference methods.
AI.conclusion: After offline training, Bayesian inference becomes significantly more efficient by avoiding repeated solutions of the FP equation or diffusion bridge simulations during inference.

title: Beyond Symmetric Alignment: Spectral Diagnostics of Modality Imbalance in Vision-Language Models in the Medical Domain
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle when applied to medical image-text data, yet the tools available to diagnose this failure remain limited. Existing representation alignment metrics are symmetric, collapsing both modalities into a single score and hiding which modality drives cross-modal degradation. We introduce the Spectral Alignment Score (SAS), an asymmetric metric that projects both modalities onto the principal eigenbasis of an anchor modality and computes eigenvalue-weighted per-eigenmode correlations, resulting in directional scores whose difference quantifies modality information imbalance. We embed SAS within a benchmarking framework evaluating 15 VLMs across natural and medical image-text datasets alongside 6 alignment metrics and bidirectional retrieval. Our experiments show that medical images retain richer structural information than their paired clinical reports, a directional asymmetry invisible to all competing metrics, and that SAS achieves the strongest zero-label correlation with retrieval performance in the medical domain, positioning it as a practical diagnostic tool for clinical deployment. Code is available at this URL: https://github.com/iamalegambetti/medical-vlms-assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SAS, an asymmetric metric to diagnose cross-modal degradation in VLMs applied to medical data, revealing that medical images hold richer information than paired clinical reports—a finding invisible to symmetric metrics—and demonstrating SAS's strong correlation with retrieval performance.
AI.motivation: Existing symmetric alignment metrics collapse both modalities into a single score, hiding which modality causes cross-modal degradation in VLMs applied to medical data.
AI.method: SAS projects both modalities onto the principal eigenbasis of an anchor modality and computes eigenvalue-weighted per-eigenmode correlations, yielding directional scores whose difference quantifies modality information imbalance.
AI.result: Medical images retain richer structural information than paired clinical reports (asymmetry invisible to symmetric metrics); SAS achieves the strongest zero-label correlation with retrieval performance in the medical domain.
AI.conclusion: SAS serves as a practical diagnostic tool for clinical deployment of VLMs, offering directional insights into modality imbalance that symmetric metrics cannot provide.

title: Measuring What Matters: Synthetic Benchmarks for Concept Bottleneck Models
summary: Concept bottleneck models predict outcomes from high-level concepts detected in inputs. Although concepts provide a simple way to reap benefits from interpretability, very few datasets include concept labels. This limits researchers' ability to determine which problems are suitable for these models, isolate the factors that drive their performance or lead to failures, or uncover which algorithms perform well. In this paper, we develop synthetic benchmarks for concept-bottleneck models, focusing on their two main use cases: decision support, in which models assist humans in making better decisions, and automation, in which models handle routine tasks without supervision. Our benchmarks can generate labeled datasets while controlling for properties that affect performance, including data modality, concept choice, annotation quality, and completeness. We demonstrate how the benchmarks can be used to evaluate representative classes of concept bottleneck models. Our demonstrations show how the benchmarks can diagnose failure modes and guide follow-up testing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces synthetic benchmarks for concept bottleneck models that can generate labeled datasets while controlling for properties like data modality, concept choice, annotation quality, and completeness, enabling better evaluation of these models in decision support and automation use cases.
AI.motivation: Few datasets include concept labels, limiting the study of concept bottleneck models. The authors aim to provide controllable synthetic benchmarks to systematically evaluate these models and diagnose their failure modes.
AI.method: Development of synthetic benchmarks that can generate labeled datasets while controlling key properties (data modality, concept choice, annotation quality, completeness) affecting performance. The benchmarks cover two main use cases: decision support and automation.
AI.result: Demonstrates how the benchmarks can be used to evaluate representative classes of concept bottleneck models, diagnose failure modes, and guide follow-up testing.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic benchmarks provide a valuable tool for understanding when concept bottleneck models work, why they fail, and which algorithms perform best, addressing the current limitations caused by scarcity of labeled datasets.

title: MeshFlow: Efficient Artistic Mesh Generation via MeshVAE and Flow-based Diffusion Transformer
summary: We present MeshFlow, a new method for generating artist-like 3D meshes. Current mesh generators often adopt Auto-Regressive (AR) next-token prediction, a natural choice given the discrete nature of mesh topology. However, AR methods scale poorly because the inference cost is quadratic in mesh size. They also require discretizing the vertex coordinates, which introduces quantization errors. To address these challenges, we introduce a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) that, supervised with a contrastive loss, represents both continuous vertex positions and discrete connectivity in a continuous latent space. This latent space is significantly more compact than prior token-based mesh representations. We then build a 3D generator based on a Rectified Flow transformer, generating all mesh vertices and edges in parallel. Our model generates meshes 18x faster than the fastest AR generator while also achieving excellent accuracy across standard mesh-generation metrics. Homepage: https://mesh-flow.github.io/, Code: https://github.com/facebookresearch/meshflow

AI.tldr: MeshFlow is a new 3D mesh generation method using a VAE with contrastive loss and a Rectified Flow transformer to generate meshes 18x faster than AR methods while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: AR-based mesh generators are slow (quadratic inference cost) and suffer from quantization errors due to discretized vertex coordinates.
AI.method: A VAE with contrastive loss encodes continuous vertex positions and discrete connectivity into a compact continuous latent space, then a Rectified Flow transformer generates all mesh vertices and edges in parallel.
AI.result: 18x faster inference than the fastest AR generator with excellent accuracy on standard mesh-generation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Parallel generation via flow-based models outperforms AR approaches in efficiency while preserving mesh quality.

title: A Geometric Characterization of the Stationary Plateau for Two-Layer Neural Networks
summary: We investigate the geometric structure of stationary plateaus that arise in the loss landscape of two-layer neural networks with smooth activation functions. We focus on the phenomenon of "neuron splitting" where duplicating a hidden neuron yields an affine set of stationary points in a wider network. We provide a comprehensive classification of all stationary points on these plateaus, determining under what conditions they constitute local minima or saddle points. Our characterization hinges on a per-neuron curvature object we term the "inner Hessian" matrix. Our analysis reveals that the definiteness of the inner Hessian and the choice of splitting coefficients jointly dictate the local geometry of the plateau. We show that "splitting" a local minimum can yield either a mixture of local minima and saddles or an all-saddle plateau, with a concrete sure-saddle region identified under mild assumptions. In contrast, splitting a saddle point always produces a plateau of saddle points. Our results unify and extend prior landscape analyses, elucidating when and how model expansion preserves or alters the nature of stationary points. These findings offer new geometric insights into the effects of width expansion and reparameterization in neural networks.

AI.tldr: This paper classifies stationary points on plateaus arising from neuron splitting in two-layer neural networks, showing that splitting local minima yields mixed outcomes (minima or saddles) while splitting saddles always yields saddles, governed by an "inner Hessian" matrix.
AI.motivation: To understand the geometric structure of stationary plateaus in the loss landscape of two-layer neural networks, particularly the phenomenon of neuron splitting and how it affects the nature of stationary points when expanding model width.
AI.method: The authors introduce a per-neuron curvature object called the "inner Hessian" matrix, analyze its definiteness, and examine how splitting coefficients jointly determine the local geometry of stationary plateaus. They provide a comprehensive classification framework for all stationary points on these plateaus.
AI.result: Splitting a local minimum can yield either a mixture of local minima and saddles or an all-saddle plateau, with a concrete sure-saddle region identified under mild assumptions. Splitting a saddle point always produces a plateau of saddle points. The definiteness of the inner Hessian and splitting coefficients jointly dictate the local geometry.
AI.conclusion: The results unify and extend prior landscape analyses, offering new geometric insights into how width expansion and reparameterization affect the nature of stationary points in neural networks.

title: Instance-Level Post Hoc Uncertainty Quantification in Object Detection
summary: Object detection is a safety-critical component of autonomous driving. It is essential to quantify the uncertainty in bounding-box predictions for safety assurance. Post hoc uncertainty quantification without retraining aligns with real-world deployment requirements; therefore, we employ the Laplace approximation. Because instance-level uncertainty is needed, linearized inference methods that require multiple backpropagations are not time-efficient, and sampling-based methods are not fully post hoc. We propose Monte-Carlo generalized linearized model (MC-GLM), which provides instance-level and approximately post hoc uncertainty quantification. The number of samples required in the Monte Carlo step is constant and independent of the number of output instances, so it can be parallelized. Experiments on the nuScenes dataset with the CenterPoint detector validate the effectiveness of our method, and the resulting uncertainties exhibit good quality.

AI.tldr: MC-GLM enables efficient, approximately post hoc, instance-level uncertainty quantification for object detection in autonomous driving using Monte-Carlo generalized linearized models with constant-time parallel sampling.
AI.motivation: Object detection is safety-critical in autonomous driving, and uncertainty quantification is essential for safety assurance. Existing methods either require retraining (not post hoc), are time-inefficient due to multiple backpropagations, or are not fully post hoc. A practical, efficient, post hoc solution for instance-level uncertainty is needed.
AI.method: Proposes MC-GLM (Monte-Carlo generalized linearized model), which applies Laplace approximation and Monte-Carlo sampling with a constant sample count independent of output instances, enabling parallelization and approximately post hoc instance-level uncertainty quantification without retraining.
AI.result: Experiments on nuScenes dataset with CenterPoint detector validate effectiveness; generated uncertainties exhibit good quality.
AI.conclusion: MC-GLM offers a practical, parallelizable, and near-post-hoc solution for instance-level uncertainty in autonomous driving object detection, balancing efficiency and uncertainty quality.

title: Policy Gradient for Continuous-Time Robust Markov Decision Processes
summary: The framework of robust Markov decision processes (RMDPs) allows the design of reinforcement learning agents that satisfy performance guarantees under worst-case transition dynamics. Traditional RMDPs consider discrete-time dynamics and recently, sample-efficient policy gradient algorithms have been considered in this context. This paper investigates policy gradient algorithms within a continuous-time RMDP framework. Policy gradients and adversarial gradients are derived using pathwise and adjoint-based formulas for stochastic and ordinary differential equations. We propose double-loop optimisers to obtain linear convergence in the oracle-based setting and an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{1}{ε^2})$ sample complexity in the sample-based setting in an analysis which also derives novel tools for the framework of undiscounted total cost MDPs. Additionally, we propose mean-field optimisers as distributional optimisers with an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{1}{K})$ oracle-based convergence rate and an $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\frac{N^2}ε)$ sample complexity under $N$-particle approximation. The effectiveness of continuous-time policy gradient algorithms is confirmed for both optimisers on continuous-time RMDPs with neural ordinary differential equation dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper extends policy gradient algorithms to continuous-time robust Markov decision processes (RMDPs), proposing double-loop and mean-field optimizers with proven convergence rates and sample complexity bounds.
AI.motivation: To bring policy gradient algorithms with performance guarantees to continuous-time RMDPs, moving beyond the discrete-time setting that has been the primary focus of existing robust RL research.
AI.method: Derives policy and adversarial gradients using pathwise and adjoint-based formulas for SDEs and ODEs. Proposes double-loop optimizers (linear oracle convergence, Õ(1/ε²) sample complexity) and mean-field optimizers (Õ(1/K) oracle convergence, Õ(N²/ε) sample complexity).
AI.result: Double-loop optimizers achieve linear convergence in oracle setting and Õ(1/ε²) sample complexity. Mean-field optimizers achieve Õ(1/K) oracle convergence and Õ(N²/ε) sample complexity under N-particle approximation. Effectiveness confirmed on continuous-time RMDPs with neural ODE dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Continuous-time policy gradient algorithms are effective for robust control, with the proposed optimizers providing strong theoretical guarantees and practical performance on neural ODE environments.

title: Real-Time Automatic License Plate Recognition Using YOLOv8, SORT Tracking, and Temporal Data Interpolation
summary: The real-time hardships of video processing seriously limit the usage of Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR) with application in dynamic traffic monitoring settings. High-fidelity recognition of unconstrained variables, e.g. drastic variations in illumination, acute camera scans, high vehicle speeds, and harsh physical concealment, is a problem that often leads to disjointed tracking paths and poor Optical Character Recognition (OCR) rates. In order to mitigate these weaknesses, the study proposes a 5 stage, end-to-end algorithmic pipeline, encompassing a smooth transition between deep learning based object detection, multi-object tracking which is kinematic in nature, and geometry temporal data interpolation. The suggested architecture takes advantage of a very powerful YOLOv8 nano model to localize the vehicle at the first stage and then Simple Online and Realtime Tracking (SORT) algorithm is used to build spatial-temporal links between frames. Another, more specific typology of YOLOv8 object detectors the license plate area, channeling the sliced array to an EasyOCR chain under the limitations of positional syntax verification. More importantly, an offline interpolation mechanism of temporal bounding box is initiated to recast fragmented paths.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a 5-stage ALPR pipeline using YOLOv8 nano for vehicle detection, SORT for multi-object tracking, a secondary YOLOv8 for license plate detection, EasyOCR with syntax verification, and offline temporal bounding box interpolation to address real-time video processing challenges like lighting variation, camera motion, and high vehicle speeds.
AI.motivation: Real-time video processing limitations severely restrict ALPR usage in dynamic traffic monitoring, especially under challenging conditions such as illumination changes, acute camera angles, high speeds, and physical concealment—leading to fragmented tracking and poor OCR rates.
AI.method: A 5-stage end-to-end pipeline: (1) YOLOv8 nano for vehicle localization, (2) SORT algorithm for multi-object kinematic tracking across frames, (3) specialized YOLOv8 for license plate detection, (4) EasyOCR with positional syntax verification for text recognition, (5) offline temporal bounding box interpolation to repair fragmented tracking paths.
AI.result: Not specified in the abstract; results are not detailed.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pipeline with temporal interpolation mitigates tracking fragmentation and OCR degradation caused by real-world video processing hardships, though specific quantitative outcomes are not provided in the abstract.

title: Federated Learning for Multi-Center Sepsis Early Prediction with Privacy-Preserving
summary: Privacy-sensitive and distributed characteristics of multi-center medical data bring severe obstacles to centralized modeling for accurate early prediction of sepsis. Federated learning (FL) has attracted growing attention as a promising framework for collaborative model development, as it allows multiple institutions to jointly train predictive models without directly sharing or centralizing raw data. Nevertheless, its practical performance, robustness, and privacy-preserving benefits remain insufficiently evaluated using real-world clinical datasets. To bridge this gap, this study systematically examines the application of federated learning to multi-center sepsis prediction. The experimental dataset consists of 648 clinically screened samples collected from three tertiary hospitals in China, with rigorous inclusion and exclusion criteria. We establish a centralized training paradigm as the performance baseline, and then implement a horizontal federated learning framework for distributed collaborative modeling. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the federated learning-based model achieves highly comparable prediction accuracy to the centralized counterpart, while fundamentally avoiding privacy leakage. Further privacy security analysis verifies that malicious attackers cannot reconstruct the original patient data from the transmitted model parameters, indicating strong resistance against data reconstruction attacks. This work not only validates the practicality and security of federated learning in clinical sepsis prediction, but also provides a reliable and feasible solution for privacy-preserving multi-center medical collaboration.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates federated learning for multi-center sepsis prediction using real clinical data from three hospitals, showing it achieves comparable accuracy to centralized models while preventing privacy leakage and resisting data reconstruction attacks.
AI.motivation: Privacy-sensitive and distributed multi-center medical data hinder centralized modeling for accurate early sepsis prediction; federated learning offers a potential solution but lacks sufficient real-world evaluation.
AI.method: Used 648 clinically screened samples from three tertiary hospitals; established centralized training baseline, then implemented horizontal federated learning for distributed collaborative modeling; conducted privacy security analysis against data reconstruction attacks.
AI.result: Federated learning model achieved highly comparable prediction accuracy to centralized counterpart; malicious attackers could not reconstruct original patient data from transmitted model parameters.
AI.conclusion: Validates practicality and security of federated learning in clinical sepsis prediction, providing a reliable solution for privacy-preserving multi-center medical collaboration.

title: MeshWeaver: Sparse-Voxel-Guided Surface Weaving for Autoregressive Mesh Generation
summary: Autoregressive mesh generation has gained attention by tokenizing meshes into sequences and training models in a language-modeling fashion. However, existing approaches suffer from two fundamental limitations: (i) low tokenization efficiency, which yields long token sequences and prevents scaling to high-poly meshes, and (ii) absence of geometry-aware guidance, as generation is conditioned only on global shape embeddings rather than local surface cues. We introduce MeshWeaver, an autoregressive framework that treats mesh generation as a surface weaving process by directly predicting the next vertex instead of independent coordinates. At its core is a multi-level sparse-voxel encoder that injects geometric context into the generative process in three complementary ways: providing voxel features as vertex representations, guiding token prediction via cross-attention to voxel features, and serving as a structural scaffold that constrains generation around the input surface. Our hierarchical design enables coarse-to-fine vertex prediction in a single decoding step, while tightly coupling the generative model with 3D geometry. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MeshWeaver achieves a state-of-the-art compression ratio of 18%, can generate meshes with up to 16K faces, and significantly improves geometric fidelity over prior approaches.

AI.tldr: MeshWeaver is an autoregressive mesh generation framework that uses multi-level sparse-voxel encoding to inject geometric context, achieving 18% compression ratio, support for meshes up to 16K faces, and improved geometric fidelity.
AI.motivation: Existing autoregressive mesh generation methods suffer from low tokenization efficiency (long sequences, cannot scale to high-poly meshes) and absence of geometry-aware guidance (conditioned only on global shape embeddings, not local surface cues).
AI.method: MeshWeaver predicts the next vertex directly (treats generation as surface weaving) using a multi-level sparse-voxel encoder that provides three functions: (1) voxel features as vertex representations, (2) cross-attention for token prediction guidance, (3) structural scaffold constraining generation around input surface. Hierarchical design enables coarse-to-fine vertex prediction in a single decoding step.
AI.result: Achieves 18% compression ratio (state-of-the-art), can generate meshes with up to 16K faces, and significantly improves geometric fidelity over prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: The tight coupling of generative model with 3D geometry via hierarchical sparse-voxel encoding effectively addresses tokenization inefficiency and lack of geometric guidance in autoregressive mesh generation.

title: Literature-Guided Minimax Optimization of Virtual Epilepsy Neurostimulation
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures. Code and interactive essay at https://github.com/liuzhitong330/tvb-llm-robust-neurostim
summary: Computational models of epilepsy promise patient-specific treatment design, but most optimization workflows still search for parameters that perform well on average. In neuromodulation, this is a weak target: a protocol that improves the mean response can still fail in the patient whose network is least tolerant to stimulation. We present a literature-guided minimax pipeline that couples PubMed-scale hypothesis extraction, The Virtual Brain (TVB) Epileptor simulations, and large-language-model-guided black-box optimization. The optimizer proposes either intrinsic model-control parameters or clinically interpretable external-stimulation protocols; TVB evaluates each proposal across sampled virtual patients; and the objective maximizes worst-case reward, defined as the negative variance of simulated seizure activity. In the intrinsic model-control experiment, the best archived parameter set improved worst-case reward from -0.5285 to -0.3182, a 39.8% gain over baseline. The clinical-style external-stimulation search produced a much smaller worst-case improvement (1.7%), and a 20-patient virtual cohort showed no aggregate benefit (p=0.9019), despite a 55% responder rate and a positive temporal-lobe subgroup signal. The study should be read as an in silico proof of concept for robust, literature-aware neurostimulation design, not as clinical evidence.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a minimax optimization pipeline combining PubMed literature mining, The Virtual Brain Epileptor simulations, and LLM-guided black-box optimization to design robust epilepsy neuromodulation protocols that improve worst-case outcomes across patient variability, demonstrating a 39.8% gain in intrinsic parameter control but limited improvement in external stimulation.
AI.motivation: Standard optimization approaches for epilepsy neuromodulation focus on average performance, which can fail for patients least tolerant to stimulation. The authors aim to develop a robust, patient-specific approach that optimizes for worst-case outcomes.
AI.method: The pipeline integrates PubMed-scale hypothesis extraction, TVB Epileptor simulations, and LLM-guided black-box optimization. The optimizer searches intrinsic model-control parameters or external-stimulation protocols, with TVB evaluating proposals across sampled virtual patients. The objective maximizes worst-case reward (negative variance of simulated seizure activity).
AI.result: Intrinsic control: worst-case reward improved from -0.5285 to -0.3182 (39.8% gain). External stimulation: only 1.7% worst-case improvement; no aggregate benefit across 20-patient cohort (p=0.9019), though 55% responder rate and positive temporal-lobe subgroup signal were observed.
AI.conclusion: This is an in silico proof of concept for robust, literature-aware neurostimulation design, not clinical evidence.

title: A New Angle on Bones: Robust Pose Estimation in X-Ray and Ultrasound
comment: Code and annotations for fracture angle assessment in radiographs: https://github.com/multimodallearning/RobustBonePoseEstimation
summary: Measuring the angle between bone structures is a routine task in medical image analysis and provides a key quantitative parameter for diagnosis and treatment planning. Automated methods can reduce time and cost while improving reproducibility. In this work, we address automatic bone pose estimation using a learning-based point candidate proposal followed by a line model to extract axis parameters. Since conventional line models such as least squares are sensitive to outliers, we incorporate false-positive reduction strategies and robust fitting techniques, such as RANSAC and Hough transforms, to improve robustness. We evaluate our method on three clinically relevant paediatric angle estimation tasks: fracture fragment assessment in radiographs and ultrasound and developmental dysplasia of the hip evaluation in ultrasound using the Graf method. Our approach achieves mean errors of $4.1^\circ$, $5.4^\circ$, and $5.51^\circ$, respectively, not only remaining within the expected clinical observer variability, but also significantly outperforming landmark-based methods. Our code and annotations for fracture angle assessment in radiographs are publicly available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: A learning-based method for automatic bone pose estimation using point candidate proposals and robust line fitting (RANSAC/Hough) achieves clinically acceptable angle measurement errors on three pediatric tasks, outperforming landmark-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Measuring bone angles is routine but manual; automated methods can reduce time, cost, and improve reproducibility in medical image analysis.
AI.method: Learning-based point candidate proposal + line model (RANSAC, Hough transforms) with false-positive reduction for robust axis parameter extraction.
AI.result: Mean errors of 4.1° (fracture radiograph), 5.4° (fracture ultrasound), 5.51° (DDH ultrasound), within clinical observer variability and outperforming landmark-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed robust fitting approach achieves clinically acceptable accuracy for automated pediatric bone angle estimation across modalities.

title: Expectations vs. Realities: The Cost of MSE-Optimal Forecasting Under Conditional Uncertainty
comment: 12 pages, Accepted for KDD 2026 Research track
summary: Multi-step time series forecasting (MSF) is commonly evaluated using point-wise error metrics such as mean squared error (MSE), implicitly treating the conditional mean as a sufficient target. We show that this can be misleading under conditional uncertainty, where the conditional expectation becomes unrepresentative of typical realized values at longer horizons. We formalize this effect through a conditional uncertainty gap and prove that whenever this gap is nonzero, no deterministic predictor can simultaneously minimize MSE and match the marginal distribution of realized futures. This establishes a fundamental, model-agnostic trade-off between point accuracy and marginal realism in MSF evaluation. Using controlled stochastic dynamical systems and nine real-world forecasting benchmarks, we empirically characterize the resulting accuracy--realism frontier and \textbf{quantify the practical cost of MSE-only model selection}. As conditional uncertainty increases with forecast horizon, the attainable set expands into a pronounced Pareto front, separating MSE-optimal but under-dispersed predictors from methods that trade accuracy for realistic marginal variability. \textbf{Across benchmarks, we find that small relaxations in MSE ($\boldsymbol{\le 5\%}$) frequently unlock disproportionate gains in marginal realism, with median improvements of $\mathbf{17.3\%}$ and gains exceeding $\mathbf{30\%}$ in some datasets.} We further show that common forecasting strategies systematically occupy different regions of this frontier: direct multi-output predictors concentrate near the accuracy-optimal extreme, while recursive strategies and sample-based inference favors marginal realism. Together, these results expose a structural failure mode of MSE-based evaluation in long-horizon forecasting and recast strategy and inference selection as navigation of an unavoidable accuracy--realism trade-off.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates a fundamental trade-off between point accuracy (MSE) and marginal realism in multi-step time series forecasting under conditional uncertainty, proving that no deterministic predictor can achieve both simultaneously when a conditional uncertainty gap exists. Empirical results show small MSE relaxations (≤5%) yield disproportionate gains in marginal realism (median 17.3%).
AI.motivation: Multi-step forecasting is typically evaluated using MSE which treats the conditional mean as sufficient. The authors show this is misleading under conditional uncertainty, where the conditional expectation becomes unrepresentative of typical realized values at longer horizons, motivating the need to understand the accuracy-realism trade-off.
AI.method: The authors formalize a "conditional uncertainty gap" and prove theoretically that no deterministic predictor can simultaneously minimize MSE and match marginal distributions when this gap is nonzero. They empirically characterize the accuracy-realism frontier using controlled stochastic systems and nine real-world benchmarks.
AI.result: Small MSE relaxations (≤5%) unlock disproportionate gains in marginal realism with median improvements of 17.3% and gains exceeding 30% in some datasets. Different forecasting strategies (direct multi-output, recursive, sample-based) systematically occupy distinct regions of the Pareto frontier.
AI.conclusion: MSE-based evaluation has a structural failure mode in long-horizon forecasting. Strategy and inference selection should be reframed as navigation of an unavoidable accuracy-realism trade-off, rather than optimization of a single point-wise metric.

title: Benchmarking Living-Screen-Native GUI Agents on Short-Video Platforms
