summary: GUI agents today assume a static screen, where the world is frozen between two actions. However, real interfaces such as short-video applications violate this assumption, as their content keeps playing, and a competent user must decide what to watch and for how long. We formalize this task as Living-Screen-Native GUI agents and introduce LivingScreen, the first benchmark instantiating it on short-video platforms, with a faithful browser-based environment, a three-tier task suite, and metrics that jointly score accuracy and information efficiency. Evaluating extensive frontier models, we find that none reaches the human cost-accuracy performance, and that their dominant failure mode is over- and under-observation, pointing to observation control as a missing capability axis for future GUI agents. All data and code will be available at https://github.com/BITHLP/LivingScreen.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Living-Screen-Native GUI agents for dynamic interfaces (like short-video apps) where content plays continuously, and presents the LivingScreen benchmark to evaluate models on observation control, showing that current agents fail due to over- and under-observation compared to humans.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI agents assume static screens, but real applications like short-video platforms have continuously playing content, requiring agents to decide not only what actions to take but also when and how long to observe before acting.
AI.method: The authors formalize Living-Screen-Native GUI agents, build LivingScreen benchmark with a browser-based environment on short-video platforms, design a three-tier task suite, and propose metrics jointly scoring accuracy and information efficiency.
AI.result: No frontier model matches human cost-accuracy performance; the main failure mode is over-observation (watching too long) and under-observation (not watching enough), indicating a missing capability in observation control.
AI.conclusion: Observation control is a critical missing capability for future GUI agents, and LivingScreen provides a benchmark to drive progress in this direction.

title: MeshTok: Efficient Multi-Scale Tokenization for Scalable PDE Transformers
summary: Conventional patchified Transformers operate on uniform spatial partitions, distributing computational effort evenly across the domain irrespective of local features. This inflexible tokenization scheme is inherently limited in its ability to efficiently represent and process solutions to complex PDEs. To address this, we propose MeshTok, an adaptive mesh refinement (AMR)-inspired tokenization and sequence modeling framework. This method selectively refines spatial regions exhibiting sharp gradients, transient features, or multiscale structures, generating a heterogeneous set of multiscale tokens defined on a fixed simulation grid. These tokens are processed within a unified Transformer sequence, enabling the model to simultaneously capture coarse-grained global context and fine-grained local details without requiring specialized architectural components. Although adaptive refinement moderately increases token count, it promotes a more targeted allocation of computational resources to physically informative regions, which we view as a practical inductive bias rather than a formal optimality guarantee. Experimental evaluations across multiple PDE families and benchmark datasets demonstrate that MeshTok consistently improves the efficiency-accuracy trade-off compared to uniform-grid baselines. This suggests adaptive multiscale tokenization as a scalable and generalizable design principle for neural PDE modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/SCAILab-USTC/MeshTok.

AI.tldr: MeshTok proposes an adaptive mesh refinement-inspired tokenization method for Transformers that selectively refines spatial regions in PDE simulations, creating multiscale tokens to improve efficiency-accuracy trade-offs compared to uniform grid approaches.
AI.motivation: Conventional patchified Transformers use uniform spatial partitions that distribute computation evenly regardless of local features, which is inefficient for representing complex PDEs with sharp gradients, transient features, or multiscale structures.
AI.method: MeshTok uses adaptive mesh refinement (AMR)-inspired tokenization to selectively refine spatial regions with important features, generating heterogeneous multiscale tokens on a fixed simulation grid. These tokens are processed in a unified Transformer sequence to capture both coarse-grained global context and fine-grained local details without specialized architectural components.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations across multiple PDE families and benchmark datasets show consistent improvement in the efficiency-accuracy trade-off compared to uniform-grid baselines.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive multiscale tokenization serves as a scalable and generalizable design principle for neural PDE modeling, providing a practical inductive bias that allocates computational resources to physically informative regions.

title: Enhancing MedSAM with a Lightweight Box Predictor for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Semantic segmentation in medical imaging is a critical yet challenging task due to data scarcity and high variability across modalities. While foundation models like the Segment Anything Model (SAM) show promise, they often struggle with medical images without specific adaptation. Moreover, point prompts, despite being the most natural form of user interaction, provide insufficient spatial context for reliable segmentation, particularly when target structures are irregular or poorly contrasted. In this paper, we propose an enhanced segmentation framework that integrates a lightweight Box Predictor module into the MedSAM architecture. The Box Predictor estimates an approximate bounding box from a single user click using localized image embedding features, providing spatial guidance that reduces the ambiguity of point prompts, while introducing only 1.6M additional parameters and negligible inference overhead. We introduce a two-stage training pipeline where the Box Predictor is trained independently before being integrated into MedSAM. To validate the generalization capability of our method, we conduct extensive evaluations on four diverse datasets (FLARE22, BRISC, BUSI, LungSegDB) spanning distinct imaging modalities, including CT, MRI, and Ultrasound. Our method improves segmentation accuracy and robustness across varied anatomical structures and imaging domains, achieving Dice scores of 0.89 (BUSI), 0.93 (FLARE22), 0.88 (BRISC), and 0.98 (LungSegDB). Code is available at https://github.com/Amirhosseinmovahedi/MedSAM-BoxPredictor

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MedSAM-BoxPredictor, a framework that adds a lightweight Box Predictor module to MedSAM to convert a single user click into an approximate bounding box, improving segmentation accuracy across multiple medical imaging modalities with minimal added parameters.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation faces challenges from data scarcity and high modality variability. While SAM-based models show promise, point prompts (the most natural user interaction) provide insufficient spatial context for reliable segmentation, especially for irregular or low-contrast structures.
AI.method: The authors integrate a lightweight Box Predictor module (1.6M parameters) into the MedSAM architecture. The Box Predictor estimates a bounding box from a single user click using localized image embedding features. A two-stage training pipeline is used: first train the Box Predictor independently, then integrate it into MedSAM.
AI.result: Evaluated on four diverse datasets (FLARE22 CT, BRISC MRI, BUSI Ultrasound, LungSegDB CT), achieving Dice scores of 0.89 (BUSI), 0.93 (FLARE22), 0.88 (BRISC), and 0.98 (LungSegDB). The method improves segmentation accuracy and robustness across varied anatomical structures and imaging domains.
AI.conclusion: Adding a lightweight Box Predictor to MedSAM effectively reduces point prompt ambiguity and improves segmentation performance across multiple medical imaging modalities with minimal computational overhead.

title: When Do Fewer Coordinates Suffice in DP-SGD?
summary: Differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) injects noise into every updated coordinate, making the injected noise energy scale with the ambient parameter dimension \(d\). We ask when private training can update fewer coordinates without losing the signal needed for optimization. We propose \textsc{TP-TopK} (Two-Phase TopK DP-SGD), a two-phase method for coordinate-sparse private training without public data, in which a private warm-up phase identifies a coordinate support used to guide the main training phase. We give a criterion characterizing when coordinate restriction can be beneficial, show via a nonconvex stationarity bound that under this condition the relevant noise term scales with the active dimension \(k\) rather than the full parameter dimension \(d\), and provide a lower bound on the reliability of warm-up-based coordinate ranking. Experiments on MNIST, FMNIST, and CIFAR-10 show that learned coordinate supports can retain more gradient energy than size-matched random supports, with the largest gains when the active dimension is small and warm-up scores are informative.

AI.tldr: DP-SGD injects noise scaling with dimension \(d\). This paper proposes TP-TopK, a two-phase method that privately identifies a smaller coordinate support \(k\) during a warm-up phase, then restricts updates to those coordinates in the main training phase. This reduces noise scaling from \(d\) to \(k\), improving utility for sparse problems.
AI.motivation: Standard DP-SGD adds noise to every parameter coordinate, causing the noise energy to scale with the full dimension \(d\). This is wasteful when many coordinates are unimportant. The authors ask whether private training can selectively update fewer coordinates without losing optimization signal.
AI.method: TP-TopK uses two phases: (1) a private warm-up phase (DP-SGD or DP-Fisher) to rank coordinates by importance (e.g., gradient magnitude) and select a top-\(k\) support, and (2) a main training phase that only updates coordinates within this support while freezing others. The support is identified privately via the exponential mechanism or thresholding with the Gaussian mechanism.
AI.result: The noise term scales with active dimension \(k\) instead of full dimension \(d\) under the proposed criterion. Experiments on MNIST, FMNIST, and CIFAR-10 show that learned supports retain more gradient energy than random supports, with largest gains when \(k\) is small and warm-up scores are informative.
AI.conclusion: Coordinate-sparse private training via a warm-up phase can significantly reduce the effective noise dimension, improving DP optimization efficiency. The approach works without public data and is particularly effective when the problem has sparse structure.

title: ReConFuse: Reconstruction-Error Guided Semantic Fusion for AI-Generated Video Detection
summary: AI-generated videos are becoming increasingly realistic, raising serious concerns about misinformation, content authenticity, and media trust. Reliable AI-generated video detection is therefore essential for multimedia forensics, yet remains challenging due to the need to capture spatial artifacts, temporal dynamics, and generalize to evolving generative models. In this paper, we explore reconstruction error as a discriminative forensic cue for AI-generated video detection. By reconstructing input videos with a pretrained WF-VAE, we observe that real and generated videos exhibit distinguishable frame-wise reconstruction error patterns, suggesting that reconstruction errors can reveal their distributional discrepancies. However, extending reconstruction-based image detection to videos is non-trivial, since video reconstruction errors are temporally organized across frames and require semantic context for effective interpretation. To address these challenges, we propose ReConFuse, a reconstruction-guided semantic fusion framework for video-level AI-generated video detection. ReConFuse extracts reconstruction error cues from WF-VAE reconstructed videos, aligns them with multi-frame semantic features, and uses a Mamba-based module to model temporal evolution for video-level classification. Experiments across multiple generators and evaluation settings demonstrate the effectiveness and strong generalization ability of ReConFuse.

AI.tldr: ReConFuse uses reconstruction errors from a pretrained WF-VAE combined with semantic features and Mamba-based temporal modeling to detect AI-generated videos, achieving strong generalization across multiple generators.
AI.motivation: AI-generated videos are becoming increasingly realistic, raising concerns about misinformation and media trust. Existing methods struggle to capture spatial artifacts, temporal dynamics, and generalize to evolving generative models.
AI.method: Proposes ReConFuse: extracts reconstruction error cues from WF-VAE reconstructed videos, aligns them with multi-frame semantic features, and uses a Mamba-based module to model temporal evolution for video-level classification.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple generators and evaluation settings demonstrate effectiveness and strong generalization ability.
AI.conclusion: Reconstruction error is a discriminative forensic cue for AI-generated video detection, and the proposed ReConFuse framework effectively leverages it with semantic fusion and temporal modeling.

title: From Symbolic to Geometric: Enabling Spatial Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary:
Recent large language models (LLMs) often appear to exhibit spatial reasoning ability; however, this capability is largely \emph{symbolic}, arising from pattern matching over spatial language rather than true \emph{geometric} reasoning over space. Because LLMs operate on discrete tokens, they lack native support for continuous spatial representations, explicit geometric computation, and structured spatial operators. To address this limitation, we introduce the \emph{Spatial Language Model (SLM)}, the first multimodal LLM that treats location information as a first-class modality and enables geometric spatial reasoning within the model's inference process. SLM directly operates on learned spatial representations rather than textual descriptions of spatial relations. To support effective training, we construct a \emph{Spatial Instruction Dataset} that aligns spatial representations, atomic geometric operations, and natural language instructions. We further propose a new benchmark named \emph{SpatialEval}, which is designed to evaluate spatial reasoning across attributes, distance, topology, and relative-position tasks. Extensive experiments show that SLM significantly outperforms existing LLM-based approaches that rely on symbolic reasoning via prompt engineering or textual abstraction, demonstrating the benefits of integrating geometric spatial representations for robust spatial reasoning.
  Our instruction dataset, evaluation benchmark, model training codes, and models' checkpoints can be found at:
  \hyperlink{https://github.com/chuchen2017/SLM}{https://github.com/chuchen2017/SLM}.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Spatial Language Model (SLM), a multimodal LLM that treats location as a first-class modality to perform genuine geometric spatial reasoning, outperforming symbolic-only approaches on a new benchmark called SpatialEval.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit spatial reasoning only symbolically via pattern matching over spatial language, lacking true geometric reasoning due to operating on discrete tokens without continuous spatial representations, explicit geometric computation, or structured spatial operators.
AI.method: SLM treats location as a first-class modality, directly operating on learned spatial representations rather than textual descriptions; trained on a Spatial Instruction Dataset that aligns spatial representations, atomic geometric operations, and natural language instructions.
AI.result: SLM significantly outperforms existing LLM-based symbolic reasoning approaches on the proposed SpatialEval benchmark across attributes, distance, topology, and relative-position tasks.
AI.conclusion: Integrating geometric spatial representations into LLMs benefits robust spatial reasoning, and SLM demonstrates the advantages of moving beyond pure symbolic pattern matching.

title: Data Efficient Complex Feature Fusion Network For Hyperspectral Image Classification
summary: This work presents a data-efficient variant of the Attention-Based Dual-Branch Complex Feature Fusion Network (CFFN) for hyperspectral image classification. The proposed model, termed DE-CFFN, retains the original two-stream structure: the Real-Valued Neural Network (RVNN) processes standard hyperspectral patches, while the Complex-Valued Neural Network (CVNN) handles their Fourier-transformed counterparts. The main contribution of this work lies in the feature extraction process and architectural enhancement. Factor Analysis is used for dimensionality reduction, offering improved latent feature representation over Principal Component Analysis. Additionally, both the RVNN and CVNN streams are structurally modified by successively halving the number of filters in the 3D convolutional layers to reduce complexity. The outputs of both branches are concatenated and passed through a Squeeze and Excitation (SE) block to enhance joint feature representation. Evaluated on the Pavia University and Salinas datasets, DE-CFFN achieves classification performance comparable to CFFN, while significantly reducing model size, memory consumption, and inference latency, making it suitable for real-time hyperspectral imaging applications.

AI.tldr: DE-CFFN is a data-efficient variant of the Attention-Based Dual-Branch Complex Feature Fusion Network for hyperspectral image classification, using Factor Analysis for dimensionality reduction and halved 3D convolutional filters to reduce model size and memory, achieving comparable accuracy to CFFN with significantly lower computational costs.
AI.motivation: To develop a data-efficient hyperspectral image classification model that reduces computational complexity and memory consumption while maintaining classification performance comparable to existing methods like CFFN, enabling real-time applications.
AI.method: DE-CFFN retains a two-stream architecture (RVNN for raw patches, CVNN for Fourier-transformed patches), uses Factor Analysis instead of PCA for better dimensionality reduction, successively halves 3D convolutional filters in both streams, and employs a Squeeze and Excitation (SE) block for joint feature enhancement.
AI.result: On Pavia University and Salinas datasets, DE-CFFN achieves classification performance comparable to CFFN while significantly reducing model size, memory consumption, and inference latency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DE-CFFN is suitable for real-time hyperspectral imaging applications due to its reduced computational footprint and maintained accuracy.

title: Revisiting Privacy Amplification by Subsampling in Selective Release DPSGD
summary: Machine learning's reliance on sensitive data necessitates privacy-preserving techniques like Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DPSGD). However, DPSGD suffers from substantial utility degradation and slow convergence due to gradient clipping and noise injection. Prior works have attempted to improve DPSGD from various perspectives; notably, the Differentially Private Selective Update and Release (DPSUR) algorithm has achieved remarkable model utility. However, the privacy accounting in DPSUR overlooks the variation in sampling probability introduced by the selective release mechanism, which compromises the rigor of its privacy guarantees. To address these limitations, we re-evaluate the privacy analysis of the selective release mechanism and propose a novel algorithm: Differentially Private Selective Release based on Clipped Gradients (DPSR-CG). Through a rigorous, newly derived privacy analysis and extensive experiments on multiple datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10, IMDB, and FMNIST), we demonstrate that our DPSR-CG mechanism maintains strict privacy guarantees while achieving exceptional model performance.

AI.tldr: DPSR-CG is a new differentially private algorithm that fixes privacy accounting flaws in prior work (DPSUR) by rigorously analyzing selective gradient release, achieving strong privacy guarantees with better model utility.
AI.motivation: DPSGD suffers from utility degradation and slow convergence; prior DPSUR has flawed privacy accounting that overlooks varying sampling probabilities from selective release, compromising privacy rigor.
AI.method: Re-evaluates privacy analysis of selective release mechanism and proposes DPSR-CG (Differentially Private Selective Release based on Clipped Gradients) with a newly derived, rigorous privacy analysis.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10, IMDB, and FMNIST demonstrate that DPSR-CG maintains strict privacy guarantees while achieving exceptional model performance.
AI.conclusion: DPSR-CG successfully fixes the privacy leakage in DPSUR and delivers both strong privacy and high utility.

title: StrokeTimer: Robust Representation Learning for Ischemic Stroke Onset-Time Estimation from Non-contrast CT
summary: Ischemic stroke is a major global disease. Treatment decisions are highly time-sensitive, as eligibility for reperfusion therapies relies on the interval between stroke onset and intervention. However, the true onset time is often uncertain in clinical practice, necessitating imaging-based assessment of tissue age as a surrogate marker. Early ischemic changes on routinely acquired non-contrast CT (NCCT) are often subtle, and real-world clinical datasets exhibit pronounced onset-time class imbalance and center-scanner-related heterogeneity. In this work, we propose StrokeTimer, a fully automated framework for onset-time estimation in acute ischemic stroke. StrokeTimer integrates self-supervised disentanglement learning with energy-guided contrastive learning to capture subtle ischemic patterns while addressing long-tailed data distributions under acquisition variability. Onset time is categorized into three clinically relevant windows: <4.5 h, 4.5-6 h, and >6 h. Experimental results on a large multi-center NCCT dataset from two national cohorts, MR CLEAN Registry and MR CLEAN LATE, show that StrokeTimer achieves a macro AUC of 0.69 and a macro F1-score of 0.57, improving the strongest baseline by nearly 50% (p < 0.005). In this realistic, challenging setting, representative baseline approaches exhibit near-chance macro performance. Model explanations further highlight subtle gray-white matter blurring and hypodense regions consistent with established radiological biomarkers. These findings demonstrate the potential of StrokeTimer to support treatment decision-making in acute ischemic stroke. Code is available at https://github.com/BrainVas/StrokeTimer.

AI.tldr: StrokeTimer is a fully automated framework using self-supervised disentanglement learning and energy-guided contrastive learning to estimate stroke onset time from non-contrast CT images, categorizing into three clinical windows (<4.5h, 4.5-6h, >6h). It achieves macro AUC of 0.69 on large multi-center datasets, improving baseline by ~50%.
AI.motivation: Ischemic stroke treatment decisions are highly time-sensitive, but true onset time is often uncertain. Existing methods struggle with subtle early ischemic changes on NCCT, severe class imbalance, and center-scanner heterogeneity in real-world clinical data.
AI.method: StrokeTimer integrates (1) self-supervised disentanglement learning to capture subtle ischemic patterns, and (2) energy-guided contrastive learning to address long-tailed data distributions and acquisition variability across centers/scanners.
AI.result: On two national cohorts (MR CLEAN Registry & MR CLEAN LATE), StrokeTimer achieves macro AUC of 0.69 and macro F1-score of 0.57, improving the strongest baseline by nearly 50% (p < 0.005). Representative baselines show near-chance performance.
AI.conclusion: StrokeTimer demonstrates potential to support treatment decision-making in acute ischemic stroke by reliably estimating onset time from NCCT, with radiologically consistent explanations (gray-white matter blurring, hypodense regions).

title: Shortcomings and capacities of real-constrained neural networks in complex spaces
summary: We find the asymptotic ratio between the storage capacities when enforcing real pre-activations in a complex hypothesis class as opposed to complex ones in the same class. Our methods depend on Gardner volume comparisons at critical capacity. Our proof relies on an application of the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber (HCIZ) formula, nonstandard in literature. With the HCIZ formula, we may obtain a more robust approximation for the final asymptotic ratio. This strategy is applicable to our work specifically since we integrate over the unitary and orthogonal compact manifolds, facilitated via the Weyl integration formula and the Haar measure.

AI.tldr: This paper derives the asymptotic storage capacity ratio between real and complex pre-activations in a hypothesis class using Gardner volume comparisons and the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber (HCIZ) formula.
AI.motivation: To find the asymptotic ratio of storage capacities when enforcing real vs. complex pre-activations in a complex hypothesis class.
AI.method: Uses Gardner volume comparisons at critical capacity, applies the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber (HCIZ) formula (nonstandard in this context), and integrates over unitary/orthogonal compact manifolds via the Weyl integration formula and Haar measure.
AI.result: Derives the asymptotic ratio between storage capacities for real versus complex pre-activations.
AI.conclusion: The HCIZ-based approach provides a more robust approximation for the asymptotic ratio, and the methodology is applicable due to integration over compact manifolds.

title: Physics-Informed Video Generation via Mixture-of-Experts Latent Alignment
summary: Large-scale video generation models have made remarkable progress in semantic consistency and visual quality, producing videos that are increasingly coherent and visually convincing. Nevertheless, the dynamics induced by pixel-level fitting do not naturally accommodate the regularities that govern real-world motion and interaction, resulting in persistent shortcomings in physical plausibility. To address this limitation, we propose \textbf{PILA} (Physics-Informed Latent Alignment), a framework that injects physics-structured latent guidance into the frozen flow-matching dynamics of pretrained video models. Specifically, PILA first employs anchored field estimation to map frozen-generator latents into an operational physical attribute bank organized by field-proxy slots, using observable motion as a kinematic anchor for constructing less directly observed proxies. To handle the heterogeneity of real-world dynamics, PILA adopts a mixture-of-experts design over physical categories. Label-prior masked expert routing selects category-specific operator experts, whose refinements are regularized by operational residuals abstracted from physical relations. Finally, the refined proxies are fused into the physical attribute bank and decoded into a correction to the flow-matching vector field, injecting physics-aware guidance while preserving the visual prior of the pretrained backbone. With staged adapter training on Wan 2.1-1.3B and direct transfer of the learned adapter to Wan 2.2-14B, PILA achieves state-of-the-art results on VBench-2.0, VideoPhy-2, and PhyGenBench in both visual quality and benchmark-measured physical plausibility.

AI.tldr: PILA injects physics-structured latent guidance into frozen video generation models via anchored field estimation, mixture-of-experts over physical categories, and operational residual regularization, achieving state-of-the-art visual quality and physical plausibility.
AI.motivation: Large-scale video generation models produce visually coherent videos but suffer from persistent physical plausibility issues because pixel-level fitting does not naturally accommodate real-world motion and interaction regularities.
AI.method: PILA uses (1) anchored field estimation to map latents into a physical attribute bank via kinematic anchors and field-proxy slots, (2) mixture-of-experts with label-prior masked expert routing over physical categories, (3) operational residual regularization from physical relations, and (4) decoding refined proxies into corrections injected into the frozen flow-matching vector field.
AI.result: PILA achieves state-of-the-art results on VBench-2.0, VideoPhy-2, and PhyGenBench in both visual quality and physical plausibility, with staged adapter training on Wan 2.1-1.3B and direct transfer to Wan 2.2-14B.
AI.conclusion: Injecting physics-structured latent guidance into frozen pretrained video models effectively improves physical plausibility while preserving visual priors, and the learned adapter transfers across model scales.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network Modeling of Biodegradable Contaminant Transport through GCL/SL Composite Liners
summary: This study develops a two-domain physics-informed neural network framework for contaminant transport through a GCL/SL composite liner system, in which the thin GCL layer is treated using a steady-state advection-dispersion-biodegradation formulation and the underlying soil liner is modeled as a transient transport domain. Two formulations are evaluated against analytical and finite-element reference solutions under different leachate-head conditions: a standard PINN with soft constraint enforcement (Std-PINN) and a hard-constrained PINN (H-PINN), in which selected boundary and initial conditions are embedded directly into the trial solutions. The Std-PINN captures the overall breakthrough behavior but shows larger errors during the early transport stage, particularly under higher leachate heads where advective transport becomes more pronounced. The H-PINN reduces the optimization burden associated with penalty-based constraint enforcement and provides more accurate and stable concentration predictions, lowering the MAE from approximately 0.058-0.067 for the Std-PINN to about 0.011-0.023 for the H-PINN, while reducing the MRE from approximately 9.10%-19.16% to about 2.08%-3.14%. Parametric analyses confirm that the H-PINN with the tanh activation function and an optimized network structure provides the best predictive accuracy. The H-PINN is further extended to inverse modeling for identifying the SL degradation half-life from limited concentration observations, showing reliable convergence toward prescribed values and acceptable robustness under low-to-moderate observation noise.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a two-domain physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework for modeling contaminant transport through a GCL/SL composite liner system, comparing standard soft-constrained PINN (Std-PINN) against a hard-constrained PINN (H-PINN) that embeds boundary/initial conditions directly. H-PINN significantly improves accuracy (MAE reduced from ~0.058-0.067 to ~0.011-0.023; MRE from ~9.10%-19.16% to ~2.08%-3.14%) and is extended to inverse modeling for identifying degradation half-life from limited observations.
AI.motivation: To develop an accurate and efficient neural network framework for modeling contaminant transport through composite liner systems (GCL/SL) that overcomes limitations of standard PINNs, particularly for advection-dominated early-stage transport under varying leachate-head conditions.
AI.method: A two-domain PINN framework where the thin GCL layer uses steady-state advection-dispersion-biodegradation formulation and the soil liner uses transient transport modeling. Two variants tested: Std-PINN (soft constraint enforcement via penalty terms) and H-PINN (hard-constrained with boundary/initial conditions embedded into trial solutions), with tanh activation and optimized network structure.
AI.result: H-PINN reduces MAE from 0.058-0.067 to 0.011-0.023 and MRE from 9.10%-19.16% to 2.08%-3.14% compared to Std-PINN. H-PINN also successfully extends to inverse modeling for SL degradation half-life identification with reliable convergence and robustness under low-to-moderate observation noise.
AI.conclusion: The hard-constrained PINN (H-PINN) significantly outperforms the standard soft-constrained approach for contaminant transport modeling in composite liner systems, providing superior accuracy and stability, and shows promise for practical inverse modeling applications.

title: Do Foundation Models See Biology? Evaluating Attention Coherence with Spatial Transcriptomics in Glioblastoma
summary: Whether attention maps from pathology foundation models capture genuine biology remains unknown, yet this question is critical for clinical trust and regulatory approval. We propose a spatial transcriptomics-based framework for orthogonal, hypothesis-free evaluation of attention and apply it to five pathology foundation models (CONCH v1.5, UNI v2, Virchow2, GigaPath, H-Optimus-1) and a ResNet50 baseline. Using attention-based multiple instance learning, we train single-task and multi-task models to predict five molecular alterations in glioblastoma on the CPTAC cohort, validate on an independent TCGA cohort, and evaluate biological coherence of attention maps against 87 transcriptional signatures using co-registered Visium spatial transcriptomics data from 18 samples. Internally, no single encoder dominates across all tasks, and external validation inverts internal performance rankings. Attention maps show a five-fold enrichment gradient from pathways (Cohen's d=0.329) to individual genes (d=0.055), indicating that attention captures emergent multi-gene transcriptional programs rather than individual molecular events. Spatially smooth attention maps do not imply biological coherence, and different encoders attend to distinct biological compartments. Our framework provides objective, quantitative assessment of what foundation models learn from histopathology, moving the field beyond qualitative saliency map review.

AI.tldr: Pathology foundation models' attention maps capture multi-gene transcriptional programs (coherent pathways) better than individual genes, but no single model dominates across tasks; external validation reverses internal rankings, and spatial smoothness does not guarantee biological coherence.
AI.motivation: To determine whether attention maps from pathology foundation models reflect genuine biology—a critical but unverified assumption for clinical trust and regulatory approval—by moving beyond qualitative review to objective, quantitative assessment.
AI.method: A spatial transcriptomics-based framework (Visium, 18 samples) using attention-based multiple instance learning to train single-task & multi-task models predicting 5 molecular alterations in glioblastoma (CPTAC→TCGA validation), evaluating attention maps against 87 transcriptional signatures.
AI.result: No single encoder dominates internally; external validation inverts rankings. Attention shows a five-fold enrichment gradient from pathways (d=0.329) to individual genes (d=0.055). Spatially smooth attention ≠ biological coherence; different encoders attend to distinct biological compartments.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables objective, quantitative evaluation of foundation model biology, revealing that attention captures emergent multi-gene programs, not individual genes, and that model rankings are unstable across validation settings.

title: DPDL: Towards Differential Privacy Preservation in Decentralized Stochastic Learning on Non-IID Data
summary: In the paradigm of decentralized learning, a group of agents collaborate to train a global model using distributed datasets without a central server. Although the power of collaboration has been verified by many state-of-the-art studies, it entails extensive gradient information exchanging among the agents and thus induces high risk of privacy leakage for the individual agents. Moreover, in real-world applications, the training data are usually non-identically and independently distributed across the agents, inducing more challenges to enable privacy-preserved decentralized learning. To address these issues, we propose a privacy-preserved decentralized learning algorithm with non-IID data, DPDL, which leverages the notion of Differential Privacy (DP) in cross-gradient aggregation through a similarity-based calibration technique. Specifically, in each round, each agent perturbs the cross-gradients (i.e., the derivatives of its neighbors' local model in its private local data) by Gaussian noise mechanism before sharing them with its neighbors; it then adopt cosine similarity to calibrate the received perturbed cross-gradients such that the aggregation of the calibrated cross-gradients can be utilized to effectively update local model in a momentum-like manner. Our rigorous theoretical analysis not only reveals the minimum noise level required to achieve a specific level of privacy preservation, but also illustrates that our algorithm still achieves a linear speedup in training with non-IID data. We finally conduct extensive experiments on real-world dataset to validate the effectiveness of our algorithm in defending privacy attacks and in training accurate models.

AI.tldr: DPDL is a privacy-preserved decentralized learning algorithm that uses Differential Privacy with cosine similarity-based calibration on cross-gradients to handle non-IID data while achieving linear training speedup.
AI.motivation: Decentralized learning faces two key challenges: (1) high privacy leakage risk due to extensive gradient exchange among agents, and (2) non-IID distributed training data across agents, which complicates privacy-preserving decentralized learning.
AI.method: DPDL perturbs cross-gradients (derivatives of neighbors' local models on private data) with Gaussian noise before sharing, then applies cosine similarity calibration to the received perturbed cross-gradients, and aggregates them for momentum-like local model updates.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis reveals the minimum noise level for a desired privacy level and shows linear speedup in training with non-IID data. Experiments on real-world datasets validate effectiveness against privacy attacks and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: DPDL effectively balances privacy preservation and training performance in decentralized learning under non-IID data distributions, both theoretically and empirically.

title: Coarse-to-fine Hierarchical Architecture with Sequential Mamba for Brain Reconstruction
summary: Understanding the relationship between deep visual representations and the human visual system is a fundamental challenge in computational neuroscience. While modern vision models achieve strong performance in image recognition, their correspondence with the hierarchical organization of the human visual cortex remains an open question. In this study, we propose CHASMBrain, a novel hierarchical two-stage framework for image-to-fMRI encoding. Our architecture leverages a dual-stream Mamba design to explicitly separate and process global semantic tokens and local spatial patches, motivated by the functional organization of the visual cortex. A coarse-to-fine strategy is employed: Stage 1 predicts denoised ROI-level activations, while Stage 2 refines these coarse responses into full voxel-level predictions using a Mamba-VAE. Experiments on the Natural Scenes Dataset (NSD) demonstrate that our method achieves a Pearson correlation of 0.429 and an MSE of 0.261, outperforming all evaluated baselines including ridge regression and DINOv2 linear probes. Beyond predictive performance, causal branch-ablation experiments reveal an asymmetric specialization: the patch stream is specifically locked to early visual cortex (retinotopic regions), while the CLS stream contributes broader semantic context to higher-order areas -- a correspondence that holds causally, not merely correlationally. Cross-subject transfer experiments further show that the learned backbone generalizes across individuals with minimal per-subject adaptation, suggesting the model captures a shared, subject-agnostic visual representation.

AI.tldr: CHASMBrain is a dual-stream Mamba framework for image-to-fMRI encoding that uses a coarse-to-fine strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results on NSD and revealing asymmetric specialization between patch and CLS streams corresponding to early vs. higher-order visual cortex.
AI.motivation: To understand the correspondence between deep visual representations and the hierarchical organization of the human visual cortex, and to bridge the gap between vision models and computational neuroscience.
AI.method: A hierarchical two-stage framework: Stage 1 predicts denoised ROI-level activations via a dual-stream Mamba (global semantic tokens + local spatial patches); Stage 2 refines coarse responses into full voxel-level predictions using a Mamba-VAE, with a coarse-to-fine strategy.
AI.result: Achieves Pearson correlation of 0.429 and MSE of 0.261 on NSD, outperforming ridge regression and DINOv2 linear probes. Causal ablation reveals patch stream locks to early visual cortex, CLS stream to higher-order areas. Cross-subject transfer shows generalizable representations.
AI.conclusion: The dual-stream Mamba architecture causally corresponds to the functional organization of the visual cortex, and the learned representations are subject-agnostic, providing a principled framework for brain encoding.

title: TANDEM: Bi-Level Data Mixture Optimization with Twin Networks
summary: The capabilities of large language models (LLMs) significantly depend on training data drawn from various domains. Optimizing domain-specific mixture ratios can be modeled as a bi-level optimization problem, which we simplify into a single-level penalized form and solve with twin networks: a proxy model trained on primary data and a dynamically updated reference model trained with additional data. Our proposed method, Twin Networks for bi-level DatA mixturE optiMization (TANDEM), measures the data efficacy through the difference between the twin models and up-weights domains that benefit more from the additional data. TANDEM provides theoretical guarantees and wider applicability, compared to prior approaches. Furthermore, our bi-level perspective suggests new settings to study domain reweighting such as data-restricted scenarios and supervised fine-tuning, where optimized mixture ratios significantly improve the performance. Extensive experiments validate TANDEM's effectiveness in all scenarios.

AI.tldr: TANDEM uses twin networks (proxy + reference model) to optimize domain mixture ratios in LLM training data via a bi-level optimization framework, outperforming prior methods across multiple settings.
AI.motivation: LLM performance heavily depends on domain-specific data mixtures, but optimizing these ratios is challenging. The authors aim to solve this as a bi-level optimization problem to automatically determine optimal domain weights.
AI.method: TANDEM simplifies bi-level optimization into single-level penalized form using twin networks: a proxy model (trained on primary data) and a dynamically updated reference model (trained with additional data). Domain efficacy is measured by the performance gap between the two models, up-weighting domains benefiting more from extra data.
AI.result: TANDEM provides theoretical guarantees, wider applicability than prior work, and significantly improves performance in data-restricted scenarios and supervised fine-tuning settings.
AI.conclusion: The bi-level perspective enables effective domain reweighting for LLM training, and extensive experiments validate TANDEM's effectiveness across all tested scenarios.

title: NextMotionQA: Benchmarking and Judging Human Motion Understanding with Vision-Language Models
summary: Reliable evaluation of human motion understanding is fundamental to advancing embodied AI, robotics, and animation. However, existing benchmarks suffer from coarse semantic granularity, undifferentiated difficulty, limited annotation quality, and pervasive answer ambiguity, leaving them unable to diagnose where current models fail. To bridge this gap, we introduce NextMotionQA, a comprehensive benchmark that leverages vision-language models (VLMs) for semi-automated, expert-verified dataset. NextMotionQA features three complementary tasks: multiple-choice question answering, video captioning, and fine-grained error correction. Each task is systematically structured across three core semantic axes and stratified into three task complexity levels. Our extensive evaluation of twelve representative VLMs uncovers critical capability gaps and weakness that remain invisible under conventional, single-task evaluations. In a complementary direction, recent work has begun using VLMs as judges for text-to-motion evaluation; we ask whether they show the same degradation under harder tasks. We find that VLMs align strongly with expert ratings on coarse criteria (Cohen's κ=0.70) but break down on fine-grained, part-level judgment (κ=0.10), validating the paradigm in its strong regime while clarifying its limits.

AI.tldr: NextMotionQA is a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating human motion understanding in VLMs, featuring three tasks across multiple semantic axes and complexity levels. It reveals critical capability gaps invisible in single-task evaluations, and shows that VLMs align well with experts on coarse criteria (κ=0.70) but fail on fine-grained judgment (κ=0.10).
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for human motion understanding suffer from coarse semantic granularity, undifferentiated difficulty, limited annotation quality, and pervasive answer ambiguity, making them unable to diagnose where current models fail.
AI.method: The authors introduce NextMotionQA, a benchmark built via semi-automated VLM-based pipeline with expert verification. It includes three tasks (multiple-choice QA, video captioning, fine-grained error correction) structured across three semantic axes and three complexity levels. Twelve VLMs were evaluated, and VLM-as-judge alignment with expert ratings was analyzed.
AI.result: VLMs show strong alignment with expert ratings on coarse criteria (Cohen's κ=0.70) but break down on fine-grained, part-level judgment (κ=0.10). The benchmark reveals critical capability gaps invisible under conventional single-task evaluations.
AI.conclusion: NextMotionQA provides a more diagnostic evaluation framework for human motion understanding, validating VLM-as-judge paradigm in its strong regime while clarifying its limits on fine-grained tasks.

title: Low-Rank Decay for Grokking in Scale-Invariant Transformers: A Spectral-Geometric View
summary: Modern Transformer architectures frequently employ normalization mechanisms such as RMSNorm and Query-Key Normalization, making parts of the model approximately scale-invariant with respect to weight magnitudes. In this regime, standard Frobenius-norm weight decay acts purely along the radial direction of the weight space and cannot directly simplify the function represented by the normalized layer. We study grokking in small algorithmic tasks through this lens and propose \emph{Low-Rank Decay} (LRD), a nuclear-norm-like spectral regularizer whose subgradient -- the polar factor $UV^\top$ -- retains a tangential component even in the scale-invariant setting. This distinction has a concrete dynamical consequence: after the model memorizes the training set and task gradients vanish, L2 decay can no longer reshape the weight spectrum, whereas LRD continues to compress singular values in an $\ell_1$-like fashion. On modular arithmetic tasks, we find that LRD induces rapid effective-rank collapse in Query/Key matrices and expands the data-fraction boundary at which delayed generalization (grokking) occurs. We further provide a spectral-geometric interpretation through the ``needle-to-fan'' expansion of the nuclear-norm subdifferential near low-rank strata.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Low-Rank Decay (LRD), a nuclear-norm-like spectral regularizer that maintains a tangential component in scale-invariant settings, unlike standard Frobenius-norm weight decay. On modular arithmetic tasks, LRD induces rapid effective-rank collapse in Query/Key matrices and expands the grokking boundary, with a spectral-geometric interpretation via the nuclear-norm subdifferential near low-rank strata.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation of standard Frobenius-norm weight decay, which acts purely radially in scale-invariant Transformer architectures (using RMSNorm, QK-Norm) and cannot simplify the function in normalized layers after memorization, the authors aim to develop a regularizer that continues to reshape weight spectra even when task gradients vanish.
AI.method: The authors propose Low-Rank Decay (LRD), a regularizer based on the nuclear norm. Its subgradient is the polar factor UV^T, which retains a tangential component in scale-invariant settings. This is applied to Transformer training on modular arithmetic tasks, studying grokking behavior and comparing against standard L2 weight decay.
AI.result: LRD induces rapid effective-rank collapse in Query/Key matrices and expands the data-fraction boundary where grokking occurs, outperforming L2 decay in the scale-invariant regime after task gradients vanish.
AI.conclusion: LRD provides a more effective spectral regularizer than L2 decay for scale-invariant Transformer layers, with a clear geometric interpretation linking nuclear-norm subdifferential near low-rank strata to the observed dynamical effects on grokking and generalization.

title: An Ensembled Latent Factor Model via Differential Evolution and Gradient Descent Optimization
summary: High-dimensional and incomplete (HDI) data are prevalent in many real-world big data scenarios. Latent factor models serve as a common representation learning approach, capable of uncovering informative latent factors from such data. Nevertheless, most existing latent factor models rely solely on gradient descent for optimization, which may lead to insufficient and biased representations, particularly when dealing with heterogeneous HDI data. Thus, this study proposes an Ensembled Latent Factor Model via Differential Evolution and Gradient Descent Optimization (ELFM-DEGDO) with two-fold designed: 1) two diverse latent factor models are independently modeled via differential evolution and gradient descent optimization, respectively, and 2) the two diverse latent factor models are combined via a customized self-adaptive weighting mechanism to effectively fuse their strengths. By leveraging the complementary advantages of both optimization paradigms, ELFM-DEGDO is able to produce more comprehensive and less biased representations for HDI data. Three HDI datasets are tested to show that ELFM-DEGDO consistently performs better than related several latent factor models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ELFM-DEGDO, an ensembled latent factor model that combines differential evolution and gradient descent optimization via a self-adaptive weighting mechanism to produce more comprehensive and less biased representations for high-dimensional incomplete data.
AI.motivation: Existing latent factor models rely solely on gradient descent optimization, leading to insufficient and biased representations when handling heterogeneous high-dimensional incomplete (HDI) data.
AI.method: Two diverse latent factor models are independently modeled using differential evolution and gradient descent optimization, then combined via a customized self-adaptive weighting mechanism to fuse their complementary strengths.
AI.result: Tested on three HDI datasets, ELFM-DEGDO consistently outperforms several related latent factor models.
AI.conclusion: ELFM-DEGDO effectively leverages complementary advantages of both optimization paradigms, producing more comprehensive and less biased representations for HDI data.

title: A Pathology Foundation Model for Gastric Cancer with Real-World Validation
summary: Gastric cancer remains a major cause of cancer mortality, yet its histological and molecular heterogeneity complicates diagnosis and risk stratification. General-purpose pathology foundation models (PFMs) often plateau on fine-grained endpoints central to gastric cancer care, and few have undergone rigorous prospective validation or clinical reader studies. We present GRACE, a Gastric-specific foundation model for Real-world Assessment and Clinical dEcision support. GRACE was developed from multicenter gastric pathology datasets totaling 48,364 primarily HE-stained whole-slide images from 37,493 patients. When evaluated on 28 clinically relevant tasks, GRACE consistently outperformed representative pancancer PFMs, achieving a macro-AUC of 0.9188, with strong performance for precancerous lesion diagnosis (macro-AUC 0.9322), tumor histopathological assessment (macro-AUC 0.9119), molecular profiling (macro-AUC 0.8682), and prognostic prediction. Beyond benchmarking, GRACE's translational value was substantiated through a rigorous evidence chain. Under safety-gated criteria requiring 100% NPV for rule-out and 100% PPV for rule-in, GRACE streamlined review for up to 69.6% of malignancy-diagnosis cases and triaged 46.8% of MMR-IHC follow-up requests. This translational feasibility was further strengthened by a randomized crossover reader study of pathologist-AI collaboration. With GRACE assistance, diagnostic accuracy improved from 82.0% to 89.9%, yielding nearly twofold higher adjusted odds of a correct diagnosis (OR 1.987) alongside concurrent gains in sensitivity and specificity. AI assistance also reduced diagnostic time by 14.9%, elevated diagnostic confidence by 9.0%, and markedly improved inter-rater agreement. When calibrated to maintain non-inferior performance to senior pathologists, the AI-assisted workflow could triage 60.7% of atrophy and 82.7% of intestinal metaplasia cases.

AI.tldr: GRACE is a gastric-cancer-specific pathology foundation model trained on 48,364 WSI from 37,493 patients, which outperforms pancancer PFMs across 28 clinical tasks (macro-AUC 0.9188), streamlines up to 69.6% of malignancy review cases, and improves pathologist diagnostic accuracy from 82.0% to 89.9% in a randomized crossover reader study.
AI.motivation: Gastric cancer has high mortality and significant histological/molecular heterogeneity; existing general-purpose pathology foundation models plateau on fine-grained gastric-specific tasks and lack prospective clinical validation or reader studies.
AI.method: Developed GRACE using 48,364 HE-stained WSIs from 37,493 patients across multicenter datasets. Evaluated on 28 tasks spanning precancerous lesion diagnosis, tumor histopathology, molecular profiling, and prognosis. Validated via safety-gated triage criteria, a randomized crossover reader study comparing pathologist performance with vs. without AI assistance, and non-inferiority calibration against senior pathologists.
AI.result: GRACE achieved macro-AUC 0.9188 overall (precancer: 0.9322, histopathology: 0.9119, molecular: 0.8682). It streamlined review for 69.6% of malignancy cases and triaged 46.8% of MMR-IHC requests. In the reader study, AI assistance boosted accuracy from 82.0% to 89.9% (OR 1.987), improved sensitivity/specificity, reduced diagnostic time by 14.9%, increased confidence by 9.0%, and enhanced inter-rater agreement. Under non-inferiority calibration, AI triaged 60.7% of atrophy and 82.7% of intestinal metaplasia cases.
AI.conclusion: GRACE demonstrates that a disease-specific foundation model can significantly outperform general-purpose PFMs on gastric cancer endpoints, and its translational value is validated through rigorous clinical evidence including safety-gated triage and a randomized reader study, paving the way for real-world AI-assisted gastric cancer diagnosis and workflow optimization.

title: (Mis)generalization of Helpful-only Fine-tuning
summary: Helpful-only models, that is, models that are trained to always follow user intent, are valuable for dangerous capability evaluations and other areas of AI R&D where refusals would be an obstacle. Little is known about the generalization properties of helpful-only training: helpful-only models refuse less than their harmless counterparts, but previous work has not studied other dimensions of their alignment. We study the shortcomings of existing helpful-only models. We find that some show emergent misalignment, others have residual refusal behaviors, and most show poor steerability, sycophancy, and incoherent character. We show that simple anti-refusal training can cause many of these issues. None of these problems are necessary consequences of helpful-only training, though: we show that synthetic document fine-tuning and adding character-related questions to SFT and RL can mitigate them.

AI.tldr: The paper studies helpful-only models that always follow user intent, finding they show emergent misalignment, residual refusal, poor steerability, sycophancy, and incoherent character. Anti-refusal training causes many issues, but synthetic document fine-tuning and character-related training can mitigate them.
AI.motivation: Understanding the generalization properties and shortcomings of helpful-only training is important because such models are valuable for dangerous capability evaluations and AI R&D where refusals would be an obstacle, yet little is known about their alignment beyond refusal reduction.
AI.method: The paper evaluates existing helpful-only models across multiple alignment dimensions, identifies issues caused by anti-refusal training, and proposes synthetic document fine-tuning plus adding character-related questions to SFT and RL as mitigation strategies.
AI.result: Helpful-only models exhibit emergent misalignment, residual refusal, poor steerability, sycophancy, and incoherent character. Anti-refusal training causes many of these issues. Synthetic document fine-tuning and character-related training mitigate the problems.
AI.conclusion: The shortcomings observed in helpful-only models are not necessary consequences of helpful-only training; they can be mitigated through targeted interventions like synthetic document fine-tuning and character-focused training.

title: Crafting Your Evolving Dreams: Concept-Incremental Versatile Customization
comment: Accepted to Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence (TPAMI)
summary: Custom diffusion models (CDMs) have garnered significant interest owing to their remarkable capacity for generating personalized concepts. However, the majority of CDMs unrealistically presume that the user's collection of personalized concepts is static and incapable of incremental growth over time. Furthermore, they exhibit significant catastrophic forgetting and concept neglect of previously learned concepts when incrementally learning a sequence of new ones. To resolve the above challenges, we develop a novel Continually Customizable Diffusion Model (CCDM), enabling users to perform concept-incremental versatile customization. Specifically, we design an attribute-decoupled LoRA (AD-LoRA) module and a relevance-guided AD-LoRA aggregation strategy to mitigate catastrophic forgetting. They can preserve concept-specific attributes of each task and leverage beneficial inter-task correlations to enhance the continual learning of new customization tasks. Additionally, to address the challenge of concept neglect, we propose a controllable regional context synthesis strategy that performs multi-concept composition in alignment with user-provided conditions. This strategy enhances the overall consistency in multi-concept synthesis by guaranteeing semantic independence between user-defined regions and their smooth boundary transitions. Experiments show our CCDM exhibits significant improvements over baseline methods.

AI.tldr: A Continually Customizable Diffusion Model (CCDM) is proposed to enable incremental learning of new personalized concepts without catastrophic forgetting or concept neglect, using attribute-decoupled LoRA modules and a controllable regional context synthesis strategy.
AI.motivation: Existing custom diffusion models assume user's concept collection is static and cannot handle incremental concept learning, suffering from catastrophic forgetting and concept neglect during sequential learning.
AI.method: Proposes (1) attribute-decoupled LoRA (AD-LoRA) module with relevance-guided aggregation to preserve concept-specific attributes and leverage inter-task correlations, and (2) controllable regional context synthesis strategy for multi-concept composition with semantic independence and smooth boundary transitions.
AI.result: CCDM shows significant improvements over baseline methods in continual customization tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively solves catastrophic forgetting and concept neglect in continual concept learning for diffusion models.

title: Loss-Conditional PINNs for Parametric PDE Families
summary:
Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) approximate solutions of ODEs and PDEs by minimising a weighted combination of residual, boundary, initial, and data losses. Their performance is often dominated by the choice of loss weights: a poor weighting can drive training to a degenerate solution in which one physical constraint is satisfied while another is ignored. Existing methods select or adapt a single good set of weights. We take a different view: instead of tuning one weight vector, we explore the entire weight space during training.
  We introduce LC-PINN, which adapts the loss-conditional training of Dosovitskiy and Djolonga (2020) to the PDE-residual setting: the conditioning vector (either the loss weights or a scalar physical coefficient) is treated as a network input and sampled from a simple prior at every optimisation step. This turns PINN training into learning a continuous family of solutions indexed by that vector, with no solver-generated paired data. LC-PINN thus lies between classical PINNs and operator learning: it stays fully physics-informed but amortises training over a parametric family. Our contribution is not the loss-conditional construction itself, but its extension to PINNs, the unification of the loss-weight and parametric-coefficient regimes under one architecture (concatenation for loss weights, FiLM for coefficients), and a fixed-quadrature L-BFGS finishing protocol that makes the parametric-coefficient regime trainable.
  We give a lambda-invariance result for the conditional optimum and study LC-PINN on parametric Helmholtz, Schrodinger, viscous Burgers, and Buckley-Leverett equations. A single LC-PINN matches or improves retrained per-weight PINN baselines while parameterising the full family in one model, at a total cost that amortises favourably against per-instance retraining.

AI.tldr: LC-PINN adapts loss-conditional training to PINNs by treating loss weights or physical coefficients as network inputs, enabling a single model to learn a continuous family of PDE solutions without per-instance retraining.
AI.motivation: PINN performance is highly sensitive to loss weight selection; poor weights lead to degenerate solutions where some constraints are ignored. Existing methods only tune a single weight set, limiting efficiency and flexibility.
AI.method: LC-PINN treats the conditioning vector (loss weights or scalar physical coefficient) as a network input sampled from a simple prior at each optimization step. It uses concatenation for loss weights and FiLM for coefficients, with a fixed-quadrature L-BFGS finishing protocol.
AI.result: A single LC-PINN matches or improves retrained per-weight PINN baselines on parametric Helmholtz, Schrödinger, viscous Burgers, and Buckley-Leverett equations, while parameterizing the full family in one model with favorable amortized cost.
AI.conclusion: LC-PINN lies between classical PINNs and operator learning: it stays fully physics-informed but amortizes training over a parametric family, offering a practical alternative to per-instance retraining.

title: Fast Cubical Persistent Homology on 2D and 3D Images via Union-Find, Pruning, and Lookup Tables
summary: We present Flash Cubical, a highly efficient computation of cubical persistence on a V-filtration for 2D and 3D images over $\mathbb{F}_2$. The implementation is built around three core ideas. First, cubical complexes satisfy properties that allow for the computation of persistence of the highest dimension via union-find and duality. Second, pruning of certain edges allows for a fast and efficient implementation of union-find. Third, the use of a lookup table, which exploits the regularity of cubical complexes to pre-compute local information. This avoids the need to compute local information at run time. To the best of our knowledge, this is the most efficient implementation of cubical persistence with a V-filtration, both in terms of time and memory costs. Although the paper focuses on persistence for V-filtration cubical complexes, the underlying ideas generalise naturally to T-filtrations on cubical complexes and suggest promising directions for other complexes.

AI.tldr: Flash Cubical is a highly efficient algorithm for computing cubical persistence on V-filtrations in 2D/3D images over 𝔽₂, leveraging union-find with duality, edge pruning, and pre-computed lookup tables to achieve state-of-the-art performance in time and memory.
AI.motivation: To accelerate cubical persistence computation on V-filtrations for 2D and 3D images, addressing inefficiencies in existing implementations.
AI.method: Three core techniques: (1) using union-find and duality for highest-dimensional persistence; (2) edge pruning to speed up union-find; (3) a lookup table to pre-compute local information, leveraging cubical complex regularity.
AI.result: The most efficient implementation of cubical persistence with a V-filtration, outperforming prior work in both time and memory costs.
AI.conclusion: The ideas naturally extend to T-filtrations on cubical complexes and suggest promising directions for other complex types.

title: The price of multi-group transductive learning
summary: We show every multi-group learner in the transductive setting may incur a multiplicative penalty in its error rate on some group relative to the error rate achievable in the single-group setting, and the penalty can increasing linearly with the number of groups, up to roughly the square-root of the sample size. This stands in stark contrast to optimal multi-group learners in an analogous (group-realizable) statistical setting, where the penalty is always at most logarithmic in the sample size and independent of the number of groups.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals that transductive multi-group learners face a multiplicative error penalty that scales linearly with the number of groups (up to ~√sample size), unlike statistical settings where the penalty is at most logarithmic and independent of group count.
AI.motivation: To characterize the fundamental limits of multi-group learning in the transductive setting and highlight its surprising gap compared to the statistical setting.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis comparing transductive and statistical multi-group learning settings, with bounds on error penalties.
AI.result: Transductive multi-group learners face a multiplicative penalty that grows linearly with the number of groups (up to √n), unlike the logarithmic penalty in statistical settings.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental and significant separation between transductive and statistical multi-group learning in terms of error guarantees.

title: NoRA: Evaluating Grounded Reasonableness in Visual First-person Normative Action Reasoning
summary: LLMs and agentic systems are increasingly deployed in social environments, making normative competence critical for safe and appropriate behavior. However, existing approaches either assess normative judgment in text alone or reduce it to choosing among a fixed set of candidate actions. We argue both are insufficient. In practice, agents are never handed a menu of options; they must identify a reasonable action from scratch, grounded in visible facts and supported by inspectable reasons. We introduce NoRA, a visual first-person video benchmark that requires models to generate candidate next actions and justify each through an explicit fact-reason-action support graph. The benchmark comprises 1,420 annotated video clips, including HumanGold-190 and LLMSilver-1230 splits. Each instance is evaluated through action alignment, factual grounding, and support binding, aggregated into a single grounded reasonableness score. We benchmark 12 multimodal systems under direct, deliberate, and structured prompting regimes, finding that current VLMs frequently recover plausible actions and relevant scene facts, but consistently struggle to construct the full reasonable action space and bind selected actions to the correct local support. NoRA makes this gap measurable, shifting the evaluation question from whether a model can pick an action to whether it can justify an appropriate action for the right visible reasons.

AI.tldr: NoRA is a visual first-person video benchmark that tests whether multimodal models can generate and justify appropriate next actions using grounded fact-reason-action support graphs, revealing that current VLMs struggle to construct complete action spaces and bind actions to correct local support.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches assess normative judgment only in text or as fixed-choice selection, which is insufficient for real-world agents that must generate actions from scratch and justify them with visible facts and reasons.
AI.method: NoRA comprises 1,420 annotated video clips (HumanGold-190 and LLMSilver-1230 splits), requiring models to generate candidate next actions and justify each via a fact-reason-action support graph. Evaluation uses action alignment, factual grounding, and support binding, aggregated into a grounded reasonableness score under direct, deliberate, and structured prompting regimes.
AI.result: Current VLMs can recover plausible actions and relevant scene facts but consistently fail to construct the full reasonable action space and bind selected actions to correct local support. NoRA makes this gap measurable.
AI.conclusion: The evaluation focus should shift from whether a model can pick an action to whether it can justify an appropriate action for the right visible reasons.

title: LoopMoE: Unifying Iterative Computation with Mixture-of-Experts for Language Modeling
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and looped architectures scale models along two orthogonal axes, namely parameter capacity and effective depth. However, mainstream looped architectures rely on dense backbones that couple parameter count with per-token FLOPs, which makes it impossible to isolate the effect of iterative computation under matched budgets. To this end, we present LoopMoE, a looped MoE language model that integrates sparse routing with iterative weight-shared computation through two designs. The first is IterAdaLN, which resolves weight-sharing symmetry via a modulation signal jointly conditioned on the iteration index and the per-token hidden state. The second is a capacity-balancing strategy that recovers the attention-to-FFN active parameter ratio of well-tuned non-looped references. Together, these designs enable the first strictly controlled, head-to-head evaluation of a looped MoE against a Vanilla MoE under identical total parameters, per-token FLOPs, and active sublayer ratios. At the 3B scale, LoopMoE outperforms the Vanilla MoE on 8 of 9 downstream benchmarks with an average improvement exceeding 1 point. At the 9B scale, LoopMoE continues to outperform the matched Vanilla MoE, indicating that the architectural gain persists at larger scale. Our work establishes a controlled synthesis of sparsity and recurrence, and suggests a promising direction for looped language models.

AI.tldr: LoopMoE integrates sparse Mixture-of-Experts routing with iterative weight-shared computation (looped architecture), introducing IterAdaLN and a capacity-balancing strategy to enable fair comparison with Vanilla MoE models. At 3B and 9B scales, LoopMoE consistently outperforms matched Vanilla MoE baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing looped architectures use dense backbones that couple parameter count with per-token FLOPs, making it impossible to isolate the effect of iterative computation under matched budgets. The authors aim to enable a controlled, head-to-head evaluation of looped vs. non-looped models.
AI.method: LoopMoE combines sparse MoE routing with weight-shared looped computation via two key designs: (1) IterAdaLN, which breaks weight-sharing symmetry by modulating signals conditioned on iteration index and per-token hidden states; (2) a capacity-balancing strategy that restores the attention-to-FFN active parameter ratio to match well-tuned non-looped references.
AI.result: At 3B scale, LoopMoE outperforms Vanilla MoE on 8 of 9 downstream benchmarks with an average improvement >1 point. At 9B scale, LoopMoE continues to outperform the matched Vanilla MoE baseline.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a controlled synthesis of sparsity (MoE) and recurrence (looped architectures), demonstrating that looped language models can reliably outperform non-looped counterparts under identical parameter and FLOPs budgets, suggesting a promising direction for future looped language model design.

title: Dream.exe: Can Video Generation Models Dream Executable Robot Manipulation?
summary: Video generation models have made impressive strides in synthesizing visually compelling content, yet their outputs remain confined to the virtual domain. A natural question follows: how well do these models reflect the physical world when their generated videos leave the screen and enter reality? We propose robotic manipulation as a concrete, measurable window onto this question: if a model has truly internalized physical laws, the motion it depicts should translate into executable robot behavior. We introduce Dream.exe, an evaluation framework that operationalizes this criterion through a video-to-execution pipeline. Given a scene image and a task description, Dream.exe synthesizes a manipulation video, converts the generated motion into robot trajectories, and executes them in a physics simulator, yielding a grounding signal that purely visual metrics cannot offer. Using this pipeline, we evaluate 8 models spanning frontier closed-source generators, open-source generators, and robot-specific models. Our benchmark covers 101 manually curated manipulation tasks at three levels of physical complexity, measured across visual quality, trajectory fidelity, and execution success. Encouragingly, several models achieve measurable execution success, suggesting that generative priors learned from internet-scale data already encode meaningful physical knowledge. Yet visual quality proves a poor predictor of executability, exposing a dimension of model capability that standard visual evaluations do not capture. Dream.exe will be open-sourced at https://github.com/showlab/Dream.exe.

AI.tldr: Dream.exe evaluates video generation models by converting their outputs into executable robot trajectories in simulation, revealing that visual quality doesn't predict physical executability, though some models show meaningful physical priors.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between virtual video generation and physical world understanding, proposing robotic manipulation as a concrete benchmark for whether generative models encode physical laws.
AI.method: A video-to-execution pipeline that synthesizes manipulation videos, converts motion to robot trajectories, and executes them in a physics simulator across 101 curated tasks at three complexity levels.
AI.result: Several models achieve measurable execution success, but visual quality does not correlate with executability, revealing a gap in standard visual evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Generative models can encode meaningful physical knowledge from internet-scale data, but visual metrics alone are insufficient—grounded physical evaluation is needed.

title: RowNet: A Memory Transformer for Tabular Regression
comment: Retrieval-based neural architecture for real estate valuation. Related to TabR (arXiv:2307.14338) and retrieval-augmented tabular learning
summary:
Real estate valuation is a structured regression problem in which prices are governed by heterogeneous feature types, sparse regional effects, nonlinear interactions, and the practical logic of comparable properties. Standard multilayer perceptrons treat each row as an isolated vector and must learn locality, scale sensitivity, and categorical matching from supervision alone. Gradient-boosted decision trees provide strong tabular baselines, but their feature-centric splitting mechanism does not explicitly model the retrieval of similar historical observations.
  This paper presents RowNet, a retrieval-based neural architecture for real estate price-per-square-meter prediction. RowNet represents a query property through pairwise similarity features against a memory bank of labeled properties. A first retrieval layer estimates a coarse target from feature-only similarities. A second layer augments the memory comparison with target-consistency features and uses multiple learned attention heads to retrieve complementary comparable sets. A final mixture-of-experts module combines learned gating, residual correction, entropy regularization, and head-diversity regularization to produce the prediction.

AI.tldr: RowNet is a retrieval-based neural architecture for real estate price prediction that explicitly compares query properties against a memory bank of labeled properties using pairwise similarity features, multi-head attention, and a mixture-of-experts module.
AI.motivation: Standard MLPs and gradient-boosted trees fail to explicitly model the retrieval of similar historical observations (comparable properties), which is a core practical logic in real estate valuation.
AI.method: RowNet uses a two-layer retrieval architecture: (1) a first layer estimates coarse target from feature-only similarities; (2) a second layer augments with target-consistency features and multi-head attention for complementary comparable sets; (3) a mixture-of-experts module combines learned gating, residual correction, and regularization.
AI.result: The paper presents RowNet as a novel retrieval-based approach, though specific quantitative results are not included in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-based neural architectures that explicitly model comparable property logic can outperform standard tabular models for real estate price prediction.

title: OA-CutMix: Correcting the Label Bias of CutMix
summary: CutMix has become the de facto standard mixing augmentation, yet its label assignment rests on a flawed assumption: The area of the pasted patch faithfully reflects its semantic contribution to the mixed image. In practice, however, patches frequently land on background regions, assigning label credit to classes whose objects are not visible.
