The mean discrepancy of the CutMix label and the semantic object area is $21.5\%$. In $17\%$ of samples an image contributes zero visible object pixels yet receives nonzero label weight. We propose Object-Aware CutMix (OA-CutMix), which corrects this bias by replacing the area-based CutMix weight with one derived from precomputed segmentation masks, assigning labels in proportion to the visible object area each image contributes to the mix. The image mixing procedure is left entirely unchanged. We evaluate OA-CutMix against 10+ static and dynamic mixing methods across 4 architectures and 6 datasets. OA-CutMix consistently achieves the highest accuracy over all tasks, outperforming even dynamic mixing methods, but at a fraction of the training-time cost. Improvements are largest for small objects, where the label bias from CutMix is greatest. Thus, correcting the label is sufficient to match or exceed the performance of methods modifying the image mixing algorithm.

AI.tldr: CutMix's label assignment is flawed because it assumes patch area reflects semantic content, leading to label bias (21.5% mean discrepancy). OA-CutMix fixes this by using segmentation masks to assign labels based on visible object area, achieving state-of-the-art results across architectures and datasets without changing the mixing procedure.
AI.motivation: CutMix's area-based label assignment is fundamentally flawed — patches often land on backgrounds, misassigning credit to invisible classes (21.5% discrepancy; 17% samples have zero visible object pixels yet get nonzero labels).
AI.method: OA-CutMix replaces area-based label weights with weights derived from precomputed segmentation masks, assigning labels proportionally to visible object area each image contributes. The image mixing procedure itself is unchanged.
AI.result: OA-CutMix consistently achieves highest accuracy over 10+ mixing methods, 4 architectures, and 6 datasets. It outperforms dynamic mixing methods at lower training cost. Gains are largest for small objects where CutMix bias is greatest.
AI.conclusion: Simply correcting the label assignment is sufficient to match or exceed performance of methods that modify the image mixing algorithm itself.

title: On Out-of-sample Embedding in UMAP
summary: Neighbor embedding algorithms reveal correlations in high-dimensional data by constructing an equivalent graph representation in a lower-dimensional space. An increasingly popular algorithm is Uniform Manifold Learning and Projection (UMAP), which uses algebraic topology to map distances between the two spaces. While it works well on many types of data sets, UMAP has trouble adding out-of-sample points to a pre-existing mapping. In particular, UMAP often places new points on the periphery of the found clusters, rather than in their interiors with their correlated neighbors. Here, we overcome this ``repulsion effect'' by optimizing pairwise interactions within the original k-nearest-neighbor graph. Moreover, we show that parameterizing UMAP obtains better embeddings than non-parametric algorithms, particularly as the data gets more complex (e.g., medical images). We also show that the repulsion effect is naturally mitigated when a parameterized UMAP is employed to embed the data. We characterize different UMAP approaches using trustworthiness, nearest neighbor classifiers, and by analyzing attractive and repulsive forces in the embeddings.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies and overcomes UMAP's "repulsion effect" that places out-of-sample points at cluster peripheries, showing that parameterized UMAP optimizes pairwise interactions and achieves better embeddings than non-parametric approaches, especially for complex data like medical images.
AI.motivation: UMAP struggles with adding out-of-sample points to pre-existing mappings, often placing them on cluster peripheries rather than with their correlated neighbors (the "repulsion effect").
AI.method: The authors optimize pairwise interactions within the original k-nearest-neighbor graph to overcome the repulsion effect, and employ a parameterized UMAP approach for embedding data.
AI.result: Parameterized UMAP naturally mitigates the repulsion effect and yields better embeddings than non-parametric algorithms, especially for complex data like medical images.
AI.conclusion: Characterizing different UMAP approaches using trustworthiness, nearest neighbor classifiers, and analysis of attractive/repulsive forces confirms the superiority of parameterized UMAP for handling out-of-sample points.

title: 3D Temporal Analysis for Autism Spectrum Disorder Screening During Attention Tasks
summary: Accurate Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) screening for school-age children is crucial to identify cases that may have been missed earlier and to enable timely interventions supporting social, cognitive, and academic development. Current ASD screening relies on subjective assessments and 2D analysis methods that fail to capture spatial displacement patterns characteristic of ASD behaviors. In this study, a novel 3D temporal analysis framework is presented, built on top of DECA (Detailed Expression Capture and Animation), a 3D modeling framework, to extract comprehensive head pose parameters (including translational components $T_x, T_y, T_z$) and facial expressions independent of pose variations. LSTM and GRU-based temporal classifiers were trained on the extracted 3D features from video data collected from 39 participants (19 ASD, 20 TD) aged 7-12 years during Virtual Reality-Continuous Performance Test tasks. The GRU-based models demonstrated superior performance, with 3D head pose features achieving 83.9\% accuracy and 3D facial features reaching 81.4\% accuracy, outperforming 2D baseline approaches by 10.7\% and 7.5\%, respectively. Furthermore, multimodal fusion of 3D head pose and facial features with PCA-based dimensionality reduction achieved the highest accuracy of 84.6\%, outperforming unimodal approaches. This work establishes a foundation for objective, automated screening tools addressing current diagnostic limitations in ASD identification for school-age populations.

AI.tldr: A novel 3D temporal analysis framework using DECA, LSTM/GRU classifiers, and multimodal fusion achieves up to 84.6% accuracy for ASD screening in school-age children, outperforming 2D methods by 7.5-10.7%.
AI.motivation: Current ASD screening relies on subjective assessments and 2D methods that fail to capture spatial displacement patterns characteristic of ASD behaviors. Accurate screening for school-age children is crucial to identify missed cases and enable timely interventions.
AI.method: Used DECA (3D modeling framework) to extract 3D head pose parameters (Tx, Ty, Tz) and facial expressions. Trained LSTM and GRU-based temporal classifiers on extracted 3D features from video data of 39 participants (19 ASD, 20 TD) aged 7-12 during VR-CPT tasks. Applied PCA-based dimensionality reduction for multimodal fusion.
AI.result: GRU models achieved 83.9% accuracy (3D head pose) and 81.4% accuracy (3D facial features), outperforming 2D baselines by 10.7% and 7.5%. Multimodal fusion achieved highest accuracy of 84.6%, surpassing unimodal approaches.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a foundation for objective, automated ASD screening tools addressing current diagnostic limitations for school-age populations.

title: ParetoPilot: Zero-Surrogate Offline Multi-Objective Optimization via Infer-Perturb-Guide Diffusion
summary: Offline multi-objective optimization (Offline MOO) aims to discover novel Pareto-optimal designs based on static datasets without expensive environment interactions. While recent generative methods have achieved notable success, they predominantly rely on external surrogate models. This dependency introduces significant computational overhead, suffers from deceptive evaluations, and deviates from the prevailing paradigm of jointly training mainstream generative models with conditions. To address these bottlenecks, we propose ParetoPilot, a novel zero-surrogate diffusion framework for offline MOO. ParetoPilot fully leverages the conditional priors inherently embedded within pre-trained diffusion models. At its core, the framework introduces the Infer-Perturb-Guide (IPG) engine, which is seamlessly interleaved within the unconditional denoising steps of the reverse generation process. First, it implicitly infers the instantaneous objective direction by matching conditional and unconditional noise predictions. Next, it mathematically orthogonalizes a parallel gravity field for strict convergence and an edgeness-aware repulsive force for mutual diversity, creating a dynamically annealed perturbation vector. Finally, this perturbed target seamlessly steers the generation process via standard Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG). Extensive experiments across 51 tasks demonstrate that ParetoPilot outperforms 14 state-of-the-art surrogate-based and inverse generative baselines. By eliminating auxiliary proxy training, our approach preserves data privacy while achieving hypervolume improvement and robust Pareto front coverage.

AI.tldr: ParetoPilot is a zero-surrogate diffusion framework for offline multi-objective optimization that eliminates external surrogate models by using the Infer-Perturb-Guide engine to leverage pre-trained diffusion models' conditional priors, achieving state-of-the-art results across 51 tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing offline MOO methods rely on external surrogate models, causing computational overhead, deceptive evaluations, and deviation from mainstream generative model training paradigms. The authors aim to eliminate this dependency.
AI.method: Proposes ParetoPilot with an Infer-Perturb-Guide (IPG) engine integrated into unconditional denoising steps: (1) Infer objective direction by matching conditional/unconditional noise predictions; (2) Orthogonalize gravity field (convergence) and repulsive force (diversity) into a dynamic perturbation vector; (3) Guide generation via standard Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG).
AI.result: Outperforms 14 state-of-the-art baselines across 51 tasks, achieves hypervolume improvement and robust Pareto front coverage while preserving data privacy.
AI.conclusion: ParetoPilot successfully eliminates auxiliary proxy training for offline MOO, reducing computational cost and avoiding deceptive surrogate evaluations, while maintaining superior optimization performance.

title: MusaCoder: Native GPU Kernel Generation with Full-Stack Training on Moore Threads GPU
summary: Native GPU kernel generation turns high-level tensor programs into executable, efficient low-level code. Existing Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with this task, while execution-based reinforcement learning suffers from sparse rewards, reward hacking, and training instability. We present MusaCoder, a full-stack training framework for native GPU kernel generation on CUDA and MUSA backends. MusaCoder combines progressive kernel-oriented data synthesis, diversity-preserving rejection fine-tuning, and execution-feedback Reinforcement Learning (RL) through MooreEval, a distributed verifier and reward environment. To stabilize RL, MusaCoder introduces PrimeEcho for first-turn-anchored multi-turn rewards, Buffered Dynamic Retry for recovering signals from all-failed hard samples, and MirrorPop for off-policy sequence filtering. Experiments on KernelBench and a MUSA-ported variant show that MusaCoder outperforms strong open-source and proprietary baselines in both correctness and empirical speedup, with the 9B model matching or exceeding frontier closed-source models and the 27B model establishing a new state of the art. These results demonstrate not only the effectiveness of full-stack execution-feedback training for native kernel generation, but also the capability of Moore Threads GPUs to support the complete LLM post-training stack, providing a practical foundation for large-model training and optimization on emerging accelerators.

AI.tldr: MusaCoder is a full-stack training framework for native GPU kernel generation that combines progressive data synthesis, diversity-preserving rejection fine-tuning, and execution-feedback reinforcement learning with stability techniques (PrimeEcho, Buffered Dynamic Retry, MirrorPop), achieving state-of-the-art results on CUDA and MUSA backends.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs struggle with native GPU kernel generation, and execution-based reinforcement learning suffers from sparse rewards, reward hacking, and training instability.
AI.method: MusaCoder combines (1) progressive kernel-oriented data synthesis, (2) diversity-preserving rejection fine-tuning, (3) execution-feedback RL via MooreEval (distributed verifier/reward environment), with stability techniques: PrimeEcho (first-turn-anchored multi-turn rewards), Buffered Dynamic Retry (recovering signals from failed samples), and MirrorPop (off-policy sequence filtering).
AI.result: MusaCoder outperforms strong open-source and proprietary baselines in correctness and speedup on KernelBench and MUSA-ported variants. The 9B model matches frontier closed-source models; the 27B model establishes a new SOTA.
AI.conclusion: Effectiveness of full-stack execution-feedback training for native kernel generation is demonstrated, and Moore Threads GPUs can support the complete LLM post-training stack, providing a foundation for large-model training on emerging accelerators.

title: ChessMimic: Per-Rating Transformer Models for Human Move, Clock, and Outcome Prediction in Online Blitz Chess
summary: We present ChessMimic, a system of three small encoder-only transformers - for move, thinking-time, and outcome prediction - conditioned on the position, recent move history, player rating, and clock state. We fit a separate instance of each model per 100-Elo rating band, trading parameter efficiency for sharper per-skill calibration. On a held-out month-wide slice of Lichess Rated Blitz games ChessMimic's human move prediction accuracy outperforms Maia-2 in every Elo band. Compared to Maia-3, our 9M parameter model's accuracy sits between Maia-3-5M and Maia-3-23M without the additional complexity of Geometric Attention Bias. In addition to the move matching model, we also train a game outcome model that conditions not only on the position, but also player ratings, time control, and remaining clock times. The outcome model achieves an AUC of 0.78 out of sample, beating Maia-2 as well as logistic regressions based on material, ratings, and clock time. Finally, we train a clock model that predicts human thinking times. The clock model provides a usable but non-SOTA per-ply think-time signal under ALLIE-style filters (Pearson r = 0.41, Spearman rho = 0.50, MAE 4.10 s, against ALLIE's reported r = 0.70), with the residual gap concentrated in per-position bucket sharpness rather than bucket-marginal calibration. A public demo is at 1e4.ai and we release code, per-band weights, and the C++ data-filter pipeline code in GitHub.

AI.tldr: ChessMimic presents three small encoder-only transformers (move, thinking-time, outcome prediction) trained per Elo band on Lichess Blitz data. Move prediction outperforms Maia-2/3 (9M params), outcome prediction achieves AUC 0.78, and clock prediction reaches moderate correlation (r=0.41) with ALLIE-style filters.
AI.motivation: To build a computationally efficient human chess mimicry system with sharper per-skill calibration by training separate models per Elo band, improving on existing Maia architectures without complex biases like Geometric Attention Bias.
AI.method: Three small encoder-only transformers trained per 100-Elo rating band: (1) move prediction conditioned on position, move history, rating, clock; (2) outcome prediction adding ratings/time control; (3) thinking-time clock model with ALLIE-style filters. Total 9M parameters.
AI.result: Move prediction beats Maia-2 in all Elo bands and sits between Maia-3-5M and Maia-3-23M. Outcome model AUC=0.78 beats Maia-2 and logistic regressions. Clock model: Pearson r=0.41, Spearman rho=0.50, MAE=4.10s (vs ALLIE r=0.70).
AI.conclusion: ChessMimic demonstrates that per-band training of compact transformers achieves strong human-mimicry performance without architectural complexity, though thinking-time prediction remains less competitive with state-of-the-art.

title: IRIS-GAN: Staged Specialist Detection of Deepfake Faces
summary: We introduce IRIS-GAN, a specialist forensic detector for synthetic face images under cross-generator shift. Rather than addressing universal synthetic-image detection, we focus on faces generated by generative adversarial networks (GANs), which are state-of-the-art in deepfake content, and train the detector through staged exposure to increasingly demanding GAN families while retaining earlier generators. The final model reaches fake-detection rates above 99% across the GAN families considered and classifies an external real-face dataset with 98.9% accuracy. Grad-CAM analysis further reveals measurable generator-dependent spatial response patterns, which remain informative for a secondary heatmap-only classifier. Out-of-family tests on diffusion-generated faces confirm that IRIS-GAN is a specialist detector, with some capability to reach non-GAN deepfakes. These results establish staged training as an effective strategy for robust GAN-face forensics.

AI.tldr: IRIS-GAN is a specialist forensic detector for GAN-generated fake faces, achieving >99% detection rates across GAN families and 98.9% accuracy on real faces using staged training with increasingly complex GAN architectures.
AI.motivation: To develop a specialized detector for GAN-generated synthetic faces (rather than a universal detector), addressing the challenge of cross-generator domain shift where different GAN architectures produce different artifacts.
AI.method: Staged training strategy that exposes the detector to increasingly demanding GAN families in sequence while retaining earlier generators, plus Grad-CAM analysis for spatial response patterns and a secondary heatmap-only classifier.
AI.result: Fake-detection rates above 99% across all GAN families considered, 98.9% accuracy on external real-face dataset, and measurable generator-dependent spatial response patterns via Grad-CAM.
AI.conclusion: Staged training is an effective strategy for robust GAN-face forensics; IRIS-GAN works as a specialist detector with some capability to generalize to non-GAN deepfakes (diffusion-generated faces).

title: When Both Layers Learn: Training Dynamics of Representing Linear Models via ReLU Networks
comment: 47 pages, 8 figures, published at the 39th Annual Conference on Learning Theory (COLT), 2026
summary: In this paper, we study the gradient descent dynamics for jointly training both layers of a one-hidden-layer ReLU network to fit a linear target function. Concretely, we consider a realizable setting where inputs are drawn i.i.d. from a Gaussian distribution and labels follow a planted linear model. This stylized framework captures salient features of end-to-end training in inverse problems and certain auto-encoder models. Despite its apparent simplicity, the dynamics remain poorly understood, in part because the loss landscape contains multiple non-strict saddle points, making it unclear why gradient descent from random initialization reliably escapes bad stationary regions. We provide a detailed characterization of the optimization landscape and prove that gradient descent from a moderately small random initialization-simultaneously training both layers-converges to a global minimizer at a linear rate with order-wise optimal sample complexity. Our analysis tracks the trajectory through three phases: an alignment phase in which hidden weights progressively align with the planted direction while the output weights maintain the correct sign pattern; a growth phase in which the norms of both layers increase while preserving alignment; and a local refinement phase in which the aligned neurons rapidly converge to the planted direction, yielding fast local convergence. To rigorously show that GD avoids non-strict saddles, we develop trajectory-level control arguments for the end-to-end dynamics. In addition, we establish novel uniform concentration results that hold along the entire trajectory, and are essential for obtaining order-wise optimal sample complexity. We corroborate our theory with extensive experiments across a range of configurations.

AI.tldr: This paper rigorously analyzes gradient descent dynamics for training both layers of a one-hidden-layer ReLU network to fit a linear target, showing convergence to a global minimizer at linear rate with optimal sample complexity through three distinct phases.
AI.motivation: Understanding why gradient descent from random initialization reliably escapes bad non-strict saddle points in end-to-end training of neural networks, particularly in inverse problems and auto-encoder models.
AI.method: The authors track gradient descent trajectory through three phases: (1) alignment phase where hidden weights align with the planted direction and output weights get correct sign, (2) growth phase where layer norms increase while maintaining alignment, and (3) local refinement phase where neurons converge rapidly to the planted direction. They develop trajectory-level control arguments to show GD avoids non-strict saddles and establish uniform concentration results.
AI.result: Gradient descent from moderately small random initialization converges to a global minimizer at a linear rate with order-wise optimal sample complexity.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a detailed characterization of the optimization landscape and rigorous proof that end-to-end gradient descent successfully escapes bad stationary regions and converges optimally, validated by extensive experiments.

title: Recent Advances and Trends in Learning-based 3D Representations
summary: The selection of an appropriate 3D representation is a fundamental design decision that dictates the efficiency, quality, and capabilities of modern computer vision and graphics pipelines for tasks such as 3D reconstruction, novel-view synthesis and rendering, shape and motion analysis, recognition, and generation. While traditional representations (\eg meshes, point clouds, and volumetric grids) remain standard outputs of 3D sensors (\eg LiDAR and 3D scanners) and are widely used in downstream applications (\eg editing and simulation), recent neural and primitive-based representations (\eg 3D Gaussian Splatting) offer compact and differentiable alternatives opening a wide range of opportunities in applications such as games, AR/VR, autonomous driving, robot navigation, and medical imaging, to name a few. The goal of this paper is to survey the main families of 3D representations from discrete explicit formats to continuous implicit fields based either on neural rendering or primitive splatting. For each type of representation, we present the general formulation and its variants, discuss its benefits and limitations, and highlight key applications. We conclude the paper by outlining the open challenges and potential directions for future research. Distinct from recent surveys that broadly cover 3D object and scene reconstruction, this paper provides a focused analysis on the evolution of 3D representations themselves. We specifically emphasize the paradigm shift toward implicit representations, offering a novel perspective on how these emerging formats fundamentally alter 3D/4D workflows.

AI.tldr: This survey paper comprehensively reviews 3D representations from traditional explicit formats (meshes, point clouds, volumetric grids) to modern implicit representations (neural fields and primitive splatting like 3D Gaussian Splatting), analyzing their formulations, benefits, limitations, and applications while highlighting the paradigm shift toward implicit representations in 3D/4D workflows.
AI.motivation: The selection of appropriate 3D representation fundamentally impacts efficiency, quality, and capabilities in computer vision and graphics pipelines. Traditional representations remain standard outputs of 3D sensors, but recent neural and primitive-based representations offer compact and differentiable alternatives opening new opportunities across diverse applications.
AI.method: The paper surveys main families of 3D representations from discrete explicit formats to continuous implicit fields based on neural rendering or primitive splatting, presenting general formulations and variants, discussing benefits and limitations, and highlighting key applications for each type.
AI.result: A focused analysis on the evolution of 3D representations themselves, distinct from broad surveys on 3D reconstruction, emphasizing the paradigm shift toward implicit representations and how these emerging formats alter 3D/4D workflows.
AI.conclusion: The paper outlines open challenges and potential directions for future research in 3D representations.

title: LimiX-2M: Mitigating Low-Rank Collapse and Attention Bottlenecks in Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) increasingly rival tree ensembles, but their performance is often compute-inefficient: with standard affine scalar tokenization, each feature injects value variation through an essentially one-dimensional channel, and feature IDs/positional signals cannot increase within-feature value degrees of freedom, yielding weak early-layer value sensitivity and redundant hidden states. We present a unified \emph{tokenize-and-route} framework for strong TFMs: \textbf{RaBEL} expands each scalar into compact localized RBF features (optionally exponent-gated) to improve conditioning and shallow-layer effective rank, while a reordered bidirectional block \textbf{S$\rightarrow$N$\rightarrow$F} aligns computation with the readout by aggregating cross-sample context before feature mixing and using attention pooling. Together, these changes yield \textbf{LimiX-2M}, a 2M-parameter model that outperforms larger TabPFN-v2 and TabICL baselines on widely used tabular benchmarks while reducing training and inference costs. These results highlight value-aware tokenization and readout-aligned routing as key levers for improving the accuracy--efficiency trade-off in TFMs. Model checkpoints and inference code are available at https://github.com/limix-ldm-ai/LimiX.

AI.tldr: RaBEL tokenization + S→N→F routing enables LimiX-2M (2M params) to outperform much larger TabPFN-v2/TabICL at lower cost.
AI.motivation: Standard affine scalar tokenization in TFMs yields weak early-layer value sensitivity and redundant hidden states because each feature is compressed into a one-dimensional channel, and positional/ID signals cannot add value-specific degrees of freedom.
AI.method: (1) RaBEL: expands each scalar into compact localized RBF features (optionally exponent-gated) for better conditioning and shallow-layer effective rank. (2) Reordered bidirectional block S→N→F: aggregates cross-sample context before feature mixing, then uses attention pooling, aligning computation with readout order.
AI.result: LimiX-2M (2M params) outperforms larger TabPFN-v2 and TabICL on standard tabular benchmarks while reducing both training and inference costs.
AI.conclusion: Value-aware tokenization and readout-aligned routing are key to improving the accuracy–efficiency trade-off in tabular foundation models.

title: MAOAM: Unified Object and Material Selection with Vision-Language Models
comment: Accepted to SIGGRAPH 2026 Conference. Project page: \href{https://jadenpark0.github.io/project_pages/maoam/}{here}
summary: Selection is a core operation in interactive image editing. To be practical, a user should be able to specify and disambiguate the desired selection region through either text or click-based interactions, and the system should support selecting not only objects but also other criteria, such as materials. Material-based selection is valuable for tasks like re-texturing surfaces or editing instances of a specific material. However, existing vision-language-model (VLM) based selection methods are object-centric and typically support a single interaction modality, limiting their applicability. In this work, we thus present Mask Any Object And Material (MAOAM), a unified selection framework that enables precise object and material-level selection across both text- and click-based interactions. MAOAM leverages a VLM with a segmentation head to produce pixel-accurate masks from user prompts: the VLM interprets the user's selection intent (object or material-level) and encodes visual entities, attributes, and spatial relations, while the segmentation head decodes the output token into a mask. A key challenge is the lack of material selection datasets with text annotations. We propose a scalable data generation pipeline: we collect real and synthetic images with material masks, and leverage VLMs to generate material descriptions with rich visual-semantics. We train MAOAM with a multi-task objective over click and text-based selection, along with an auxiliary VQA task derived from the material descriptions to facilitate deeper material understanding. Despite being trained with uni-modal prompts, our model exhibits an emergent improvement in selection when combining text and clicks at inference, enabling flexible image editing workflows. Experiments demonstrate accurate and coherent selections across diverse objects, materials, and interaction scenarios, highlighting robustness in practice.

AI.tldr: MAOAM is a unified image selection framework that supports both object and material-level selection via text and click interactions, using a VLM with a segmentation head, a scalable data pipeline for material annotations, and multi-task training that enables emergent multi-modal improvement at inference.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-based selection methods are object-centric and support only a single interaction modality (text or click), lacking support for material-based selection which is valuable for tasks like re-texturing or editing specific materials.
AI.method: MAOAM uses a VLM with a segmentation head; the VLM interprets user intent (object or material level) and encodes entities/attributes/spatial relations, while the segmentation head decodes into pixel-accurate masks. It uses a scalable data pipeline collecting real/synthetic images with material masks and VLM-generated descriptions, trained with multi-task objective over click/text selection plus an auxiliary VQA task.
AI.result: The model achieves accurate and coherent selections across diverse objects, materials, and interaction scenarios. An emergent improvement is observed when combining text and clicks at inference, despite being trained with uni-modal prompts.
AI.conclusion: MAOAM provides a practical, robust unified selection framework for interactive image editing, successfully bridging object and material-level selection across multiple interaction modalities.

title: Episodic Memory Temporal Consistency for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) frequently suffers from severe reward sparsity and exploration bottlenecks. While episodic memory mechanisms mitigate these issues by reusing high-return trajectories, they often trap agents in local optima due to unconstrained incentive distribution and semantic representation collapse. To address this, we propose Episodic Memory Temporal Consistency (EMTC), a framework that robustly constructs and selectively leverages historical experiences. EMTC introduces two synergistic components: (1) a Temporally Consistent Semantic Embedder that integrates contrastive learning with time-conditioned state reconstruction, preventing representation collapse and enabling precise memory retrieval; and (2) a Temporal Consistency Gating Mechanism that dynamically modulates episodic incentives based on temporal consistency error. This adaptive gate filters misleading signals from pseudo-successful trajectories, effectively mitigating Q-value overestimation. We provide theoretical guarantees, establishing a strict error bound that directly links the observable temporal consistency error to the underlying trajectory optimality and representation quality. Extensive evaluations on the SMAC and GRF benchmarks demonstrate that EMTC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Notably, compared to the strongest episodic baseline, EMTC achieves absolute win-rate improvements of up to 24% in super-hard SMAC scenarios and an average improvement of 28% across GRF tasks.

AI.tldr: EMTC introduces temporal consistency learning and adaptive gating to improve episodic memory in cooperative MARL, achieving up to 28% win-rate improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Cooperative MARL suffers from reward sparsity and exploration bottlenecks. Existing episodic memory methods reuse high-return trajectories but trap agents in local optima due to unconstrained incentive distribution and semantic representation collapse.
AI.method: EMTC has two components: (1) Temporally Consistent Semantic Embedder, using contrastive learning and time-conditioned state reconstruction to prevent representation collapse; (2) Temporal Consistency Gating Mechanism, which dynamically filters episodic incentives based on temporal consistency error to mitigate Q-value overestimation.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on SMAC and GRF benchmarks. Achieves up to 24% absolute win-rate improvement in super-hard SMAC scenarios and 28% average improvement across GRF tasks compared to the strongest episodic baseline.
AI.conclusion: EMTC effectively addresses local optima traps in episodic memory MARL by enforcing temporal consistency, with theoretical guarantees linking temporal consistency error to trajectory optimality and representation quality.

title: DiverAge: Reliable Pluralistic Face Aging with Cross-Age Identity Relation Guidance
summary: Face aging plays an important role in long-term biometric analysis, cross-age identity verification, and forensic identity analysis. Since the same subject may exhibit multiple plausible appearances at a target age due to genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors, face aging is inherently a one-to-many generation problem. However, pluralism alone is insufficient for reliable face aging: a model should provide appearance-level candidate diversity within each age group while maintaining sequence-level ordinal reliability across ordered age groups. Existing deterministic aging methods can synthesize visually plausible age-progressed faces, but usually lack stochastic diversity. In contrast, pluralistic aging methods introduce local appearance variations, but often fail to explicitly regulate the identity evolution of the full aging sequence. In this paper, we propose \textbf{DiverAge}, a hierarchical pluralistic face aging framework based on diffusion autoencoding. DiverAge preserves appearance-level diversity through stochastic diffusion decoding and age-conditioned semantic modulation. To improve sequence-level reliability, we introduce a Cross-age Identity Relation Regulator (CARR), an inference-time guidance strategy that jointly denoises multiple target age groups. CARR is guided by a Cross-age Identity Similarity (CIS) prior estimated from real same-identity cross-age pairs, and suppresses excessive cross-age identity drift through one-sided sampling-time guidance without modifying the training objective or introducing extra trainable parameters. Experiments demonstrate that DiverAge improves sequence-level ordinal reliability while maintaining identity preservation, age accuracy, image quality, and appearance-level diversity.

AI.tldr: DiverAge is a hierarchical pluralistic face aging framework using diffusion autoencoding that preserves appearance diversity via stochastic decoding and improves cross-age identity consistency through an inference-time Cross-age Identity Relation Regulator (CARR) guided by real same-identity cross-age similarity priors.
AI.motivation: Face aging is inherently a one-to-many problem due to genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors. Existing deterministic methods lack stochastic diversity, while pluralistic methods fail to regulate identity evolution across the full aging sequence. The authors aim to achieve both appearance-level diversity and sequence-level ordinal reliability.
AI.method: DiverAge uses diffusion autoencoding with stochastic decoding and age-conditioned semantic modulation for appearance diversity. A Cross-age Identity Relation Regulator (CARR) is introduced as an inference-time guidance strategy that jointly denoises multiple target age groups, guided by a Cross-age Identity Similarity (CIS) prior from real data, to suppress identity drift without modifying training objectives or adding parameters.
AI.result: DiverAge improves sequence-level ordinal reliability while maintaining identity preservation, age accuracy, image quality, and appearance-level diversity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical pluralistic framework effectively balances diversity and cross-age identity consistency in face aging, achieving both appearance-level candidate diversity and sequence-level ordinal reliability.

title: Smart Picks in the Dark: Towards Efficient RLVR for Reasoning via Tracing Metacognitive Pivots
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has greatly advanced large reasoning models (LRMs), but it requires timely training on a huge fully-annotated dataset. To this end, data-efficient RLVR methods have been widely studied from two perspectives: (i) data selection methods identify a small subset of "golden" samples that yield near-full-data performance, but they rely on a pre-existing pool of labeled data. (ii) unsupervised RLVR methods train the model using its own internal supervision signals on large-scale unlabeled data, yet they exhibit suboptimal performance. Accordingly, we investigate the "pick in the dark" setup for RLVR, which aims to select, without prior supervision, unlabeled samples that are most beneficial for training and worthy of annotation. Through systematic analysis, we demonstrate that smart picks hinge on a well-calibrated uncertainty estimator to enable strategic partitioning of data for adaptive training regimes. Building on this insight, we propose PivotTrace, a three-way data triage framework that leverages attention dynamics to trace metacognitive pivots during reasoning. By precisely quantifying uncertainty through pivot density, PivotTrace achieves automated data routing to synergistically maximize both annotation and training efficiency. Empirically, PivotTrace surpasses the fully supervised LRM with only 29.3% annotated samples and 2.75 faster convergence.

AI.tldr: PivotTrace is a data-efficient RLVR framework that uses attention dynamics to trace metacognitive pivots during reasoning, enabling automated triage and annotation of unlabeled data, achieving full-data performance with only 29.3% annotated samples and 2.75× faster convergence.
AI.motivation: RLVR requires timely training on huge fully-annotated datasets. Existing data-efficient methods either require pre-existing labeled data (data selection) or exhibit suboptimal performance (unsupervised RLVR). The goal is to select the most beneficial unlabeled samples for annotation without prior supervision.
AI.method: PivotTrace is a three-way data triage framework that leverages attention dynamics to trace metacognitive pivots during reasoning. It quantifies uncertainty through pivot density, enabling strategic partitioning of data for adaptive training regimes and automated data routing.
AI.result: PivotTrace surpasses fully supervised LRM with only 29.3% annotated samples and achieves 2.75× faster convergence.
AI.conclusion: Smart picks for RLVR hinge on a well-calibrated uncertainty estimator, and PivotTrace demonstrates that tracing metacognitive pivots via attention dynamics effectively enables data-efficient RLVR in the "pick in the dark" setup.

title: HD-DinoMoE: A Class-Aware Hierarchical Dual Mixture-of-Experts Network for Scleral Anomaly Segmentation in Complex Acquisition Scenarios
comment: Submitted to Medical Image Analysis; 47 pages, 31 figures, 14 tables
summary: Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) ocular inspection provides empirical cues for assessing scleral surface anomalies, but its clinical use remains subjective and difficult to quantify. To support intelligent and quantifiable ocular inspection, this study presents the TCM-inspired Artificial Intelligence Ocular Auxiliary Diagnosis System (TAO) and focuses on pixel-level scleral surface anomaly segmentation. For clinical and user-acquired images affected by multi-source distributional discrepancies, diverse anomaly morphologies, and scleral specular reflection (SSR), we propose HD-DinoMoE, a class-aware hierarchical dual mixture-of-experts network. HD-DinoMoE combines class-aware dual-stream DINOv3 feature fusion with class-specific multi-expert decoding to segment Vessels, Yellow and Black Spots, and Blood Spots. A three-stage backbone-frozen routing strategy stabilizes dual-backbone adaptation; Progressive Confidence Penalty (PCP) Loss reduces high-confidence false positives and segmentation leakage in SSR regions; and Class-Aware Adaptive Sample Weighting (CA-ASW) balances sample- and class-level training contributions. We further construct the Multi-label Scleral Anomaly Segmentation Dataset (ML-SASD), a new benchmark with Clinical, Wild, and Mix settings and pixel-wise annotations for three anomaly categories. On ML-SASD-Mix, HD-DinoMoE achieves a mean Dice of 72.11% and a mean Intersection-over-Union of 58.44%, while maintaining favorable boundary localization and specular-region false-positive control. It also shows competitive generalization on the Vessels subset of the public SBVPI dataset. These results indicate that HD-DinoMoE provides a feasible segmentation solution for TAO under complex acquisition scenarios. The code and data access information are available at https://github.com/FX-CMX/HD-DinoMoE.

AI.tldr: HD-DinoMoE is a hierarchical dual mixture-of-experts network that segments scleral surface anomalies (vessels, spots) for TCM-inspired ocular diagnosis, achieving 72.11% mean Dice on the new ML-SASD benchmark.
AI.motivation: Traditional Chinese Medicine ocular inspection is subjective and hard to quantify; this work aims to support intelligent, quantifiable scleral anomaly assessment despite challenges like multi-source distribution shifts, diverse anomaly morphologies, and scleral specular reflection.
AI.method: HD-DinoMoE combines class-aware dual-stream DINOv3 feature fusion with class-specific multi-expert decoding. It uses a three-stage backbone-frozen routing strategy, Progressive Confidence Penalty (PCP) Loss for SSR regions, and Class-Aware Adaptive Sample Weighting (CA-ASW). The authors also introduce the ML-SASD benchmark with Clinical, Wild, and Mix settings.
AI.result: On ML-SASD-Mix, HD-DinoMoE achieves 72.11% mean Dice and 58.44% mean IoU with strong boundary localization and false-positive control in specular regions. It also generalizes well on the SBVPI Vessels subset.
AI.conclusion: HD-DinoMoE provides a feasible segmentation solution for the TAO system under complex acquisition scenarios, demonstrating the potential of AI-driven TCM ocular inspection.

title: GeoMin: Data-Efficient Semi-Supervised RLVR via Geometric Distribution Modeling
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) significantly advances LLM reasoning, yet it faces a dilemma: standard supervised scaling is throttled by high annotation costs, while unsupervised alternatives suffer from severe model collapse. Recent semi-supervised RLVR methods address this by using a small labeled set to guide unlabeled data, achieving a promising trade-off between training efficacy and annotation cost. However, they suffer from a severe data-efficiency bottleneck due to the reliance on coarse performance heuristics, leaving a vast majority of valuable instances underutilized. To this end, we propose GeoMin, which models global feature distributions on labeled data to decode the structural discrepancy between correct and incorrect rollouts, thereby establishing a robust prior to assess the reliability of self-reward signals and fully unleash the potential of unlabeled data. Empirically, GeoMin outperforms the strongest baselines by +4.1% and even surpasses fully supervised models with only 10% of the annotations, demonstrating remarkable data efficiency.

AI.tldr: GeoMin improves semi-supervised RLVR for LLMs by modeling global feature distributions to better leverage unlabeled data, achieving +4.1% over baselines and surpassing fully supervised models with only 10% annotations.
AI.motivation: Semi-supervised RLVR methods face a data-efficiency bottleneck because they rely on coarse performance heuristics, leaving most valuable unlabeled instances underutilized.
AI.method: GeoMin models global feature distributions on labeled data to decode structural discrepancies between correct and incorrect rollouts, creating a robust prior to assess self-reward signal reliability and better exploit unlabeled data.
AI.result: GeoMin outperforms strongest baselines by +4.1% and surpasses fully supervised models using only 10% of the annotation cost.
AI.conclusion: GeoMin offers a data-efficient solution to the RLVR dilemma by effectively bridging the gap between supervised and unsupervised approaches.

title: Hierarchical Space Partition for Surface Reconstruction
comment: Published in 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
summary: Generating compact polygonal models from point clouds is a key problem in 3D vision and computer graphics. However, due to inherent limitations of LiDAR scanning (e.g. range constraints and occlusions), critical scene information is often missing, leading to degraded reconstruction accuracy. To address this, we propose a plane assembling strategy that effectively recovers missing details while maintaining model compactness. We classify all the planes extracted from the scene into three categories: highly visible, barely visible, and invisible. The invisible planes, which are recovered by scene structure analysis, indicate the missing details. The three types of planes correspond to the three growth priorities. Each plane grows according to the priority level, and the space is partitioned progressively, namely, the hierarchical partition. Subsequently, we generate a watertight polygonal mesh from the partition via a min-cut-based optimization. Finally, comparisons on public datasets show the effectiveness and superiority of our method against mainstream approaches. The project page is available at https://hsr-3dv.github.io/.

AI.tldr: A method for generating compact polygonal models from point clouds that recovers missing details by classifying planes into three visibility categories and using hierarchical partition with priority-based plane growth, followed by min-cut optimization for watertight mesh generation.
AI.motivation: LiDAR scanning limitations (range constraints, occlusions) cause missing scene information, leading to degraded reconstruction accuracy. Existing methods struggle to recover these missing details while maintaining model compactness.
AI.method: 1) Classifies extracted planes into three categories: highly visible, barely visible, and invisible (recovered via scene structure analysis). 2) Assigns growth priorities to each category. 3) Performs hierarchical partition where planes grow according to priority levels. 4) Generates watertight polygonal mesh via min-cut optimization.
AI.result: Comparative evaluations on public datasets demonstrate effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method over mainstream approaches.
AI.conclusion: The plane assembling strategy with priority-based hierarchical partition effectively recovers missing details from LiDAR point clouds while maintaining model compactness and watertightness.

title: Rollout-Level Advantage-Prioritized Experience Replay for GRPO
summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards with GRPO is a standard approach for post-training reasoning LLMs. It remains sample inefficient. Each rollout is used for a single gradient update and then discarded. Naive replay is not well suited in this setting because LLM policies drift quickly per gradient step. Stored rollouts therefore become stale and can destabilize training. We propose a rollout-level replay buffer for GRPO that stores and samples individual rollouts rather than whole groups. The buffer bounds staleness through age eviction. Any rollout older than tau_max training steps is removed. The buffer also preserves on-policy data via fresh-anchored composition. Each batch keeps its fresh on-policy rollouts and then concatenates replay rollouts drawn separately from the buffer. We prioritize replay by per-rollout advantage magnitude and recycle individual rollouts whose advantages are large. Across three Qwen3-Base scales on five math benchmarks, our method outperforms GRPO and naive replay baselines. Gains are positive at every scale and grow with model size. The largest gain is +4.35 pp on the five-benchmark average at 4B. Under an AES metric that jointly measures accuracy and token efficiency, the efficiency margin over GRPO is again largest at 4B, at +0.579.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a rollout-level replay buffer for GRPO in RL post-training of LLMs, which addresses sample inefficiency by storing, prioritizing, and recycling individual rollouts with age-based eviction and fresh-anchored composition. It achieves significant gains over GRPO and naive replay, especially at larger model scales.
AI.motivation: Standard GRPO for post-training reasoning LLMs is sample inefficient because each rollout is used for a single gradient update then discarded. Naive replay fails due to rapid policy drift causing stale rollouts that destabilize training.
AI.method: A rollout-level replay buffer for GRPO that: (1) stores and samples individual rollouts rather than whole groups, (2) bounds staleness via age eviction (removing rollouts older than tau_max steps), (3) preserves on-policy data via fresh-anchored composition (keeping fresh rollouts + concatenating replay rollouts), and (4) prioritizes replay by per-rollout advantage magnitude, recycling individual rollouts with large advantages.
AI.result: Outperforms GRPO and naive replay across Qwen3-Base at three scales on five math benchmarks. Gains grow with model size; largest gain is +4.35 pp average at 4B. AES metric (accuracy + token efficiency) shows largest margin over GRPO at 4B (+0.579).
AI.conclusion: The proposed replay buffer significantly improves sample efficiency and performance of GRPO-based RL for reasoning LLMs, with positive and scaling gains across model sizes.

title: CDPM-Align: Multi-Scale Guidance-Aligned Diffusion Pretraining for Robust Few-Shot Anatomical Landmark Detection
summary: Anatomical landmark detection is a fundamental task in medical image analysis supporting a wide range of diagnostic and interventional workflows. Although recent methods have achieved sub-millimetric localisation, accuracy alone is not sufficient for clinical deployment, requiring reliability and robustness in prediction. Despite its clinical relevance, the impact of representation learning in this context is still underexplored. In this work, we introduce CDPM-align, a multi-scale guidance-aligned conditional diffusion pre-training for anatomical landmark detection. Our experimental setup focuses on a few images and a few annotation regimes. Specifically, we employ three popular heterogeneous small-scale benchmark datasets for representation learning via conditional generative pre-training. Furthermore, we consider low-annotation scenarios for the downstream task of landmark detection, with 10 and 25 annotated images, reflecting realistic trade-offs between clinical effort and resource constraints for annotations. Our results confirm that generative pre-training enables the model to learn a robust representation. This improves both accuracy and uncertainty on the downstream tasks, advancing towards safe and efficient clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: CDPM-align introduces a multi-scale guidance-aligned conditional diffusion pre-training method for anatomical landmark detection that improves accuracy and uncertainty estimation, especially in few-image and low-annotation regimes, advancing clinical deployability.
AI.motivation: Anatomical landmark detection requires not just accuracy but also reliability and robustness for clinical use, yet representation learning's impact in this area remains underexplored. The authors aim to address this gap, particularly under realistic data scarcity.
AI.method: The method uses multi-scale guidance-aligned conditional diffusion pre-training (CDPM-align) on three heterogeneous small-scale benchmark datasets. It then evaluates downstream landmark detection in low-annotation scenarios (10 and 25 annotated images).
AI.result: Generative pre-training via CDPM-align improves both accuracy and uncertainty estimation in downstream landmark detection tasks under limited annotation settings.
AI.conclusion: Conditional diffusion pre-training enables robust representation learning, helping models perform reliably with few annotations, moving toward safer clinical deployment of landmark detection systems.

title: SurvPFN: Towards Foundation Models for Survival Predictions
comment: 10 pages, 1 figure. Accepted to "Foundation Models for Structured Data" Workshop at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) have made rapid progress in standard classification and regression, but time-to-event survival prediction tasks have remained largely untouched. Unlike in standard regression tasks, survival prediction models must account for censored data. Standard TFMs cannot handle natively censored data, leading to biased and inaccurate predictions, making them unsuitable for real-world applications. To overcome this fundamental limitation, we propose \texttt{SurvPFN}, a prior-data fitted network (PFN), for survival prediction tasks. We pretrain \texttt{SurvPFN} on millions of synthetic survival prediction tasks to learn survival via distributional regression that accounts for censored data. \texttt{SurvPFN} works by (1) generating data with Weibull event times and a non-informative censoring mechanism; (2) integrating a censored event indicator; and (3) minimizing a censored negative log-likelihood. On SurvSet, a collection of real-world survival tasks, \texttt{SurvPFN} is highly competitive with classical and deep survival baselines without per-dataset fitting, a survival-specific architecture, or feature engineering. We show that survival can be treated as a continuous-time distributional regression problem with censored loss, unlocking the power of PFNs for time-to-event predictions.

AI.tldr: SurvPFN is a Prior-data Fitted Network that extends tabular foundation models to survival prediction by handling censored data through distributional regression on synthetic Weibull-generated tasks, achieving competitive performance against classical and deep survival models without per-dataset retraining or specialized architecture.
AI.motivation: Standard tabular foundation models (TFMs) cannot handle natively censored data in survival prediction tasks, leading to biased and inaccurate predictions, making them unsuitable for real-world clinical and reliability applications.
AI.method: SurvPFN pretrains on millions of synthetic survival tasks where data is generated using Weibull event times with non-informative censoring, integrates a censored event indicator, and minimizes a censored negative log-likelihood loss for distributional regression.
AI.result: On the SurvSet benchmark, SurvPFN is highly competitive with classical (e.g., Cox PH) and deep survival baselines without requiring per-dataset fitting, survival-specific architecture, or feature engineering.
AI.conclusion: Survival can be effectively treated as a continuous-time distributional regression problem with censored loss, enabling PFNs to perform time-to-event predictions while generalizing zero-shot across diverse survival tasks.

title: BreastGPT: A Multimodal Large Language Model for the Full Spectrum of Breast Cancer Clinical Routine
summary: Breast cancer remains a leading cause of cancer-related mortality among women. Its clinical management requires multimodal reasoning across a clinical workflow that spans \textit{screening}, \textit{diagnosis} and \textit{treatment planning}, where each stage involves distinct imaging modalities, task objectives, and reasoning patterns. However, constrained by data scarcity and model versatility, existing medical MLLMs are typically evaluated on isolated modalities or narrow task families, limiting their ability to support workflow-level clinical reasoning. In this work, we first introduce \textbf{BreastStage}, a workflow-aligned breast imaging instruction corpus comprising 1.86M instruction-following pairs curated from 17 sub-datasets across 5 imaging modalities and 136 task templates. Its held-out split, \textbf{BreastStage-Bench}, provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating multimodal reasoning across the breast cancer care continuum. Building on this corpus, we propose \textbf{BreastGPT}, a unified MLLM equipped with a dual-branch visual encoder and concept-preserving token compression to bridge the scale gap between standard radiology and gigapixel pathology. On BreastStage-Bench, BreastGPT achieves 75.66\% closed-ended accuracy and 89.92\% open-ended score, outperforming both general-purpose and medical-specific MLLMs across clinical stages and task formats. These results suggest that workflow-aligned data and cross-scale visual modeling are critical for clinically grounded medical MLLMs. All data, code, and model checkpoints are released at https://yangyy-liu.github.io/BreastGPT.io.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces BreastStage (a 1.86M instruction-following dataset spanning 5 imaging modalities and 136 task templates across the breast cancer clinical workflow), BreastStage-Bench (a benchmark for workflow-level evaluation), and BreastGPT (a unified MLLM with dual-branch visual encoder and concept-preserving token compression). BreastGPT achieves 75.66% closed-ended accuracy and 89.92% open-ended score, outperforming existing MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing medical MLLMs are evaluated on isolated modalities or narrow task families, lacking the ability to support workflow-level clinical reasoning across screening, diagnosis, and treatment planning stages of breast cancer care.
AI.method: The authors constructed BreastStage, a workflow-aligned breast imaging instruction corpus with 1.86M pairs from 17 sub-datasets, 5 imaging modalities, and 136 task templates. They then developed BreastGPT, a unified MLLM with a dual-branch visual encoder to handle both standard radiology and gigapixel pathology images, and concept-preserving token compression for efficiency.
AI.result: BreastGPT achieves 75.66% closed-ended accuracy and 89.92% open-ended score on BreastStage-Bench, outperforming both general-purpose and medical-specific MLLMs across clinical stages and task formats.
AI.conclusion: Workflow-aligned data and cross-scale visual modeling are critical for developing clinically grounded medical MLLMs.

title: Dynamic Multi-Pair Trading Strategy in Cryptocurrency Markets with Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: This study aims to determine whether the application of Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) as a specialized execution overlay can enhance pair trading in highly volatile cryptocurrency markets. Although classical implementations of the strategy have proven successful in traditional equities, they frequently exhibit rigidity and suffer from severe divergence risks when applied to high-variance environments. To address this need, this research introduces novel concepts. To construct a robust system, we developed a hierarchical "Filter-then-Rank" pair selection methodology and a proprietary "Fixed Risk, Adaptive Mean" execution model. The system employs a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) layer to govern execution decisions within strict deterministic risk management boundaries. Evaluated on 1-hour interval data from the Binance USD-M Futures market, the optimized RL policy achieved an out-of-sample performance that substantially outperformed the heuristic baseline. A stationary circular block bootstrap robustness check confirms that the agent's risk-adjusted outperformance is statistically significant at the 10 percent level. Although falling marginally short of the stricter 5 percent threshold, this result highlights the extreme idiosyncratic variance characteristic of digital assets. Ultimately, this thesis contributes to the quantitative finance literature by introducing a hybrid architecture that combines statistical arbitrage with DRL execution policies. Furthermore, it delivers a novel framework for safe reinforcement learning via deterministic shielding, proving that anchoring a neural policy to statistically robust boundaries successfully mitigates severe divergence risks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hybrid system combining statistical arbitrage (pair trading) with Deep Reinforcement Learning (PPO+LSTM) for cryptocurrency markets, introducing a "Filter-then-Rank" pair selection method and a "Fixed Risk, Adaptive Mean" execution model. The RL agent, shielded by deterministic risk boundaries, outperforms heuristic baselines on Binance USD-M Futures data, with statistically significant risk-adjusted returns at the 10% level.
AI.motivation: Classical pair trading strategies work in traditional equities but suffer from rigidity and severe divergence risks when applied to high-variance cryptocurrency markets. A more adaptive, risk-aware execution overlay is needed.
AI.method: Hierarchical "Filter-then-Rank" pair selection; "Fixed Risk, Adaptive Mean" execution model; PPO agent with LSTM layer governing execution within deterministic risk management boundaries; evaluated on 1-hour Binance USD-M Futures data.
AI.result: The optimized RL policy substantially outperformed the heuristic baseline out-of-sample. A stationary circular block bootstrap test confirmed risk-adjusted outperformance is statistically significant at the 10% level (marginally below the 5% threshold).
AI.conclusion: The hybrid statistical arbitrage + DRL architecture successfully mitigates severe divergence risks via deterministic shielding, contributing a novel safe reinforcement learning framework for quantitative finance.

title: Geometry-Aware Distillation for Prompt Tuning Biomedical Vision-Language Models
comment: Preprint. Code is available at https://github.com/tientrandinh/OGKD
summary: Current prompt-based and adapter-based tuning of vision-language models (VLMs) is attractive for medical imaging, where clinical data sensitivity favors frozen backbones and annotations are limited. However, these methods typically optimize only the ground-truth class, treating all other classes as equally incorrect, ignoring clinically meaningful class relations and yielding unstable decision boundaries in limited-supervision settings. We propose Omni-Geometry Knowledge Distillation (OGKD), a new framework that injects class-relation structure into the teacher to produce directional targets that preserve the ground truth while respecting inter-class geometry. Using these targets, we develop two distillation losses: Global Geometry-Aware Distillation (GAD) operates on the global image token, and Label-Guided Geometry Distillation (LGD) applies the same geometry to attentive patch tokens to improve fine-grained alignment. Across comprehensive experiments and analyses on 11 widely-used medical datasets for base-to-novel and few-shot evaluations, our OGKD achieves substantially better performance, consistently improving accuracy by an average absolute gain of 1.7%-2.8% over all prior state-of-the-art VLM adaptation counterparts. It also robustly generalizes to unseen classes and yields more reliable predictions than other approaches. Our code is available at https://github.com/tientrandinh/OGKD.

AI.tldr: OGKD is a new knowledge distillation framework for vision-language models in medical imaging that preserves class-relation geometry instead of treating all non-target classes as equally incorrect, achieving 1.7%-2.8% absolute accuracy gains over prior SOTA across 11 medical datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt-based and adapter-based VLM tuning methods optimize only the ground-truth class, treating all other classes as equally incorrect, which ignores clinically meaningful class relations and yields unstable decision boundaries in limited-supervision settings.
AI.method: OGKD injects class-relation structure into the teacher model to produce directional targets preserving ground truth while respecting inter-class geometry, then uses two distillation losses: Global Geometry-Aware Distillation (GAD) on the global image token, and Label-Guided Geometry Distillation (LGD) on attentive patch tokens for fine-grained alignment.
AI.result: Across 11 medical datasets for base-to-novel and few-shot evaluations, OGKD achieves consistent accuracy improvements of 1.7%-2.8% absolute gain over all prior SOTA VLM adaptation methods, with robust generalization to unseen classes and more reliable predictions.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating class-relation geometry into knowledge distillation significantly improves VLM adaptation for medical imaging, especially under limited supervision, and the approach generalizes robustly to unseen classes.

title: HalfNet: Randomized Neural Networks with Learned Subspace Geometry
summary: Many researchers investigated neural networks with some of their weights fixed to values randomly drawn from a given distribution, e.g., $N(0, I)$. Our proposed HalfNet draws random weights from $N(0, Σ)$, where $Σ$, which defines the geometry of the distribution, has a low-rank factorization that we learn from data. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate that HalfNet can match the performance of fully trained multilayer perceptrons while using substantially fewer parameters. Spectral analysis indicates that much of the predictive power of neural networks lies in the geometry of their weight space rather than in the precise values of individual parameters, and we observe that accuracy scales smoothly with rank. HalfNet is not a neural architecture trick for low-rank structure; it implements a data-dependent random embedding that can also be interpreted through supervised metric learning, or random-feature and kernel perspectives.

AI.tldr: HalfNet replaces most neural network weights with random draws from a learned low-rank covariance matrix, achieving competitive performance with far fewer trained parameters.
AI.motivation: To investigate how much predictive power comes from the geometry (distribution shape) versus precise values of neural network weights, and to reduce the number of trained parameters.
AI.method: HalfNet fixes most weights to random draws from N(0, Σ), where Σ has a low-rank factorization learned from data. Only the covariance structure (geometry) is trained, not individual weight values.
AI.result: HalfNet matches fully trained MLP performance on MNIST and CIFAR-10 with substantially fewer parameters. Accuracy scales smoothly with the rank of Σ.
AI.conclusion: Much of neural networks' predictive power lies in weight space geometry rather than precise parameter values; the approach connects to metric learning, random features, and kernel methods.

title: Scene-Centric Unsupervised Video Panoptic Segmentation
comment: CVPR 2026. Oliver Hahn and Christoph Reich - both authors contributed equally. Code: https://github.com/visinf/cups/tree/main/videocups Project page: https://visinf.github.io/videocups/
summary: Video panoptic segmentation (VPS) aims to jointly detect, segment, and track all objects while partitioning the video into semantically consistent regions. We introduce the task setting of unsupervised VPS, omitting any human supervision. Existing unsupervised scene understanding works mainly focused on image segmentation tasks; the video domain remains underexplored. We propose VideoCUPS, the first unsupervised VPS approach. VideoCUPS generates temporally consistent panoptic video pseudo-labels from scene-centric videos by exploiting unsupervised depth, motion, and visual cues. Training on these pseudo-labels using a novel Video DropLoss yields an accurate, unsupervised VPS model. To benchmark progress, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation protocol and four competitive baselines, extending state-of-the-art unsupervised panoptic image and instance video segmentation models to VPS. VideoCUPS outperforms all baselines and demonstrates strong label-efficient learning. With VideoCUPS, our evaluation protocol, and baselines, we provide a strong foundation for future research on unsupervised VPS.

AI.tldr: VideoCUPS is the first unsupervised video panoptic segmentation method that generates pseudo-labels using depth, motion, and visual cues, then trains a model with a novel Video DropLoss, outperforming all baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised scene understanding works focus on image segmentation tasks, leaving the video domain underexplored. The authors aim to address this gap by introducing unsupervised VPS without any human supervision.
AI.method: VideoCUPS generates temporally consistent panoptic video pseudo-labels from scene-centric videos by exploiting unsupervised depth, motion, and visual cues, then trains on these pseudo-labels using a novel Video DropLoss.
AI.result: VideoCUPS outperforms all four competitive baselines (extending state-of-the-art unsupervised panoptic image and instance video segmentation models) and demonstrates strong label-efficient learning.
AI.conclusion: VideoCUPS provides a strong foundation for future research on unsupervised VPS, along with a comprehensive evaluation protocol and baselines.

title: QuBLAST: A Framework for Quantizing Large Language Models with Block-Level Compression Approach and Activation Scaling Strategy
summary: LLMs have become the state-of-the-art algorithms for solving NLP tasks. However, they typically come at huge computational and memory costs, thus making them difficult to deploy on embedded systems. Toward this, state-of-the-art methods typically employ uniform post-training quantization (PTQ) across attention blocks of the network, hence overlooking the potential of applying different quantization levels in the same network. They also employ complex operations to mitigate the negative impact of activation outliers, hence incurring high computational overheads. Moreover, they have not considered evaluation using emerging LLMs with non-conventional attention architectures (e.g., state-space models), which pose different challenges in applying quantization. To address these limitations, we propose QuBLAST, a novel PTQ methodology that employs block-level compression approach with activation scaling strategy for LLMs. Block-level compression approach enables mixed-precision quantization across blocks of the network, while activation scaling strategy efficiently mitigates the negative impact of activation outliers. Specifically, QuBLAST first analyzes the sensitivity of different attention blocks in the pre-trained model through the cross-entropy loss analysis. QuBLAST leverages this sensitivity analysis to determine the weight quantization level for each attention block in the model. Furthermore, QuBLAST employs the activation scaling map for each block to control the range of activation values and mitigate the negative impact of activation outliers, thereby enabling better quantization results. Experimental results show that, QuBLAST reduces model sizes by 40%-45.2% across different model architectures (i.e., Qwen3-8B, Llama3-8B, Mistral v0.1-8B, and Falcon H1R-7B), while maintaining the performance within 5% perplexity increase for the WikiText-2 and WikiText-103 datasets.

AI.tldr: QuBLAST is a post-training quantization method for LLMs that uses mixed-precision block-level compression and activation scaling to reduce model size by 40-45.2% while keeping perplexity increase under 5%.
AI.motivation: LLMs suffer from huge computational and memory costs, making deployment on embedded systems difficult. Existing PTQ methods use uniform quantization across attention blocks, overlook mixed-precision opportunities, employ complex operations for activation outliers, and fail to evaluate on non-conventional attention architectures like state-space models.
AI.method: QuBLAST uses: (1) cross-entropy loss sensitivity analysis to determine per-block weight quantization levels; (2) an activation scaling map per block to control activation value ranges and mitigate outlier impacts; enabling mixed-precision block-level compression.
AI.result: Reduces model sizes by 40%-45.2% across Qwen3-8B, Llama3-8B, Mistral v0.1-8B, and Falcon H1R-7B, with less than 5% perplexity increase on WikiText-2 and WikiText-103 datasets.
AI.conclusion: QuBLAST effectively addresses limitations of uniform PTQ by enabling mixed-precision quantization and efficient outlier mitigation, achieving substantial compression with minimal performance degradation across diverse LLM architectures.

title: Plan, Watch, Recover: A Benchmark and Architectures for Proactive Procedural Assistance
summary: We envision a proactive multi-modal assistant system which gives users real-time step-by-step guidance on a procedural task, autonomously deciding \textit{when} to interrupt, and \textit{how} to coach. However, progress is limited by the absence of large-scale, cross-domain benchmarks that reflect realistic conditions, particularly the common case in which users deviate from the expected step sequence. We address this gap with four contributions: \textbf{(1)}~we release \textbf{EgoProactive}, a large-scale wearable-egocentric dataset for proactive procedural assistance with explicit Out-of-Plan (OOP) annotations and recovery steps; \textbf{(2)}~we augment five established benchmarks (Ego4D, EPIC-KITCHENS, EgoExo4D, HoloAssist, HowTo100M) into \textbf{Pro\textsuperscript{2}Bench} under a unified proactive-guidance schema; \textbf{(3)}~we propose a \textbf{decoupled planner--interaction architecture} specialized for procedural state, visual cues, and recovery injection; \textbf{(4)}~we introduce a post-training recipe that transfers across model families, validated by cross-backbone replication on Llama~4 and Qwen-3.6-VL. In extensive experiments, our trained Llama-4 system substantially improves objective intervention quality over strong proprietary baselines (Claude Opus~4.6, Gemini~3.1~Pro, GPT~5.2) and open-weight baselines (Qwen3~VL~235B) baselines across all six datasets. Oracle-plan experiments further show that, when plan quality is controlled, the trained duplex model produces high-quality guidance and large gains on Out-of-Plan recovery.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces EgoProactive (a large-scale egocentric dataset for proactive procedural assistance), Pro²Bench (a unified benchmark from five existing datasets), a decoupled planner-interaction architecture, and a post-training recipe. Their Llama-4 system outperforms strong baselines (Claude, Gemini, GPT) across six datasets, especially in Out-of-Plan recovery scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal assistant systems lack large-scale, cross-domain benchmarks that reflect real-world conditions—especially the frequent case where users deviate from expected step sequences (Out-of-Plan behavior). This gap limits progress toward proactive assistants that autonomously decide when to interrupt and how to coach.
AI.method: Four contributions: (1) EgoProactive dataset with OOP annotations; (2) Pro²Bench unifying five existing benchmarks; (3) a decoupled planner-interaction architecture for procedural state, visual cues, and recovery injection; (4) a post-training recipe transferable across model families (validated on Llama 4 and Qwen-3.6-VL).
AI.result: The trained Llama-4 system substantially outperforms proprietary baselines (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT 5.2) and open-weight baselines (Qwen3 VL 235B) across all six datasets. Oracle-plan experiments confirm high-quality guidance and large gains in Out-of-Plan recovery when plan quality is controlled.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dataset, benchmark, architecture, and training recipe enable proactive procedural assistance that handles real-world deviations effectively, outperforming both proprietary and open-source state-of-the-art models.

title: Learning symplectic model reduction based on a approximation theorem of symplectic embeddings
summary: High-dimensional Hamiltonian systems play a central role in many scientific and engineering disciplines, with dynamics evolving on symplectic manifolds. Although deep learning provides powerful tools for constructing low-dimensional surrogates from data, the intrinsic symplectic structure is easily destroyed during model reduction. As a result, a standard autoencoder may produce latent coordinates that do not support a Hamiltonian flow, leading to unstable long-time prediction. In this paper, we first establish a universal approximation theorem for symplectic embeddings. Based on this theory, we propose symplecticity-preserving autoencoders (SpAE), in which the decoder is parameterized as a symplectic embedding and the encoder is constructed as the corresponding symplectic projection. This architecture is expressive enough to approximate nonlinear symplectic embeddings and the associated symplectic projections, preserves the symplectic structure exactly by construction, and can be trained by standard unconstrained optimization, thereby improving both reconstruction and prediction accuracy. Extensive experiments on high-dimensional lattice and particle systems demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes symplecticity-preserving autoencoders (SpAE) for model reduction of high-dimensional Hamiltonian systems, where the decoder is a symplectic embedding and the encoder is the corresponding symplectic projection, ensuring exact preservation of symplectic structure for stable long-time prediction.
AI.motivation: Standard autoencoders destroy the intrinsic symplectic structure of Hamiltonian systems during model reduction, producing latent coordinates that do not support Hamiltonian flow and cause unstable long-time predictions.
AI.method: First establishes a universal approximation theorem for symplectic embeddings, then proposes SpAE where the decoder is parameterized as a symplectic embedding and the encoder as the corresponding symplectic projection, trained via standard unconstrained optimization.
AI.result: Experiments on high-dimensional lattice and particle systems demonstrate effectiveness in improving both reconstruction and prediction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SpAE preserves symplectic structure exactly by construction while being expressive enough to approximate nonlinear symplectic embeddings and projections, enabling stable and accurate long-time prediction.

title: Food-R1: A Unified Multi-Task Food Vision-Language Model with Reinforcement Learning
