summary: Recent studies have explored Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for food analysis. However, most existing methods rely primarily on supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which often limits reasoning and generalization capabilities. Moreover, high-quality large-scale nutritional annotations remain scarce. To address these issues, we introduce CalorieBench-80K, a large-scale benchmark with curated calorie labels and dietary advice annotations. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first food image benchmark to incorporate Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotations for calorie reasoning. We also propose Food-R1, a unified food VLM trained in a multi-task learning paradigm to equip the model with broad capabilities. Food-R1 undergoes CoT-based cold-start instruction tuning, followed by reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to improve reasoning and performance. Experiments on CalorieBench-80K and representative benchmarks show that Food-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines across food-related tasks. The code, model weights, and benchmark annotations are available at the project repository.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CalorieBench-80K (a large-scale benchmark with CoT calorie reasoning annotations) and Food-R1 (a unified food VLM trained via cold-start CoT instruction tuning + GRPO-based reinforcement fine-tuning), which outperforms baselines across food-related tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM methods for food analysis rely on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with limited reasoning/generalization capabilities, and high-quality large-scale nutritional annotations are scarce.
AI.method: (1) Construct CalorieBench-80K with curated calorie labels, dietary advice, and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotations. (2) Propose Food-R1 trained via multi-task learning: CoT-based cold-start instruction tuning + reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) using GRPO.
AI.result: Food-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines on CalorieBench-80K and representative benchmarks across food-related tasks.
AI.conclusion: CoT-based cold-start tuning combined with GRPO-based RFT effectively improves reasoning and performance for food VLMs, and the proposed benchmark fills the gap in large-scale nutritional annotations with reasoning chains.

title: VentAgent: When LLMs Learn to Breathe -- Multi-Objective Arbitration for ARDS Ventilation
summary: Mechanical ventilation for Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) requires balancing competing physiological goals, including oxygenation, lung protection, and acid-base homeostasis. However, current data-driven methods, especially those imitating retrospective Electronic Health Records (EHR), often suffer from imitation bias. They may capture superficial correlations from inconsistent clinical demonstrations, such as associating passive ventilator settings with survival because such settings are common in stable patients, and thus fail to generalize to volatile or out-of-distribution phenotypes. Standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods also struggle with the adversarial trade-offs of critical care and often produce opaque policies with limited clinical interpretability. To address these limitations, we introduce VentAgent, a hierarchical framework in which Large Language Models (LLMs) act as transparent arbitrators for mechanical ventilation. We reformulate ventilation control as a dynamic Multi-Objective Arbitration process rather than single-objective optimization. VentAgent decomposes decision-making into three interpretable stages: Perception, Planning, and Orchestration. By leveraging the semantic reasoning capabilities of LLMs, it synthesizes strategies from heterogeneous experts and resolves conflicting clinical priorities through an explicit coordination mechanism. Evaluations on a high-fidelity physiological simulator show that VentAgent outperforms state-of-the-art RL and classical control baselines. Moreover, it converts control decisions into human-readable reasoning chains, offering a safer, more interpretable, and adaptable paradigm for critical care automation.

AI.tldr: VentAgent is a hierarchical LLM-based framework for mechanical ventilation in ARDS that uses multi-objective arbitration across three interpretable stages (Perception, Planning, Orchestration) to outperform RL and classical control baselines while providing human-readable reasoning chains.
AI.motivation: Current data-driven methods for mechanical ventilation suffer from imitation bias by capturing superficial correlations from inconsistent EHR data, and standard RL methods struggle with adversarial trade-offs in critical care while producing opaque, uninterpretable policies.
AI.method: VentAgent is a hierarchical framework using LLMs as transparent arbitrators, reformulating ventilation control as dynamic multi-objective arbitration with three interpretable stages: Perception, Planning, and Orchestration, leveraging semantic reasoning to synthesize strategies from heterogeneous experts.
AI.result: VentAgent outperforms state-of-the-art RL and classical control baselines on a high-fidelity physiological simulator, converting control decisions into human-readable reasoning chains.
AI.conclusion: VentAgent offers a safer, more interpretable, and adaptable paradigm for critical care automation by addressing imitation bias and providing transparent decision-making through LLM-based arbitration.

title: Multi-Camera AR Guidance System for Surgical Instrument Handling and Assembly: Investigating Workload and Efficiency
summary:
The handling and assembly of instruments during surgery imposes high cognitive demands on scrub nurses, particularly when instruments are unfamiliar. We present a supporting guidance system for surgical instrumentation that combines multi-camera 6D pose estimation with augmented reality in-situ visualization on a head-mounted display without the requirement for additional markers. Pose estimation and consecutive camera calibration are achieved through known objects. The 6D pose estimation network is trained purely on synthetic data, aiming for better generalizability and real-world applicability. The AR guidance displays tooltip localization cues and step-wise assembly animations. Via gaze-based selection and a foot pedal, users can switch between assembly steps in intraoperative use.
  In a technical evaluation, our approach outperforms state-of-art 6D pose estimation. A user study with 29 scrub nurses was conducted in a surgical simulation of knee arthroplasty, comparing the system against a paper manual. AR guidance significantly reduced the perceived workload compared. Objectively, AR guidance reduced task completion time by 21.3\% (4.76 minutes). Specifically, scrub nurses less experienced with the instrument set benefited when using the system. Error frequencies were comparable between conditions. Qualitative feedback highlighted improved process clarity, reduced information overload, and perceived independence. To summarize, our marker-free multi-camera AR guidance approach for surgical instruments can, subjectively and objectively, improve intraoperative instrumentation performance, particularly for untrained scrub nurses.

AI.tldr: A marker-free AR guidance system using multi-camera 6D pose estimation and head-mounted display helps scrub nurses assemble surgical instruments, reducing workload and task completion time by 21.3% in a knee arthroplasty simulation study with 29 participants.
AI.motivation: Handling unfamiliar surgical instruments imposes high cognitive demands on scrub nurses, and existing guidance methods (e.g., paper manuals) are insufficient for reducing this cognitive load.
AI.method: Multi-camera 6D pose estimation (trained on synthetic data) + AR in-situ visualization on a head-mounted display + gaze-based selection and foot pedal interaction for step-wise assembly guidance.
AI.result: Outperformed state-of-art 6D pose estimation technically; user study showed AR guidance reduced perceived workload and task completion time by 21.3% (4.76 minutes), especially benefiting less experienced scrub nurses, with comparable error rates to paper manuals.
AI.conclusion: The marker-free multi-camera AR guidance approach can subjectively and objectively improve intraoperative instrumentation performance, particularly for untrained scrub nurses.

title: Explainably Safe Reinforcement Learning
summary: Trust in a decision-making system requires both safety guarantees and the ability to interpret and understand its behavior. This is particularly important for learned systems, whose decision-making processes are often highly opaque. Shielding is a prominent model-based technique for enforcing safety in reinforcement learning. However, because shields are automatically synthesized using rigorous formal methods, their decisions are often similarly difficult for humans to interpret. Recently, decision trees became customary to represent controllers and policies. However, since shields are inherently non-deterministic, their decision tree representations become too large to be explainable in practice. To address this challenge, we propose a novel approach for explainable safe RL that enhances trust by providing human-interpretable explanations of the shield's decisions. Our method represents the shielding policy as a hierarchy of decision trees, offering top-down, case-based explanations. At design time, we use a world model to analyze the safety risks of executing actions in given states. Based on this analysis, we construct both the shield and a high-level decision tree that classifies states into risk categories (safe, critical, dangerous, unsafe), explaining why a situation may be safety-critical. At runtime, we generate localized decision trees that explain which actions are allowed and why others are deemed unsafe. Our method facilitates explainability of the safety aspect in safe-by-shielding reinforcement learning, requires no additional information beyond what is already used for shielding, incurs minimal overhead, and integrates readily into existing shielded RL pipelines. In our experiments, we compute explanations using decision trees that are several orders of magnitude smaller than the original shield.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method for explainable safe reinforcement learning by representing shielding policies as hierarchical decision trees, providing human-interpretable explanations of safety decisions while maintaining minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Trust in decision-making systems requires both safety guarantees and interpretability. Shielding is effective for safety in RL but its decisions are opaque and non-deterministic, making standard decision tree representations too large to be explainable in practice.
AI.method: The method uses a world model to analyze safety risks at design time, constructing a shield and a high-level decision tree classifying states into risk categories (safe, critical, dangerous, unsafe). At runtime, localized decision trees explain which actions are allowed and why others are unsafe.
AI.result: The computed explanations use decision trees several orders of magnitude smaller than the original shield, demonstrating efficient and human-interpretable explanations.
AI.conclusion: The approach facilitates explainability of safety in safe-by-shielding RL, requires no extra information beyond what is already used, incurs minimal overhead, and integrates readily into existing shielded RL pipelines.

title: M$^3$Eval: Multi-Modal Memory Evaluation through Cognitively-Grounded Video Tasks
comment: We present an evaluation designed for multi-modal memory in multi-modal models
summary: As multi-modal models advance towards long-form video understanding, memory emerges as a critical capability. Despite substantial efforts in developing video datasets and benchmarks, existing works primarily focus on perception and reasoning, without systematically evaluating memory: what models retain, how faithfully information is preserved, and how robust memory remains under interference. To address this gap, we introduce M$^3$Eval, the first comprehensive evaluation framework and benchmark for probing different memory dimensions in multi-modal models. Grounded in cognitive psychology, our design features carefully constructed tasks that isolate key aspects of memory. Leveraging M$^3$Eval, we conduct extensive experiments across representative multi-modal models, revealing consistent weaknesses and distinctive behaviors. We find that models struggle to maintain disentangled representations when processing parallel video streams, exhibit interference patterns differing substantially from those observed in human memory, ground memory sources more reliably in the spatial domain than the temporal domain, and demonstrate limited symbolic memory. Collectively, our benchmark provides a valuable resource for future research, while our findings highlight memory as a fundamental yet underexplored capability and offer insights for designing more effective memory mechanisms in multi-modal models. Our code and dataset are available at https://pku-value-lab.github.io/m3eval-homepage.

AI.tldr: M$^3$Eval is the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating memory capabilities in multi-modal models, revealing that current models struggle with disentangled representations, exhibit unusual interference patterns, ground spatial memory better than temporal memory, and have limited symbolic memory.
AI.motivation: Existing video datasets and benchmarks focus on perception and reasoning, but systematically evaluate memory—what models retain, how faithfully information is preserved, and how robust memory is under interference—remains overlooked.
AI.method: The authors introduce M$^3$Eval, a benchmark grounded in cognitive psychology with carefully constructed tasks that isolate key aspects of memory (e.g., parallel streams, interference, spatial vs. temporal grounding, symbolic memory) for probing multi-modal models.
AI.result: Experiments reveal that: (1) models struggle to maintain disentangled representations in parallel video streams; (2) interference patterns differ substantially from human memory; (3) memory grounding is more reliable in spatial than temporal domains; (4) symbolic memory is limited.
AI.conclusion: Memory is a fundamental yet underexplored capability in multi-modal models; M$^3$Eval provides a valuable resource and insights for designing more effective memory mechanisms.

title: ALINC: Active Learning for Inductive Node Classification via Graph Sampling
summary: Active learning (AL) for node classification typically focuses on selecting the most informative nodes for annotation within one or a few large graphs (e.g., in social network analysis). However, in other domains, such as molecular chemistry or electronic design automation, datasets consist of thousands of independent graphs. In many of these inductive settings, annotating an individual node requires a full-graph analysis, which effectively yields the remaining node labels on-the-fly. Therefore, these scenarios require AL strategies that select entire graphs instead of single nodes, a problem which has not been tackled in the literature so far. Thus, we introduce ALINC, an AL framework for inductive node classification via graph sampling. It bridges the existing methodological gap by elevating node-level utility measures to graph-level selection criteria through various aggregation mechanisms. In an extensive benchmark including ten strategies, three aggregation methods, and four datasets, we identify CoreSet, TypiClust, and BADGE as the top-performing graph sampling strategies. Our detailed analysis further reveals that the choice of the aggregation method is pivotal, as it substantially affects model performance and annotation costs. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of ALINC in two use case studies: site-of-metabolism prediction in molecules and design automation of printed circuit board schematics.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ALINC, an active learning framework for inductive node classification that selects whole graphs rather than individual nodes for annotation. It bridges a gap in existing AL literature by aggregating node-level utility measures to graph-level selection criteria. Experiments show CoreSet, TypiClust, and BADGE as top strategies, with aggregation method choice being critical for performance.
AI.motivation: Existing active learning for node classification focuses on selecting individual nodes within a few large graphs (e.g., social networks). However, in domains like molecular chemistry or EDA, datasets contain thousands of independent graphs, and annotating a single node requires full-graph analysis, yielding all other node labels on-the-fly. No prior work addresses graph-level selection strategies for this inductive setting.
AI.method: ALINC (Active Learning for INductive Node Classification) elevates node-level utility measures to graph-level selection criteria via various aggregation mechanisms. It enables graph sampling strategies that select entire graphs instead of individual nodes, bridging the gap between node-level AL and graph-level selection in inductive settings.
AI.result: In benchmarking across ten strategies, three aggregation methods, and four datasets, CoreSet, TypiClust, and BADGE emerge as top-performing graph sampling strategies. The choice of aggregation method significantly impacts both model performance and annotation costs.
AI.conclusion: ALINC effectively addresses the gap in active learning for inductive node classification by enabling graph-level selection. The framework's effectiveness is demonstrated in two real-world use cases: site-of-metabolism prediction in molecules and design automation of printed circuit board schematics.

title: Why Muon Outperforms Adam: A Curvature Perspective
summary: Muon improves training efficiency over Adam in large language-model training by about two times, but the local geometric source of this advantage remains unclear. Our work takes a first step toward demystifying Muon's superiority over Adam from a curvature perspective. First, we apply a second-order Taylor approximation to the training landscape and show that Muon achieves a larger one-step loss decrease than Adam at matched validation loss. The two optimizers have comparable first-order gains, but Muon consistently incurs a smaller second-order curvature penalty. Second, we decompose this curvature penalty into the squared update norm and Normalized Directional Sharpness (NDS). We find that Muon and Adam have comparable update norms, so Muon's smaller curvature penalty is driven by lower NDS, not update scale. Third, we study how training data and model structure shape Muon's NDS advantage. Using Zipf-Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) data with controlled imbalance, we show that data imbalance amplifies Muon's NDS advantage over Adam. A within-/cross-layer decomposition further shows that, in the middle and late stages of training, Muon's lower NDS is mainly sustained by smaller within-layer curvature. Beyond empirical evidence, we analyze stylized quadratic problems with heterogeneous curvature and gradient alignment toward high-curvature modes. We prove that Muon attains a smaller average NDS than GD by balancing update energy across curvature groups; when curvature heterogeneity is sufficiently strong, this also yields lower local quadratic loss after the same number of steps.

AI.tldr: Muon achieves about 2x training efficiency over Adam in LLM training. This paper demystifies Muon's advantage from a curvature perspective: Muon incurs smaller curvature penalty (lower Normalized Directional Sharpness) than Adam, especially under data imbalance, and this advantage is sustained by smaller within-layer curvature in middle/late training stages.
AI.motivation: Muon improves training efficiency over Adam by ~2x in large language model training, but the local geometric source of this advantage is unclear. This paper aims to demystify Muon's superiority from a curvature perspective.
AI.method: 1) Apply second-order Taylor approximation to training landscape to compare one-step loss decrease between Muon and Adam. 2) Decompose curvature penalty into squared update norm and Normalized Directional Sharpness (NDS). 3) Use Zipf-PCFG data with controlled imbalance to study data effects. 4) Perform within-/cross-layer decomposition for curvature analysis. 5) Analyze stylized quadratic problems theoretically.
AI.result: Muon achieves larger one-step loss decrease than Adam at matched validation loss. Both have comparable first-order gains, but Muon consistently has smaller second-order curvature penalty. This is driven by lower NDS (not update norm). Data imbalance amplifies Muon's NDS advantage. In middle/late training, Muon's lower NDS is mainly from smaller within-layer curvature. Theoretical analysis proves Muon attains smaller average NDS than GD by balancing update energy across curvature groups.
AI.conclusion: Muon's training efficiency advantage over Adam stems from its lower Normalized Directional Sharpness (NDS), which leads to smaller curvature penalty. This advantage is amplified by data imbalance and sustained by smaller within-layer curvature in later training stages. Theoretical results confirm Muon balances update energy across curvature groups more effectively than gradient descent.

title: Handwriting Extraction and Analysis of Signature Lists in Swiss Popular Initiatives
summary: Popular initiatives and referendums are central to Swiss democracy, yet the validation of handwritten signature lists remains a labor-intensive manual process. This paper investigates the potential of automated document analysis methods, including OCR and AI-based handwriting analysis, to support this task. We propose a pipeline combining template-based line segmentation with text recognition and writer retrieval techniques, evaluated on a dataset of 443 handwritten entries from 418 writers. Results show that OCR struggles with out-of-vocabulary handwriting, with a CER of 29.6% for first names. In contrast, writer retrieval performs more robustly, reaching an mAP of 50.6%. Furthermore, our experiments indicate that off-the-shelf OCR systems are not sufficiently reliable for transcription of handwritten signature data, particularly for short, out-of-vocabulary entries such as names or addresses. However, writer retrieval methods can effectively identify visually similar entries across signature lists, making them a suitable tool for supporting the detection of potential duplicate submissions based on handwriting similarity.

AI.tldr: This paper explores automated analysis of Swiss initiative/referendum signature lists using OCR and writer retrieval. OCR achieved 29.6% CER on first names, while writer retrieval reached 50.6% mAP, proving more effective for detecting potential duplicate submissions.
AI.motivation: Manual validation of handwritten signature lists for Swiss popular initiatives and referendums is labor-intensive, motivating the need for automated document analysis to support this task.
AI.method: A pipeline combining template-based line segmentation with OCR and AI-based handwriting analysis (writer retrieval) was proposed and evaluated on 443 handwritten entries from 418 writers.
AI.result: OCR achieved 29.6% CER for first names, while writer retrieval reached 50.6% mAP. Off-the-shelf OCR was unreliable for short, out-of-vocabulary entries like names/addresses.
AI.conclusion: Writer retrieval methods are more suitable than OCR for supporting duplicate submission detection based on handwriting similarity in Swiss signature lists.

title: Towards Accurate Model Selection in Deep Unsupervised Domain Adaptation
summary: Deep unsupervised domain adaptation (Deep UDA) methods successfully leverage rich labeled data in a source domain to boost the performance on related but unlabeled data in a target domain. However, algorithm comparison is cumbersome in Deep UDA due to the absence of accurate and standardized model selection method, posing an obstacle to further advances in the field. Existing model selection methods for Deep UDA are either highly biased, restricted, unstable, or even controversial (requiring labeled target data). To this end, we propose \textit{Deep Embedded Validation} (\textbf{DEV}), which embeds adapted feature representation into the validation procedure to obtain unbiased estimation of the target risk with bounded variance. The variance is further reduced by the technique of control variate. The efficacy of the method has been justified both theoretically and empirically.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Deep Embedded Validation (DEV), a novel model selection method for Deep Unsupervised Domain Adaptation that provides unbiased, low-variance estimation of target risk without requiring labeled target data.
AI.motivation: Algorithm comparison in Deep UDA is difficult due to the lack of accurate and standardized model selection methods. Existing approaches are biased, restricted, unstable, or controversial (requiring labeled target data).
AI.method: DEV embeds adapted feature representation into the validation procedure to obtain unbiased estimation of target risk with bounded variance, further reducing variance using control variate technique.
AI.result: The method is justified both theoretically (bounded variance, unbiasedness) and empirically (efficacy demonstrated in experiments).
AI.conclusion: DEV provides a reliable, unbiased, and low-variance model selection method for Deep UDA without needing labeled target data, addressing a key bottleneck in the field.

title: MetaPoint: Unlocking Precise Spatial Control in Agentic Visual Generation
summary: Generative visual models fundamentally struggle with precise spatial control. This arises from a core disconnect: models can process textual descriptions of space but cannot directly map numerical coordinates onto the 2D image canvas. We introduce MetaPoint, a method that bridges this gap by representing a continuous 2D coordinate as a single, special token. Crucially, MetaPoint requires no new architectural components; it directly leverages the model's inherent positional encoding schemes to interpret these coordinates, treating our token as a virtual point on the canvas. This lightweight approach enables pixel-level control of an object's position with one token or its bounding box with two, all without requiring architectural changes or bespoke attention masking. The MetaPoint tokens are designed to be compositional, serving as spatial primitives. This allows a planner agent to decompose a high-level user request into a structured sequence of primitives for the generator. By providing a simple, precise, and scalable building block for spatial control, MetaPoint unlocks more powerful compositional generative agents and enables intuitive, interactive editing systems.

AI.tldr: MetaPoint introduces a special token representing continuous 2D coordinates to enable precise spatial control in generative visual models without architectural changes.
AI.motivation: Existing generative visual models struggle with precise spatial control because they can process textual descriptions but cannot directly map numerical coordinates onto the 2D image canvas.
AI.method: MetaPoint represents a continuous 2D coordinate as a single special token, leveraging the model's inherent positional encoding schemes to interpret coordinates as virtual points on the canvas, requiring no new architectural components.
AI.result: The method enables pixel-level control of object position with one token or bounding box with two tokens, and supports compositional spatial primitives for decomposing high-level user requests.
AI.conclusion: MetaPoint provides a simple, precise, and scalable building block for spatial control, enabling compositional generative agents and intuitive interactive editing systems.

title: Learning Long Range Spatio-Temporal Representations over Continuous Time Dynamic Graphs with State Space Models
summary:
Continuous-time dynamic graphs (CTDGs) provide a richer framework to capture fine-grained temporal patterns in evolving relational data. Long-range information propagation is a key challenge while learning representations, wherein it is important to retain and update information over long temporal horizons. Existing approaches restrict models to capture one-hop or local temporal neighborhoods and fail to capture multi-hop or global structural patterns. To mitigate this, we derive a parameter-efficient state-space modeling framework for continuous-time dynamic graphs (CTDG-SSM) from first principles. We first introduce continuous-time Topology-Aware higher order polynomial projection operator (CTT-HiPPO), a novel memory-based reformulation of HiPPO to jointly encode temporal dynamics and graph structure. The solution from CTT-HiPPO is obtained by projecting the classical HiPPO solution through a polynomial of the Laplacian matrix, yielding topology-aware memory updates that admit an equivalent state-space formulation for CTDGs (CTDG-SSM). Then a computationally efficient discrete formulation is obtained using the zero-order hold approach for model implementation.
  Across benchmarks on dynamic link prediction, dynamic node classification, and sequence classification, CTDG-SSM achieves state-of-the-art performance. Notably, it achieves large performance gains on datasets that require long range temporal (LRT) and spatial reasoning.

AI.tldr: CTDG-SSM is a state-space model for continuous-time dynamic graphs that captures both temporal dynamics and graph structure via a novel CTT-HiPPO operator, achieving state-of-the-art on link prediction, node classification, and sequence classification tasks, especially on long-range reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing CTDG methods capture only one-hop/local temporal neighborhoods and fail to capture multi-hop or global structural patterns, limiting their ability to handle long-range information propagation over temporal horizons.
AI.method: Derives a state-space model (CTDG-SSM) from first principles by introducing a Continuous-Time Topology-Aware HiPPO (CTT-HiPPO) operator, which projects classical HiPPO through a polynomial of the Laplacian matrix for topology-aware memory updates, then discretized via zero-order hold for efficient computation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across dynamic link prediction, dynamic node classification, and sequence classification benchmarks, with particularly large gains on datasets requiring long-range temporal and spatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed topology-aware state-space formulation effectively addresses long-range information propagation in CTDGs, outperforming existing approaches on tasks requiring both temporal and structural reasoning.

title: Anchor3R: Streaming 3D Reconstruction with Transient Anchors for Long-Horizon Visual Mapping
summary: Long-horizon online visual mapping is a core capability for robot perception, requiring continuous camera-motion and scene-geometry estimation from visual streams under bounded memory and computation. Recent feed-forward 3D reconstruction models provide strong geometric priors, but their streaming variants often predict poses in a fixed coordinate system tied to the first frame or a persistent scene memory. This fixed-gauge design leads to train--test mismatch, attention bias toward early anchors, and accumulated drift on sequences much longer than those seen during training. We propose \emph{Anchor3R}, a streaming 3D reconstruction framework that treats feed-forward reconstruction as current-centric local measurement prediction rather than persistent global-gauge regression. At each time step, Anchor3R predicts window-relative poses and a local pointmap in the current-frame coordinate system, turning streaming reconstruction into relative-pose measurement generation. These measurements support online pose updates, while loop-closure reinsertion and motion averaging align the trajectory and transform local pointmaps into a coherent global reconstruction. Experiments on indoor, outdoor, driving, and RGB-D benchmarks show that Anchor3R improves long-horizon pose accuracy and dense reconstruction quality over existing streaming baselines, while supporting bounded-memory online inference.

AI.tldr: Anchor3R is a streaming 3D reconstruction framework that treats feed-forward reconstruction as current-centric local measurement prediction rather than persistent global-gauge regression, improving long-horizon pose accuracy and dense reconstruction quality under bounded memory.
AI.motivation: Existing streaming 3D reconstruction models use fixed-gauge designs (tied to first frame or persistent memory), causing train-test mismatch, attention bias toward early anchors, and accumulated drift on long sequences.
AI.method: Predicts window-relative poses and local pointmaps in the current-frame coordinate system at each step, treating reconstruction as relative-pose measurement generation, then uses online pose updates, loop-closure reinsertion, and motion averaging to align trajectory into global reconstruction.
AI.result: Outperforms existing streaming baselines on indoor, outdoor, driving, and RGB-D benchmarks for long-horizon pose accuracy and dense reconstruction quality with bounded-memory online inference.
AI.conclusion: Anchor3R effectively addresses the fixed-gauge limitations in streaming 3D reconstruction by adopting a current-centric local measurement paradigm, enabling robust long-horizon online visual mapping.

title: Test-Time Compute Scaling for ASR with Depth-Conditioned Looped Transformers
summary: End-to-end ASR systems typically use fixed-depth acoustic encoders at inference, making it difficult to trade additional test-time computation for improved recognition without training a larger model. A natural approach is to reuse a shared Transformer block recurrently, but we find that naive looping does not fully exploit additional recurrent compute. We introduce LARM, a depth-conditioned looped Transformer that turns recurrent encoder depth into a controllable test-time compute axis. LARM combines sparse CTC checkpoints, supervision-clock embeddings, FiLM depth conditioning, and delayed soft-posterior feedback. These components structure the loop into recognition checkpoints separated by latent refinement phases and allow shared weights to specialize across recurrent steps. On LibriSpeech, LARM improves WER as the number of inference loops increases and achieves performance competitive with deeper unshared-parameter baselines. Our results show that test-time compute scaling can extend beyond autoregressive language-model reasoning to continuous non-autoregressive speech recognition.

AI.tldr: LARM introduces a depth-conditioned looped Transformer for end-to-end ASR that enables controllable test-time compute scaling by reusing shared weights across recurrent steps, achieving improved WER on LibriSpeech with more inference loops.
AI.motivation: Fixed-depth acoustic encoders in end-to-end ASR systems cannot trade additional test-time computation for improved recognition without training larger models. The authors aim to enable controllable test-time compute scaling in ASR.
AI.method: LARM reuses a shared Transformer block recurrently with depth conditioning, combining sparse CTC checkpoints, supervision-clock embeddings, FiLM depth conditioning, and delayed soft-posterior feedback to structure the loop into recognition checkpoints separated by latent refinement phases.
AI.result: On LibriSpeech, LARM improves WER as the number of inference loops increases and achieves performance competitive with deeper unshared-parameter baselines.
AI.conclusion: Test-time compute scaling can extend beyond autoregressive language-model reasoning to continuous non-autoregressive speech recognition.

title: UniCAD: A Unified Benchmark and Universal Model for Multi-Modal Multi-Task CAD
summary: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) underpins modern engineering and manufacturing by enabling the creation of precise, editable 3D models. However, CAD research typically studies tasks in isolation, and multi-modal, multi-task learning for CAD is hindered by the absence of a unified benchmark. To address this gap, we introduce UniCAD, a comprehensive benchmark for multi-modal CAD learning that covers point-to-CAD reconstruction, text/image-to-CAD generation, and CAD question answering across diverse input modalities. Alongside the benchmark, we present UniCAD-MLLM, a universal multi-modal large language model that ingests text, images, sketches, and point clouds and performs these heterogeneous tasks in an end-to-end fashion within a single framework. Extensive experiments on the UniCAD and Fusion360 benchmarks demonstrate that UniCAD-MLLM achieves state-of-the-art performance across all tasks, outperforming existing task-specific and multi-task baselines. We will release the dataset, code, and pretrained models to accelerate future research.

AI.tldr: UniCAD is a new comprehensive benchmark for multi-modal CAD learning (reconstruction, generation, QA), with UniCAD-MLLM as a unified multi-modal LLM achieving SOTA across tasks.
AI.motivation: CAD research lacks a unified benchmark for multi-modal, multi-task learning, as existing studies treat tasks in isolation.
AI.method: They introduce UniCAD (a comprehensive benchmark covering reconstruction, generation, QA) and UniCAD-MLLM (a universal multi-modal LLM handling text, images, sketches, and point clouds in one framework).
AI.result: UniCAD-MLLM achieves state-of-the-art performance on UniCAD and Fusion360 benchmarks across all tasks, outperforming task-specific and multi-task baselines.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark and model provide a unified foundation for future multi-modal CAD research, with public release of dataset, code, and models.

title: Cone-Compatible Monge Geometry for High-Dimensional Ordered Optimal Transport
summary: High-dimensional optimal transport is seldom available in closed form. The one-dimensional case is exceptional because the order of the real line is compatible with convex transport costs, making monotone rearrangement optimal. This paper studies when an analogous Monge structure can be recovered in higher dimensions from a partial order. We introduce a cone-compatible Monge geometry: a closed convex cone (K) induces the order (x\preceq_K y) whenever (y-x\in K), and is compatible with a cost if ordered pairs satisfy a Monge exchange inequality. For squared Mahalanobis costs (c_M(x,y)=(x-y)^\top M(x-y)), we prove a sharp characterization: compatibility holds exactly when (K) is acute under the (M)-inner product, namely (u^\top Mv\ge0) for all (u,v\in K), equivalently (K\subseteq K_M^*). Under this condition, measures supported on cone chains admit a quantile-type closed-form optimal coupling, yielding exact transport under the original ground cost rather than after projection or metric replacement. We distinguish the resulting cone-chain Wasserstein metric on canonically ordered chain distributions from an extended directed cone transport cost on general measures, and develop feasibility, duality, stability, approximation, Gaussian recovery, statistical, and computational results. The theory is complementary to sliced and tree Wasserstein distances: it is not a universal fast surrogate, but a way to obtain interpretable, direction-valid, original-space monotone transport for ordered high-dimensional data.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces cone-compatible Monge geometry for high-dimensional optimal transport, showing that for squared Mahalanobis costs, compatibility holds exactly when the cone is acute under the M-inner product, enabling closed-form optimal transport for measures supported on cone chains.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional optimal transport rarely has closed-form solutions, unlike the one-dimensional case where monotone rearrangement works. The authors aim to recover an analogous Monge structure in higher dimensions using partial orders induced by convex cones.
AI.method: The paper defines cone-compatible Monge geometry via a closed convex cone K inducing a partial order, with compatibility determined by a Monge exchange inequality. For squared Mahalanobis costs, they prove compatibility iff K is acute under the M-inner product. They develop cone-chain Wasserstein metrics, feasibility, duality, stability, approximation, and computational results.
AI.result: Sharp characterization: cone-cost compatibility holds iff uᵀMv ≥ 0 for all u,v ∈ K (equivalently K ⊆ K_M^*). Under this condition, measures on cone chains admit closed-form optimal coupling via quantile-type transport, yielding exact transport under the original ground cost.
AI.conclusion: The theory complements sliced and tree Wasserstein distances—not as a universal fast surrogate, but as a way to obtain interpretable, direction-valid, original-space monotone transport for ordered high-dimensional data.

title: MaCo-GAN: Manifold-Contrastive Adversarial Learning for Single Image Super-Resolution
summary: Conventional Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) for Single Image Super-Resolution (SISR) often struggle with hallucinated artifacts, largely because standard discriminators evaluate overall image naturalness rather than strict conditional realism. To address this, we propose MaCo-GAN, a novel manifold-contrastive GAN framework that replaces the conventional adversarial loss with a supervised contrastive objective. A core component of our method is a dynamic fake sample synthesizer that transforms ground truth (GT) data into a spectrum of challenging, perceptually plausible fake images that strictly maintain low-resolution (LR) correspondence. Utilizing these synthesized samples, we establish a robust contrastive minimax game: the generator is trained to attract its predictions toward on-manifold fakes (low distortion) and repel them from off-manifold fakes (high distortion), while the discriminator optimizes the exact opposite. By simply replacing the adversarial loss of a baseline SR model with our proposed objective, we demonstrate consistent improvements in the perception-distortion trade-off across various benchmarks. Extensive ablation studies validate the effectiveness of our framework and provide deep insights into the dynamics of this conditional contrastive game.

AI.tldr: MaCo-GAN replaces adversarial loss in GAN-based super-resolution with a manifold-contrastive objective, using a dynamic fake sample synthesizer to create perceptually plausible fakes, achieving improved perception-distortion trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Conventional GANs for SISR produce hallucinated artifacts because standard discriminators evaluate overall naturalness rather than strict conditional realism (LR correspondence).
AI.method: Proposes a manifold-contrastive GAN framework with: (1) a dynamic fake sample synthesizer that transforms GT into challenging, perceptually plausible fakes maintaining LR correspondence; (2) a contrastive minimax game where generator attracts predictions toward on-manifold fakes (low distortion) and repels from off-manifold fakes (high distortion), while discriminator does the opposite.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in perception-distortion trade-off across various benchmarks by simply replacing the adversarial loss of a baseline SR model with the proposed objective. Extensive ablation studies validate effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: MaCo-GAN effectively addresses hallucination artifacts in GAN-based SISR via conditional contrastive learning, offering a plug-and-play alternative to standard adversarial loss.

title: Graph-Guided Universum Learning in Generalized Eigenvalue Proximal SVMs for Alzheimer's Disease Classification
summary: Early and accurate detection of Alzheimer's disease (AD) is important for timely intervention and disease management. Generalized Eigenvalue Proximal Support Vector Machine (GEPSVM) and its Universum-based variants have shown promising results for AD classification. However, existing methods treat Universum samples as independent points and do not consider the geometric relationships among them. This paper proposes two graph-guided Universum learning models, namely UG-GEPSVM and IUG-GEPSVM, for AD versus cognitively normal (CN) classification using structural MRI data. In the proposed framework, mild cognitive impairment (MCI) subjects are used as Universum data to provide intermediate information between AD and CN classes. A graph is constructed over the Universum samples using Gaussian similarity, Minimum Spanning Tree connectivity, and multi-hop propagation. From this graph, a Laplacian matrix is derived that captures the geometric structure of the MCI samples. This Laplacian-based regularization is incorporated into the learning process in place of the conventional independent Universum penalty term. UG-GEPSVM integrates this regularization into the generalized eigenvalue formulation, while IUG-GEPSVM extends the numerically stable improved GEPSVM framework using a standard eigenvalue formulation. Experiments on ADNI MRI dataset variants using ICA- and PCA-based features at five different noise levels show that both proposed models consistently outperform existing GEPSVM and Universum-based methods. UG-GEPSVM achieves the highest average AUC of 88.07% and maintains stable performance under increasing noise levels. Statistical tests further confirm the significance of the observed improvements.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes two graph-guided Universum learning models (UG-GEPSVM and IUG-GEPSVM) for Alzheimer's disease classification, which leverage MCI subjects as Universum data and incorporate graph-based geometric structure regularization instead of treating Universum samples independently. The models outperform existing methods on ADNI MRI data.
AI.motivation: Existing GEPSVM and Universum-based methods for AD classification treat Universum samples as independent points and ignore the geometric relationships among them, limiting their performance.
AI.method: Two graph-guided Universum GEPSVM models (UG-GEPSVM and IUG-GEPSVM) that construct a graph over MCI Universum samples using Gaussian similarity, Minimum Spanning Tree connectivity, and multi-hop propagation, then incorporate Laplacian-based regularization into the learning framework.
AI.result: On ADNI MRI data with ICA/PCA features at five noise levels, both proposed models consistently outperform existing GEPSVM and Universum-based methods. UG-GEPSVM achieves the highest average AUC of 88.07% with stable performance under increasing noise.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating geometric structure of Universum data via graph-guided regularization significantly improves AD classification performance and robustness.

title: InstantRetouch: Efficient and High-Fidelity Instruction-Guided Image Retouching with Bilateral Space
summary: Language-guided photo retouching aims to adjust color and tone while preserving geometry and texture. Recently, diffusion-based retouching shows a superior visual quality, but often struggles with both fidelity issues due to its generative nature and efficiency because of its iterative sampling process. In this work, we propose an efficient and fidelity-preserving retouching method using bilateral space manipulation, which is both compact and content-decoupled. Specifically, instead of directly editing pixels or image latents, our model predicts a low-resolution bilateral grid of affine transforms, which are sliced using a learned guidance map and then applied to the full-resolution image. This approach yields both high fidelity and improved efficiency. To retain strong priors of a pretrained generative model, we distill a multi-step diffusion model into our bilateral grid framework using Variational Score Distillation, complemented by a prompt alignment loss to guide instruction-following behavior. Additionally, we introduce a new benchmark and evaluate our method across multiple dimensions: fidelity, instruction following, and efficiency. Compared to the latest retouch methods, like Gemini-2.5-Flash (Nano-Banana), our method can avoid content drift, significantly improve latency, and generate visually pleasing edits, while maintaining a high level of fidelity. Project page: https://openimaginglab.github.io/InstantRetouch/.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes InstantRetouch, an efficient and fidelity-preserving photo retouching method that uses bilateral space manipulation (predicting a low-resolution bilateral grid of affine transforms) instead of direct pixel/latent editing, distilled from a pretrained diffusion model via Variational Score Distillation to maintain strong priors while avoiding content drift and improving latency.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based photo retouching methods suffer from fidelity issues due to their generative nature and efficiency problems due to iterative sampling. The goal is to achieve both high fidelity and real-time efficiency while following language instructions.
AI.method: The model predicts a low-resolution bilateral grid of affine transforms, which are sliced using a learned guidance map and applied to the full-resolution image. A multi-step diffusion model is distilled into this bilateral grid framework using Variational Score Distillation with a prompt alignment loss for instruction-following.
AI.result: The method avoids content drift, significantly improves latency, and generates visually pleasing edits with high fidelity compared to latest methods like Gemini-2.5-Flash (Nano-Banana).
AI.conclusion: Bilateral space manipulation enables efficient and fidelity-preserving language-guided photo retouching, outperforming diffusion-based approaches in both speed and content preservation while maintaining visual quality.

title: Contrastive Learning and Correlation Clustering for Sequences of Network Telescope Data
summary: Understanding activities of Internet scanners is challenging; it often requires identifying relationships between sources, a task for which semantic annotations are scarce. This work investigates whether semantically meaningful pairwise relationships between sequences of network flow records can be estimated by contrastive learning, without pretraining and without annotations. To this end, we propose a transformer model that embeds minimally preprocessed sequences of network flow records and train it using contrastive learning. With the similarities obtained from this model, we state a correlation clustering problem and solve it locally. Experimentally, we show: Learned similarities are higher on average for sequences originating from the same source than for sequences originating from different sources, and this property generalizes to unseen sequences of unseen sources. Moreover, correlation clustering yields clusters consistent with scanner labels. The complete source code of the algorithms and for reproducing the experiments is publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper uses contrastive learning and a transformer model to embed network flow sequences and cluster Internet scanners without annotations, achieving meaningful groupings that align with scanner labels.
AI.motivation: Understanding Internet scanner activities is challenging due to the difficulty of identifying relationships between sources and the scarcity of semantic annotations.
AI.method: A transformer model embeds minimally preprocessed sequences of network flow records, trained via contrastive learning. The resulting similarities are used to formulate and solve a correlation clustering problem locally.
AI.result: Learned similarities are higher for same-source sequences than different-source sequences, generalizing to unseen sources. Correlation clustering produces clusters consistent with scanner labels.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive learning without pretraining or annotations can effectively embed network flow sequences and identify meaningful scanner relationships, with publicly available source code for reproducibility.

title: ZipSplat: Fewer Gaussians, Better Splats
summary: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting methods reconstruct a scene from posed or pose-free images in a single forward pass, yet current approaches predict one Gaussian per input pixel, tying the representation budget to camera resolution rather than scene complexity. A flat wall and a richly textured object thus produce equally many Gaussians despite very different geometric needs. We propose ZipSplat, a token-based feed-forward model that decouples Gaussian placement from the pixel grid. A multi-view backbone extracts dense visual tokens, and k-means clustering compresses them into a compact set of scene tokens. Cross- and self-attention refine these tokens, and a lightweight MLP decodes each into a group of Gaussians with unconstrained 3D positions. Because clustering is applied at inference, a single trained model spans the quality-efficiency curve without retraining. ZipSplat operates without ground-truth poses or intrinsics, yet sets a new state of the art on DL3DV and RealEstate10K with ${\sim}6{\times}$ fewer Gaussians than pixel-aligned methods, surpassing the best pose-free baseline by 2.1dB and 1.2dB PSNR, respectively. It further generalizes zero-shot to Mip-NeRF360 and ScanNet++, outperforming all comparable baselines. Our project page is at ${\href{https://veichta.com/zipsplat}{https://veichta.com/zipsplat}}$.

AI.tldr: ZipSplat introduces a token-based feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting method that decouples Gaussian placement from pixel grid, using k-means clustering to compress visual tokens into a compact set, achieving state-of-the-art results with ~6x fewer Gaussians than pixel-aligned methods.
AI.motivation: Current feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting methods predict one Gaussian per pixel, tying the representation budget to camera resolution rather than scene complexity, so a flat wall and a textured object produce equally many Gaussians despite different geometric needs.
AI.method: ZipSplat uses a multi-view backbone to extract dense visual tokens, applies k-means clustering to compress them into compact scene tokens, refines them with cross- and self-attention, and decodes each token into a group of Gaussians with unconstrained 3D positions via a lightweight MLP. Clustering is applied at inference, enabling a single model to span the quality-efficiency curve without retraining.
AI.result: Sets new SOTA on DL3DV and RealEstate10K with ~6x fewer Gaussians than pixel-aligned methods, surpassing the best pose-free baseline by 2.1dB and 1.2dB PSNR respectively. Generalizes zero-shot to Mip-NeRF360 and ScanNet++, outperforming all comparable baselines.
AI.conclusion: ZipSplat effectively decouples Gaussian count from pixel resolution, enabling more efficient scene representation without sacrificing quality, and operates without ground-truth poses or intrinsics.

title: Trace-Mediated Peak Bias: Bridging Temporal Credit Assignment and Cognitive Heuristics in Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Temporal credit assignment is central to both biological and artificial intelligence, yet its interaction with non-linear function approximation is poorly understood. We identify a systematic failure mode in deep reinforcement learning (RL) termed Trace-Mediated Peak Bias (TMPB). At intermediate eligibility trace depths, agents irrationally prefer trajectories with high-magnitude reward ``peaks'' over alternatives with higher cumulative returns. This provides a mechanistic account of the Peak-End Rule: a human memory bias where experiences are judged by their most intense moments rather than integrated utility. We show that TMPB emerges because traces amplify distal Temporal Difference errors into ``gradient shocks'' that fixed-step-size Stochastic Gradient Descent cannot normalize, leading to global overestimation. Conversely, adaptive optimizers mitigate this pathology via second-moment normalization. Our results suggest that human-like saliency distortions may emerge naturally from the mathematical constraints of credit assignment in distributed systems, and that adaptive optimization is a theoretical necessity for rational value estimation.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies Trace-Mediated Peak Bias (TMPB) in deep RL, where agents irrationally prefer high-magnitude reward peaks over higher cumulative returns at intermediate trace depths, providing a mechanistic account of the Peak-End Rule from human psychology. The bias arises from amplified gradient shocks that fixed-step SGD cannot normalize, while adaptive optimizers mitigate the issue.
AI.motivation: To understand the interaction between temporal credit assignment and non-linear function approximation, specifically addressing the systematic failure mode where deep RL agents exhibit biased behavior reminiscent of human cognitive biases like the Peak-End Rule.
AI.method: The authors identify a failure mode (TMPB) through analysis of eligibility traces in deep RL, showing that traces amplify distal TD errors into gradient shocks. They compare fixed-step-size SGD (which cannot normalize these shocks) with adaptive optimizers (which mitigate via second-moment normalization).
AI.result: TMPB causes agents to prefer high-magnitude reward peaks over higher cumulative returns at intermediate trace depths, leading to global overestimation due to gradient shocks that fixed-step SGD cannot handle. Adaptive optimizers effectively mitigate this pathology.
AI.conclusion: Human-like saliency distortions (Peak-End Rule) may emerge naturally from mathematical constraints of credit assignment in distributed systems, and adaptive optimization is theoretically necessary for rational value estimation.

title: Who Needs Labels? Adapting Vision Foundation Models With the Metadata You Already Have
summary: We propose a label-free approach to adapt powerful but generic vision foundation models to specialized scientific domains. Standard supervised fine-tuning is often ill-suited to these settings: labels are scarce, and task-specific training can collapse the model's generality and hurt robustness. We instead leverage metadata to adapt representations to new domains in a self-supervised manner. Our method, FINO, combines a standard self-supervised objective with flexible metadata guidance that handles both highly granular discrete metadata and continuous metadata. It encourages the representation to preserve informative factors while suppressing spurious ones. Across subcellular fluorescence microscopy, Earth observation, wildlife monitoring, and medical imaging, FINO consistently outperforms standard unsupervised domain adaptation and fully supervised adaptation. It also exceeds highly-specialized domain-specific state of the art, while using no task labels for backbone adaptation and only lightweight probes for supervision.

AI.tldr: FINO is a label-free method that uses metadata-guided self-supervision to adapt vision foundation models to specialized scientific domains, outperforming both unsupervised and fully supervised domain adaptation across four scientific imaging domains.
AI.motivation: Standard supervised fine-tuning struggles in specialized scientific domains due to scarce labels and risks collapsing model generality and robustness. The authors propose a label-free alternative that preserves model generality while adapting to new domains.
AI.method: FINO combines a standard self-supervised objective with flexible metadata guidance that handles both discrete and continuous metadata, encouraging representations to preserve informative factors while suppressing spurious ones.
AI.result: FINO consistently outperforms standard unsupervised domain adaptation and fully supervised adaptation across subcellular fluorescence microscopy, Earth observation, wildlife monitoring, and medical imaging. It exceeds specialized domain-specific SOTA using no task labels for backbone adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Metadata-guided self-supervision is an effective alternative to supervised fine-tuning for adapting foundation models to scientific domains, achieving strong performance without requiring task labels.

title: Curvature-aware dynamic precision approach for physics-informed neural networks
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have become a promising framework for simulating partial differential equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws directly into neural network training. However, recent studies show that PINN optimisation is sensitive to numerical precision. Existing implementations commonly use either single precision (FP32), which is computationally efficient but prone to failure modes, or double precision (FP64), which is robust but substantially expensive. This creates a trade-off between computational efficiency and numerical accuracy. To reduce the computational cost of double-precision training while retaining prediction accuracy, we propose a curvature-aware precision controller that adapts numerical precision during training rather than treating it as a fixed implementation choice. The proposed method reuses curvature information derived from the limited-memory BFGS (L-BFGS) optimiser to construct a precision controller, retaining FP32 when lower precision is sufficient and promoting computation to FP64 when the training dynamics indicate numerical sensitivity or precision-limited stagnation. We evaluate the proposed approach on four canonical PINN failure-mode benchmarks and an irradiance-driven ordinary differential equation example. We further test the proposed approach across different neural network architectures. The method consistently matches or even slightly exceeds full FP64 solution accuracy while reducing training time relative to full double-precision training on all benchmark equations. The obtained results indicate that precision sensitivity in PINN optimisation is phase-dependent, and that selectively applying higher precision only during numerically critical stages can lower computational cost without sacrificing predictive accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a curvature-aware precision controller that adaptively switches between FP32 and FP64 precision during PINN training, matching FP64 accuracy while reducing computational cost by using higher precision only during numerically critical phases.
AI.motivation: PINN optimization is sensitive to numerical precision, creating a trade-off between FP32 (efficient but prone to failures) and FP64 (robust but expensive). The goal is to reduce computational cost while retaining prediction accuracy.
AI.method: A precision controller that reuses curvature information from the L-BFGS optimizer to adaptively switch precision: FP32 when sufficient, FP64 when training dynamics indicate numerical sensitivity or precision-limited stagnation.
AI.result: The method matches or exceeds full FP64 accuracy on four canonical PINN failure-mode benchmarks and an ODE example, while reducing training time across different architectures.
AI.conclusion: Precision sensitivity in PINN training is phase-dependent; selectively applying higher precision only during critical numerical stages reduces cost without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Continual Visual and Verbal Learning Through a Child's Egocentric Input
summary: Children learn the meanings of words from a continuous, temporally structured stream of egocentric experience. Recent work shows that neural networks can also learn word-referent mappings from a child's egocentric video recordings, but they cycle through the shuffled data for hundreds of epochs, contrasting with how children actually encounter their environment. We introduce BabyCL, a continual multimodal learning framework that processes the SAYCam dataset in a single chronological pass, combining streaming visual representation learning with an image-text contrastive objective. BabyCL combines a multi-stage temporal segmentation of the stream with a dual replay buffer that independently manages visual and multimodal histories, and it is jointly trained with three contrastive losses on a shared backbone. Under a matched optimization budget, BabyCL outperforms streaming learning baselines on the SAYCam Labeled-S 4AFC benchmark, substantially narrowing the gap to an upper bound of offline training. Ablations show that the gains are robust to the length of the online temporal segmentation window and the eviction rule of the replay buffer. Together, these results show that meaningful word-referent mappings can emerge under training conditions much closer to a child's actual experience.

AI.tldr: BabyCL is a continual multimodal learning framework that processes egocentric video data in a single chronological pass, using temporal segmentation and dual replay buffers to learn word-referent mappings under conditions closer to how children actually learn.
AI.motivation: Previous neural network models learn word-referent mappings from child egocentric video but cycle through shuffled data for hundreds of epochs, unlike children who experience temporally continuous streams. The authors aim to close this gap by training under more realistic conditions.
AI.method: BabyCL processes SAYCam dataset in a single chronological pass using: (1) multi-stage temporal segmentation of the stream, (2) dual replay buffer managing visual and multimodal histories independently, and (3) joint training with three contrastive losses on a shared backbone.
AI.result: BabyCL outperforms streaming learning baselines on SAYCam Labeled-S 4AFC benchmark, narrowing the gap to offline training. Gains are robust to temporal window length and replay buffer eviction rules.
AI.conclusion: Meaningful word-referent mappings can emerge under training conditions much closer to a child's actual experience, suggesting more ecologically valid learning paradigms are feasible.

title: An Empirical Audit of Input Encoders for Multi-Channel Signal Transformers
summary: Transformers consuming multi-channel scalar signals must embed $C$ simultaneous values into one $d_{\text{model}}$-dimensional vector per time step. We empirically audit eight input encoders -- spanning a shared-scalar baseline, per-channel linear projections, an orthogonality regulariser, a nonlinear MLP stem, block-partitioned concatenation, channel-independent and channel-as-token architectures, and a projected positional encoding -- on a synthetic benchmark designed to make channel identity informative and on ETTh1 as a real-data check, measured in next-step negative log-likelihood (NLL). The headline is one of practical near-equivalence within a wide "top tier": the standard per-channel linear projection (nn.Linear(C, $d_{\text{model}}$)) matches every alternative in that tier up to small, statistically real but practically modest, differences. Two encoders lose decisively: the shared-scalar baseline, which collapses for information-theoretic reasons we make explicit, and the channel-independent PatchTST-spirit baseline, which underperforms on both benchmarks and overfits universally on the synthetic one. Paired tests resolve two small gaps: projecting the sinusoidal positional encoding through a learned linear layer edges the rest at small $C$, with a direct geometric probe showing the mechanism is positional-channel orthogonalisation; a nonlinear MLP stem edges them at the largest $C$ we test, with the gap shrinking under more training data. The practical recommendation is to use nn.Linear(C, $d_{\text{model}}$) by default and reach for something more elaborate only when the task at hand gives a real reason to do so. Code and data to reproduce every experiment in this paper are available at https://github.com/OssiLehtinen/channel-encoder-audit

AI.tldr: This paper audits eight input encoders for transformers processing multi-channel scalar signals, finding that the standard per-channel linear projection (nn.Linear(C, d_model)) performs nearly as well as any alternative, with only minor differences in edge cases. The shared-scalar baseline and channel-independent PatchTST-style encoder perform decisively worse.
AI.motivation: Transformers must encode multiple simultaneous scalar channel signals into a single d_model-dimensional vector per time step, but it's unclear which input encoder design is optimal. The paper systematically compares eight encoder variants to resolve this question.
AI.method: The authors empirically audit eight input encoders on a synthetic benchmark designed to make channel identity informative and on ETTh1 real data, using next-step negative log-likelihood (NLL) as the metric. Encoders include shared-scalar baseline, per-channel linear projection, orthogonality regulariser, nonlinear MLP stem, block-partitioned concatenation, channel-independent, channel-as-token, and projected positional encoding.
AI.result: The standard per-channel linear projection (nn.Linear(C, d_model)) matches every alternative in the top tier with only small, practically modest differences. Two encoders lose decisively: the shared-scalar baseline and the channel-independent PatchTST-spirit baseline. Small gaps exist: projected positional encoding edges others at small C, while nonlinear MLP stem edges them at large C.
AI.conclusion: The practical recommendation is to use nn.Linear(C, d_model) by default and reach for something more elaborate only when the task specifically warrants it.

title: GeM-NR: Geometry-Aware Multi-View Editing for Nonrigid Scene Changes
summary: Recent developments in multi-view image editing with generative models have brought us a step closer toward general 3D content generation and customization. Most existing works focus on rigid or appearance-only edits by utilizing the geometry of the unedited scene. This naturally limits these methods to edits that preserve the underlying scene structure. Other approaches are trained for specific image editing tasks, such as object removal and addition. Despite this progress, general nonrigid edits, i.e., edits that substantially change the scene geometry, remain challenging for existing methods. We propose GeM-NR, a fast and flexible training-free approach for general multi-view consistent image editing, including edits that drastically change the geometry and appearance of the scene. Given an anchor image edited with a chosen backbone editor (such as FLUX, Qwen, BrushNet) and a query unedited image, GeM-NR edits the query image consistently with the anchor edit. The method incorporates multiple stages: (i) depth map estimation, where we propose a strategy to maximize the alignment between the 3D point clouds of the edited and unedited scenes, (ii) projection onto a query viewpoint, and (iii) refinement of the obtained image conditioned on the unedited query. The conditioning-based formulation scales well from two to many views of an object. We demonstrate the ability of our method to handle edits with significant changes in geometry and appearance, something that existing methods struggle with. We perform an extensive evaluation showing that our method improves consistency for a wide variety of edit tasks, including generating 3D representations of the edited scene. Both quantitative and qualitative results indicate the state-of-the-art performance of our method in terms of edit quality as well as geometric and photometric consistency across multiple views.

AI.tldr: GeM-NR is a training-free method for multi-view consistent image editing that handles nonrigid edits (changes in geometry and appearance) by using depth alignment, viewpoint projection, and conditioning-based refinement, achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse edit tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-view image editing methods are limited to rigid or appearance-only edits due to reliance on unedited scene geometry, or are trained for specific tasks like object removal. General nonrigid edits that substantially change scene geometry remain challenging.
AI.method: GeM-NR operates in three stages: (i) depth map estimation with a strategy to maximize 3D point cloud alignment between edited and unedited scenes, (ii) projection onto the query viewpoint, and (iii) refinement conditioned on the unedited query image. It uses a backbone editor (e.g., FLUX, Qwen, BrushNet) for the anchor edit and is training-free.
AI.result: The method handles edits with significant geometry and appearance changes, scales from two to many views, and shows state-of-the-art performance in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations for edit quality, geometric consistency, and photometric consistency.
AI.conclusion: GeM-NR enables general nonrigid multi-view consistent editing without training, outperforming existing methods on diverse tasks including generating 3D representations of edited scenes.

title: Beyond Structural Symmetries: Linear Mode Connectivity via Neuron Identifiability
summary: Many striking phenomena in deep learning, such as linear mode connectivity and the structured behavior of training dynamics, are closely tied to parameter symmetries: transformations that leave the realized function unchanged. Despite growing attention to parameter symmetries, the exact interplay between parameters, data, and representations remains underexplored. To investigate this, we develop a theoretical framework of effective function classes, i.e., the set of functions a neuron can realize on its input support, and the norm cost of realizing them. We then formalize effective symmetry breaking via neuron identifiability across independent training runs. Our analysis shows that neural networks can admit large families of approximately equivalent solutions even in structurally asymmetric models. We further show that neuron identifiability enables representation merging without prior alignment, and characterize when such merging admits a linear low-loss path. These findings highlight the role of effective function classes in affecting the loss landscape.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a theoretical framework of effective function classes to study parameter symmetries in deep learning, showing that neural networks admit large families of approximately equivalent solutions and that neuron identifiability enables representation merging without prior alignment.
AI.motivation: Despite growing attention to parameter symmetries (transformations leaving the realized function unchanged), the exact interplay between parameters, data, and representations remains underexplored.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework of effective function classes (the set of functions a neuron can realize on its input support and the norm cost of realizing them), and formalize effective symmetry breaking via neuron identifiability across independent training runs.
AI.result: 1) Neural networks can admit large families of approximately equivalent solutions even in structurally asymmetric models. 2) Neuron identifiability enables representation merging without prior alignment. 3) The conditions under which such merging admits a linear low-loss path are characterized.
AI.conclusion: Effective function classes play a significant role in shaping the loss landscape of deep neural networks.

title: Measuring Model Robustness via Fisher Information: Spectral Bounds, Theoretical Guarantees, and Practical Algorithms
summary: The robustness of deep neural networks is crucial for safety-critical deployments, yet existing evaluation methods are often attack-dependent and lack interpretability. We propose a principled, attack-agnostic robustness metric based on the spectral norm of the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM), which quantifies the worst-case sensitivity of the model's output distribution to input perturbations. Theoretically, we establish that the FIM equals the variance of the input Jacobian and derive closed-form spectral bounds for common architectures, including VGG, ResNet, DenseNet, and Transformer, providing the first theoretical robustness ranking. To enable scalable evaluation, we develop efficient algorithms, including power iteration and Hutchinson-based estimation, that support both white-box and black-box settings. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, including CIFAR, ImageNet, and medical images, and across multiple architectures show a strong correlation between our metric and adversarial vulnerability. Our framework serves as an interpretable diagnostic tool that complements attack-based evaluations, offering insights into architectural sensitivity and guiding the design of more robust models. Code is available at: https://github.com/franz-chang/SRP/.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new robustness metric for deep neural networks based on the spectral norm of the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM), which measures worst-case output sensitivity to input perturbations. The authors provide theoretical bounds for common architectures, develop efficient estimation algorithms (power iteration, Hutchinson-based), and empirically validate strong correlation with adversarial vulnerability across multiple datasets (CIFAR, ImageNet, medical images) and architectures (VGG, ResNet, DenseNet, Transformer). The method serves as an attack-agnostic, interpretable diagnostic tool.
AI.motivation: Existing robustness evaluation methods are often attack-dependent and lack interpretability. A principled, attack-agnostic metric is needed to assess and compare model robustness reliably.
AI.method: The authors propose using the spectral norm of the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) as a robustness metric. They derive theoretical closed-form spectral bounds for VGG, ResNet, DenseNet, and Transformer architectures, and develop efficient estimation algorithms (power iteration, Hutchinson-based) for both white-box and black-box settings.
AI.result: Strong correlation is demonstrated between the proposed FIM-based metric and adversarial vulnerability across multiple datasets (CIFAR, ImageNet, medical images) and architectures. The first theoretical robustness ranking is provided for common architectures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework serves as an interpretable, attack-agnostic diagnostic tool that complements existing attack-based evaluations, offering insights into architectural sensitivity and guiding the design of more robust models.

title: Controllable Dynamic 3D Shape Generation via 3D Trajectories and Text
summary: We introduce T2Mo, a feed-forward framework for controllable dynamic 3D shape generation conditioned on 3D trajectories and text. Due to the inherent ambiguity of language, generating precisely intended motions using text alone remains challenging. To address this, we adopt 3D trajectories as controllable spatial guidance, specifying the exact paths along which selected points should move. By combining both, T2Mo generates object motions that spatially adhere to the given trajectories while globally reflecting the text semantics. To robustly handle trajectory inputs with arbitrary configurations, ranging from dense to sparse and unevenly distributed, we further propose a shape-grounded trajectory embedding that maps an input trajectory set into a shape-aware token set covering the entire object. We conduct extensive comparisons against text-based baselines and cascaded video-based baselines that combine trajectory-guided video generation with video-to-dynamic mesh generation. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations, along with user studies, demonstrate that our approach produces motions that more faithfully follow the given prompts with higher expressiveness while preserving motion quality.
