Empirically, IGA outperforms eight baselines across four benchmarks with accuracy gains up to 14.3 pp over ERM-SFT and a Logical Consistency Score of 0.031 versus 0.142 -- a fourfold improvement in representational invariance.

AI.tldr: IGA introduces a gradient alignment framework that trains LLMs on logically isomorphic examples across diverse semantic domains, using gradient conflict masking and SVD projection to achieve up to 14.3 pp accuracy gains and a fourfold improvement in logical consistency over standard methods.
AI.motivation: LLMs suffer from shortcut learning, failing on out-of-distribution inputs with similar logic but different surface semantics. This undermines reasoning distillation pipelines.
AI.method: IGA uses (i) Logical Isomer Sets (same logic, different domains), (ii) a Continuous Gradient Conflict Mask that suppresses high-variance gradient dimensions, and (iii) truncated SVD projection onto the LoRA manifold for parameter efficiency.
AI.result: Outperforms eight baselines with up to 14.3 pp accuracy gains over ERM-SFT and Logical Consistency Score of 0.031 vs 0.142 (fourfold improvement).
AI.conclusion: IGA yields tighter OOD generalization bounds than ERM, scales with number of isomer domains, and converges at standard SGD rate under mild regularity.

title: Validity Threats for Foundation Model Research
summary: Controlled experiments are the backbone of machine learning research, but at the scale of modern foundation models, they have become prohibitively expensive. Instead, the community increasingly relies on research strategies that approximate the ideal experiment at a fraction of the cost: proxy experiments and scaling laws, observational studies with publicly available models, and single-run designs that leverage variation within individual training runs. In this work, we argue that there is no free lunch when approximating large-scale experiments on a compute budget. Specifically, savings in compute come at the cost of validity threats -- hidden and sometimes untestable assumptions that, when violated, can invalidate research claims. To help navigate such threats, we propose an evaluation framework that casts foundation model research as a causal inference problem. Within this framework, we evaluate different research strategies through four types of validity adapted from the empirical social sciences -- statistical, internal, external, and construct validity. We find that each strategy comes with a characteristic validity profile: proxy experiments trade external and construct validity for statistical and internal validity; observational studies face confounding and effect heterogeneity; and single-run designs are strained by interference between treated units. This analysis reveals several validity threats that have received insufficient attention in the literature. Overall, our evaluation framework provides researchers with a practical toolkit for scrutinizing validity threats in foundation model research~designs.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that cost-saving approximations of large-scale experiments in foundation model research introduce validity threats, and proposes a causal inference framework with four validity types (statistical, internal, external, construct) to evaluate common research strategies.
AI.motivation: Controlled experiments for modern foundation models have become prohibitively expensive, forcing researchers to rely on cheaper approximations. However, these trade-offs introduce hidden validity threats that can undermine research claims.
AI.method: The authors cast foundation model research as a causal inference problem and evaluate three research strategies (proxy experiments, observational studies, single-run designs) through four types of validity adapted from empirical social sciences: statistical, internal, external, and construct validity.
AI.result: Each strategy has a characteristic validity profile: proxy experiments trade external/construct validity for statistical/internal validity; observational studies face confounding and effect heterogeneity; single-run designs suffer from interference between treated units.
AI.conclusion: There is no free lunch in approximating large-scale experiments. The framework provides a practical toolkit for researchers to scrutinize validity threats in foundation model research designs, highlighting insufficiently addressed threats.

title: In-Context Graphical Inference
summary: Marginal inference in discrete graphical models forces a choice between exactness and scalability: exact algorithms are intractable for high-treewidth graphs, while iterative approximations (Belief Propagation, variational methods) sacrifice convergence guarantees on frustrated topologies. We argue that this dichotomy stems from a mismatched inductive bias: iterative methods abandon the sequential elimination structure that makes exact inference correct. We introduce In-Context Graphical Inference (ICG-I), an autoregressive Graph Transformer that restores this structure by mimicking Variable Elimination with learned, Tensor- Train-compressed intermediate factors, paired with a Dirichlet output layer and Weighted Conformal Prediction for calibrated, distribution-free coverage guarantees under topological shift. We prove that TT compression errors propagate at most lincarly through the autoregressive chain, that the Dirichlet-Multinomial loss is a proper scoring rule, and that WCP maintains coverage with a quantifiable degradation under estimated density ratios. We conducted intensive experiments to evaluate ICG-I and achieved state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks. ICG-I reduces MAE from 0.041 (best baseline) to 0.020 on standard instances and achieves 0.048 on N=500 frustrated spin glasses where BP diverges entirely.

AI.tldr: ICG-I is an autoregressive Graph Transformer that performs marginal inference in graphical models by mimicking Variable Elimination with Tensor-Train compression, achieving SOTA results including handling frustrated spin glasses where Belief Propagation diverges.
AI.motivation: The dichotomy between exact (intractable for high treewidth) and approximate (no convergence guarantees on frustrated topologies) inference methods stems from a mismatched inductive bias—iterative methods abandon the sequential elimination structure that makes exact inference correct.
AI.method: ICG-I is an autoregressive Graph Transformer that mimics Variable Elimination with learned Tensor-Train-compressed intermediate factors, uses a Dirichlet output layer, and applies Weighted Conformal Prediction for calibrated, distribution-free coverage guarantees under topological shift.
AI.result: ICG-I reduces MAE from 0.041 (best baseline) to 0.020 on standard instances and achieves 0.048 on N=500 frustrated spin glasses where BP diverges entirely, achieving SOTA across all benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Tensor-Train compression errors propagate at most linearly through the autoregressive chain, the Dirichlet-Multinomial loss is a proper scoring rule, and WCP maintains coverage with quantifiable degradation under estimated density ratios.

title: Graph Cascades: Contagion-Based Mesoscopic Rewiring for Structure-Aware Graph Machine Learning
summary: We introduce Graph Cascades, a mesoscopic rewiring strategy for Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Graph Transformers (GTs) that captures intermediate-scale graph structure beyond purely local edges or fully global attention. Using contagion-based diffusion processes, Graph Cascades constructs, in O(|V|+|E|) time, an auxiliary graph where node pairs supported by repeated multi-hop reinforcement are promoted to direct neighbors. We theoretically characterize when reinforcement-based rewiring helps: sufficient conditions under which reinforcement-based edge selection is more label-aligned than direct adjacency, an SBM witness in which two-hop reinforcement is perfectly homophilic, and a formalization of mesoscopic connectivity via graph effective resistance. Empirically, across node-classification benchmarks, Graph Cascades improves multiple GNN and sparse-GT backbones, with the most reliable gains observed on heterophilic and moderate- to high-degree homophilic graphs. The theoretical conditions also identify regimes where mesoscopic rewiring is unlikely to be beneficial -- low-degree regular graphs and graphs with structural bottlenecks -- and these predictions match the observed failures. We additionally observe tight correlations between performance and structural properties in the rewired graphs.

AI.tldr: Graph Cascades is a mesoscopic rewiring method for GNNs/GTs that uses contagion-based diffusion to build auxiliary graphs capturing intermediate-scale structure, with theoretical guarantees and empirical gains on heterophilic and moderate-to-high degree homophilic graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing GNNs rely on purely local edges or fully global attention, missing intermediate-scale (mesoscopic) graph structure. The authors aim to bridge this gap by developing an efficient rewiring strategy that captures multi-hop reinforcement patterns.
AI.method: Graph Cascades uses contagion-based diffusion processes to construct, in O(|V|+|E|) time, an auxiliary graph where node pairs with repeated multi-hop reinforcement become direct neighbors. Theoretical analysis via SBM and effective resistance formalizes when reinforcement helps.
AI.result: Empirically, Graph Cascades improves multiple GNN and sparse-GT backbones, especially on heterophilic and moderate-to-high degree homophilic graphs. Theoretical predictions of failure cases (low-degree regular graphs, structural bottlenecks) match observed failures.
AI.conclusion: Mesoscopic rewiring via contagion-based diffusion is a principled, efficient way to improve GNN performance, with clear theoretical boundaries on when it is beneficial vs. not.

title: FLAGG: Flexible Autoregressive Graph Generation
comment: Accepted for publication at JMLR, currently in press
summary: The Deep Graph Generation's panorama spans two extremes: one-shot and sequential models. The former generates nodes and edges jointly, while the latter samples them autoregressively. Each method performs better in different graph domains depending on size and topology, but neither is applicable to all graph categories. For instance, one-shot methods struggle with generating large graphs, while sequential methods underperform on smaller graphs. A possible way to overcome these limitations is to flexibly combine the two methods in a unique system. In this work, we propose the FLAGG (Flexible Autoregressive Graph Generation) framework, which sequentially generates portions of graphs with one-shot models. FLAGG can apply any one-shot model to make it autoregressive, allowing flexibility in choosing the sequential policy. This policy is specified through a stochastic node removal process, which an Insertion Model learns to reverse. We evaluate FLAGG with the DiGress one-shot model on several data sets of different graph sizes and domains. We show that the approach outperforms both one-shot and autoregressive baselines in terms of sampling quality.

AI.tldr: FLAGG combines one-shot and sequential graph generation by using one-shot models autoregressively, outperforming both approaches individually across different graph sizes and domains.
AI.motivation: One-shot and sequential graph generation methods each have limitations—one-shot struggles with large graphs, sequential underperforms on small graphs—and neither is universally applicable across all graph categories.
AI.method: FLAGG (Flexible Autoregressive Graph Generation) sequentially generates graph portions using one-shot models, with a stochastic node removal process defining the sequential policy that an Insertion Model learns to reverse.
AI.result: FLAGG with DiGress one-shot model outperforms both one-shot and autoregressive baselines in sampling quality across multiple datasets of varying sizes and domains.
AI.conclusion: Flexibly combining one-shot and sequential methods in a unified framework (FLAGG) overcomes individual limitations and achieves superior graph generation performance.

title: RIDE: An Open Dataset and Benchmark for Train Delay Prediction
summary: Train delay prediction is an important problem for both passengers and railway operators, yet progress in the field remains difficult to assess due to the lack of standardized datasets, prediction targets, and evaluation protocols. To address this gap, we introduce RIDE, an open dataset and benchmark for train delay prediction built at nationwide scale over the Belgian railway network. RIDE covers 94.5M train events, 3.6M journeys, and 35.7M weather records from 2023 to 2025. It is organized as a layered data pipeline from raw railway and weather sources to two public releases: a reusable intermediate relational dataset and model-ready benchmark datasets. The benchmark standardizes the prediction task and the training and testing data. It also provides a unified evaluation protocol that supports direct comparison across models. Using this framework, we provide the first comprehensive comparative evaluation of non-learning, statistical learning, and deep learning models. We show that learning-based methods clearly outperform non-learning models, with graph neural networks achieving the best mean performance, while the strongest learning-based models remain relatively close to one another. Beyond aggregate mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean squared error (RMSE), the framework also provides breakdowns by prediction horizon and delay change, enabling more detailed analysis of model behavior across forecasting regimes.

AI.tldr: RIDE is a large-scale open benchmark for train delay prediction on the Belgian railway network, standardizing datasets, prediction targets, and evaluation protocols, with findings that learning-based models (especially GNNs) outperform non-learning approaches.
AI.motivation: Progress in train delay prediction is hard to assess due to the lack of standardized datasets, prediction targets, and evaluation protocols.
AI.method: Built a layered data pipeline from raw railway and weather sources (94.5M train events, 3.6M journeys, 35.7M weather records, 2023–2025) into two public releases: an intermediate relational dataset and model-ready benchmarks with standardized tasks and evaluation protocols.
AI.result: Learning-based models outperform non-learning models; graph neural networks achieve the best mean performance, though top learning models are relatively close. Framework provides breakdowns by prediction horizon and delay change beyond MAE/RMSE.
AI.conclusion: RIDE provides a unified evaluation framework enabling fair and detailed comparisons across models, advancing reproducible research in train delay prediction.

title: Learning What Not to Impute: An Uncertainty-Aware Diffusion Framework for Meaningful Missingness
summary: Missing value imputation is a fundamental task in machine learning, with most existing methods assuming that all missing entries correspond to unobserved regular values. In many real-world datasets, however, missingness may arise from two distinct sources: some entries are meaningfully missing (intrinsically absent and semantically valid), while others are missing due to the observation process and should be imputed. We formalize this distinction as a selective imputation problem, where the goal is to jointly infer which missing entries should be preserved and which should be recovered. To address this challenge, we propose Diff-Joint, a diffusion-based framework that jointly models tabular data together with a latent missingness mask. The method alternates between conditional sampling and uncertainty-aware aggregation to iteratively refine both imputed values and missingness labels. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that Diff-Joint effectively identifies meaningfully missing entries while achieving competitive imputation accuracy and improved downstream task performance.

AI.tldr: Diff-Joint is a diffusion-based framework that jointly models tabular data and latent missingness masks to distinguish between meaningfully missing entries (preserved) and observation-process missing entries (imputed), achieving competitive imputation and improved downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Existing missing value imputation methods assume all missing entries are unobserved regular values, but real-world data often has two distinct missingness sources: entries that are intrinsically absent (should be preserved) vs. entries missing due to observation process (should be imputed).
AI.method: Diff-Joint: a diffusion-based framework that jointly models tabular data with a latent missingness mask, alternating between conditional sampling and uncertainty-aware aggregation to iteratively refine both imputed values and missingness labels.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate effective identification of meaningfully missing entries, competitive imputation accuracy, and improved downstream task performance.
AI.conclusion: The distinction between meaningfully missing and observation-process missing entries is important; Diff-Joint effectively solves this selective imputation problem.

title: RePercENT: Scaling Disentangled Representation Learning Beyond Two Modalities
summary: To leverage the full potential of multimodal data, we need representations that go beyond the state-of-the-art alignment and fusion approaches and exploit all cross-modal interactions without sacrificing modality-specific information. Learning disentangled representations is a principled way to identify these underlying shared and unique factors that are hidden in observational data. However, while multimodal disentanglement is a compelling paradigm, existing methods are largely confined to the two-modality regime due to its inherent scalability bottleneck. To address this, we propose RePercENT, a self-supervised framework designed to surpass these limitations and unlocks scalable pairwise disentanglement beyond two modalities. Through a multimodal `plug-and-play' architecture, our approach operates directly on pre-extracted embeddings, eliminating the need for extensive joint pre-training while making no assumptions regarding the underlying modalities or foundation model backbones. Moreover, we introduce a joint optimization objective for simultaneously deriving the shared and unique components, and provide formal theoretical guarantees that characterize the optimality of our solution. Across diverse modalities and tasks, RePercENT successfully recovers disentangled components while maintaining competitive performance and significantly reducing computational complexity.

AI.tldr: RePercENT is a self-supervised framework for scalable multimodal disentanglement that works beyond two modalities via a plug-and-play architecture operating on pre-extracted embeddings, eliminating joint pre-training while providing theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal disentanglement methods are largely limited to two modalities due to scalability bottlenecks, preventing exploitation of cross-modal interactions in datasets with more than two modalities.
AI.method: RePercENT uses a plug-and-play architecture operating on pre-extracted embeddings (no joint pre-training needed), with a joint optimization objective that simultaneously derives shared and unique components across multiple modalities, backed by theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: Across diverse modalities and tasks, RePercENT successfully recovers disentangled components, maintains competitive performance, and significantly reduces computational complexity compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Scalable pairwise disentanglement beyond two modalities is achievable without extensive joint pre-training, enabling practical multimodal representation learning with reduced computational cost.

title: Graph Set Transformer
summary: We introduce the Graph Set Transformer (GST), a neural network architecture for learning on sets of graphs, designed for tasks in which per-element predictions depend on set-wide context as well as local structure. Existing architectures, including DeepSets and SetTransformer, require pre-encoded graph embeddings from a separate GNN, creating a bottleneck between feature extraction and set-level contextualisation. In contrast, GST interleaves node-level feature propagation and cross-graph contextual modelling at every layer, fusing the two levels of information through a gating mechanism. We evaluate GST on a controlled synthetic suite designed to isolate set-conditional structural reasoning and on three real-data benchmarks spanning per-atom reaction-centre identification, reaction yield prediction, and image classification. Under matched parameter budgets, GST performs better than the baselines across these settings. An architectural ablation strongly suggests that the interleaving of local and set context contributes substantially to this advantage.

AI.tldr: The Graph Set Transformer (GST) is a new architecture for learning on sets of graphs that interleaves node-level feature propagation and cross-graph contextual modeling at every layer, outperforming baselines on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing architectures (DeepSets, SetTransformer) require pre-encoded graph embeddings from a separate GNN, creating a bottleneck between feature extraction and set-level contextualization. GST aims to fuse these two levels of information more tightly.
AI.method: GST interleaves node-level feature propagation and cross-graph contextual modeling at every layer, fusing the two through a gating mechanism, rather than using separate pre-encoding and set-processing stages.
AI.result: Under matched parameter budgets, GST outperforms baselines on a controlled synthetic suite, per-atom reaction-centre identification, reaction yield prediction, and image classification benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Interleaving local graph structure and set context contributes substantially to GST's advantage over pipeline-based approaches.

title: Towards Efficient and Evidence-grounded Mobility Prediction with LLM-Driven Agent
summary: Individual-level mobility prediction is central to urban simulation, transportation planning, and policy analysis. Supervised sequence models achieve strong accuracy but require task-specific training and offer limited decision-level transparency. Recent LLM-based methods improve interpretability, yet mostly rely on static prompts and single-pass inference, limiting their ability to seek additional evidence when mobility signals are weak or conflicting. We propose \method{}, a training-free LLM-driven agent framework that formulates next-location prediction as adaptive evidence-controlled decision making. \method{} resolves routine cases through a fast path based on historical regularity, while ambiguous cases trigger iterative tool use over recent trajectories, historical behavior, stay-move likelihood, and geographical evidence. Across three mobility datasets, AgentMob achieves the strongest overall performance among training-free LLM-based methods, with GPT-5.4 reaching 71.42\% Acc@1 on BW, 33.14\% on YJMob100K, and 33.50\% on Shanghai ISP. On BW non-fast-path cases, the LLM controller improves Acc@1 from 30.65\% to 48.62\% over a same-tool statistical baseline, showing that its main benefit lies in resolving ambiguous predictions through adaptive evidence gathering. Our code is available at https://github.com/Unknown-zoo/AgentMob.

AI.tldr: AgentMob is a training-free LLM agent framework for next-location prediction that uses adaptive evidence-controlled decision making, combining a fast path for routine cases with iterative tool use for ambiguous ones, achieving strong results across three mobility datasets.
AI.motivation: Supervised sequence models achieve strong accuracy but require task-specific training and lack transparency, while existing LLM methods rely on static prompts and single-pass inference, limiting their ability to gather additional evidence when mobility signals are weak or conflicting.
AI.method: A training-free LLM-driven agent framework with two paths: a fast path based on historical regularity for routine cases, and an iterative tool-use path for ambiguous cases that gathers recent trajectories, historical behavior, stay-move likelihood, and geographical evidence.
AI.result: Achieves 71.42% Acc@1 on BW, 33.14% on YJMob100K, and 33.50% on Shanghai ISP, outperforming other training-free LLM methods. On non-fast-path cases, LLM controller improves Acc@1 from 30.65% to 48.62% over a statistical baseline.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive evidence gathering through iterative tool use is the key benefit for resolving ambiguous predictions, and the training-free approach offers both interpretability and strong performance.

title: Deep Embedded Multiplicative DMD for Algebra-Preserving Koopman Learning
summary: Koopman theory turns nonlinear dynamics into a linear spectral problem. In computation, however, everything depends on a hard finite-dimensional choice: the observables must be expressive, nearly invariant under the dynamics, and, ideally, compatible with composition. Deep Koopman methods learn flexible coordinates, whereas structure-preserving methods enforce operator identities on fixed dictionaries. We combine these ideas by introducing Deep Embedded Multiplicative Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DeepMDMD), a method that learns a latent space and a partition of it, while enforcing the Koopman product rule as an exact algebraic constraint. Training alternates between an exact multiplicative operator update and a differentiable latent-clustering step that promotes Koopman closure. The result is a finite transition map on learned latent cells. Its nonzero spectrum lies on the unit circle, its dictionary is shaped by the dynamics rather than by ambient geometry, and forecasts are made in latent coordinates before being decoded to physical space. Across Hamiltonian, chaotic, and fluid examples, DeepMDMD learns dictionaries that are far more compact and dynamically coherent than those produced by geometric MDMD partitions. It reduces spectral pollution, reveals richer continuous-spectrum structure, and gives stable forecasts under severe noise. In high-dimensional flows, including a 158,624-dimensional cylinder wake and a noisy $Re=20,000$ lid-driven cavity, it preserves coherent structures and long-time spectral statistics where state-space MDMD fails. These results suggest a practical rule for Koopman learning: learn the coordinates, constrain the algebra.

AI.tldr: DeepMDMD combines deep learning with Koopman theory by learning a latent space and partition while enforcing the Koopman product rule as an exact algebraic constraint, producing compact, dynamically coherent dictionaries for stable forecasting.
AI.motivation: Existing Koopman methods face a trade-off: deep learning offers flexible coordinates but lacks structure preservation, while structure-preserving methods enforce operator identities on fixed, often suboptimal dictionaries. The paper aims to combine both strengths.
AI.method: DeepMDMD alternates between an exact multiplicative operator update and a differentiable latent-clustering step that enforces the Koopman product rule as an exact algebraic constraint, learning a partition-aware latent space for a finite transition map.
AI.result: DeepMDMD learns more compact and dynamically coherent dictionaries than geometric MDMD partitions, reduces spectral pollution, reveals richer continuous-spectrum structure, provides stable forecasts under severe noise, and scales to high-dimensional flows (e.g., 158,624-dimensional cylinder wake).
AI.conclusion: The paper suggests a practical rule for Koopman learning: learn the coordinates, constrain the algebra, demonstrating that jointly learning latent representations with algebraic constraints yields superior performance.

title: Failed Reasoning Traces Tell You What Is Fixable (But Not by Reading Them)
summary: When post-trained language models fail on reasoning problems, the common test-time-scaling response is to spend more compute on additional attempts, and the failed traces play no further role. We argue this discards a crucial signal; some failures come from unlucky sampling, where more rollouts help, while others are structural and resist resampling regardless of budget. We propose that failed traces encode recoverability structure: the inference-time signature of which test-time interventions can rescue a given failure. Three problem-level trajectory features, derived from the structure of available interventions, recover this structure from the distributional signature of failed rollouts, not their text. They cluster failures into stable regimes, characterize the failure topography of different post-training methods ($84.3{\pm}4.3\%$ accuracy, $+20\%$ over a majority-class baseline), and support a training-free routing rule that lifts rescue by $+12.2\%$ on the deployment-relevant Steerable-Hard subset (failures where retry is insufficient and a bounded intervention is reachable). The features and the routing rule transfer across two cross-family probes. The same three features thus convert failed traces from discarded data into a diagnostic object, supporting test-time routing and post-training analysis without training-time or weight-space access.

AI.tldr: Failed traces from language models encode recoverability structure through three trajectory features that predict which test-time interventions can rescue failures, enabling training-free routing that improves rescue rates by 12.2% on hard subsets.
AI.motivation: Current practice discards failed reasoning traces entirely, but failures are not all alike—some are recoverable with more sampling while others are structural. The paper aims to extract diagnostic information from failed rollouts to inform test-time interventions.
AI.method: Three problem-level trajectory features are derived from the distributional signature of failed rollouts (not text content). These cluster failures into stable regimes and characterize failure topography across post-training methods. A training-free routing rule is developed to select appropriate interventions.
AI.result: The approach achieves 84.3±4.3% accuracy in characterizing failure topography (+20% over majority baseline). The routing rule lifts rescue by +12.2% on the Steerable-Hard subset. Features and routing rule transfer across two cross-family probes.
AI.conclusion: Failed traces should be treated as diagnostic objects rather than discarded data. Three simple features can support test-time routing and post-training analysis without requiring training-time or weight-space access, converting waste into actionable information.

title: Generating Financial Time Series by Matching Random Convolutional Features
summary: Generating realistic financial time series is challenging as training data is often limited to a single historical path. With such scarce data, overfitting is hard to avoid, especially under adversarial training where a trained discriminator can memorize the training samples. To mitigate this, recent approaches train generators to minimize the discrepancy between untrained feature representations of real and generated time series. In these works, the feature maps are based on path signatures, which can fail to capture relevant time series properties at tractable truncation depths. In this work, we instead train generators by matching random convolutional features of real and generated time series. Existing random convolutional feature maps, such as Rocket and Hydra, have been shown to provide informative representations of real-world time series, but cannot supervise generative models because they are non-differentiable. We introduce SOCK (SOft Competing Kernels), a fully differentiable random convolutional feature map, suited to train generative time series models. We show that generators trained by matching random SOCK features consistently outperform signature and diffusion baselines across a wide range of small-sample financial datasets. We further demonstrate SOCK's expressiveness on two-sample hypothesis testing and time series classification tasks, where SOCK matches or outperforms existing unsupervised feature maps.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SOCK, a differentiable random convolutional feature map for training generative time series models that outperforms signature and diffusion baselines on small-sample financial datasets.
AI.motivation: Generating realistic financial time series is challenging due to limited training data (single historical path), leading to overfitting in adversarial training. Existing approaches using path signatures fail to capture relevant properties at tractable truncation depths.
AI.method: The authors propose SOCK (SOft Competing Kernels), a fully differentiable random convolutional feature map that enables training generative models by matching random convolutional features of real and generated time series, overcoming the non-differentiability limitation of existing methods like Rocket and Hydra.
AI.result: Generators trained with SOCK consistently outperform signature and diffusion baselines across diverse small-sample financial datasets. SOCK also matches or outperforms existing unsupervised feature maps on two-sample hypothesis testing and time series classification.
AI.conclusion: SOCK provides a more effective and expressive approach for generative time series modeling with limited data, with broader applicability to hypothesis testing and classification tasks.

title: Reinforcement Learning from Rich Feedback with Distributional DAgger
summary: Reasoning models have advanced rapidly, but the dominant reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) recipe remains surprisingly narrow: sample many responses and reward each with a single bit indicating whether the final answer is correct. Yet many settings provide rich feedback, including execution traces, tool outputs, expert corrections, and model self-evaluations. We study how to use such feedback through a distributional variant of the classic imitation learning algorithm DAgger, where the learner has local access to an expert distribution on states visited by the current policy. This yields a simple forward cross-entropy objective that admits a blackbox expert and whose sequence-level gradient {conduct rich credit assignment by propagating} future expert-student disagreement back to earlier decisions. We show that prior RL with self-distillation objectives based on reverse KL or Jensen-Shannon fail to guarantee monotonic policy improvement: even when the expert has higher reward, their updates may increase probability on worse actions. In contrast, we show that forward cross-entropy admits monotonic policy improvement and enjoys guarantees on regret. We further show that our objective optimizes a lower bound on teacher-weighted likelihood of success, leading to improved Pass@N. Empirically, our approach, DistIL, improves over RLVR and RL with self-distillation baselines across a variety of domains: scientific reasoning, coding, and solving hard mathematical problems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes DistIL, a distributional imitation learning algorithm that uses forward cross-entropy (via a DAgger variant) to leverage rich feedback beyond binary correctness signals, demonstrating monotonic policy improvement and superior performance in reasoning, coding, and math tasks.
AI.motivation: Current RLVR methods are narrow, using only a single correctness bit as reward, ignoring richer feedback like execution traces, tool outputs, and expert corrections that are available in many settings.
AI.method: The authors propose a distributional variant of DAgger, where the learner matches an expert distribution on visited states via a forward cross-entropy objective with blackbox expert access, enabling rich credit assignment through sequence-level gradients.
AI.result: DistIL achieves monotonic policy improvement (unlike reverse KL/Jensen-Shannon based methods) and improves Pass@N; empirically outperforms RLVR and self-distillation baselines on scientific reasoning, coding, and hard math problems.
AI.conclusion: Forward cross-entropy with distributional DAgger is a principled and effective way to leverage rich feedback for policy improvement, with theoretical guarantees stronger than prior RL with self-distillation approaches.

title: BBOmix: A Tabular Benchmark for Hyperparameter Optimization of Unsupervised Biological Representation Learning
summary: The rapid advancement of high-throughput sequencing has led to large, high-dimensional omics datasets. Deep unsupervised learning architectures, particularly Autoencoders (AEs), are increasingly used for dimensionality reduction and representation learning in this domain. However, AEs are highly sensitive to architectural choices and hyperparameters, and unsupervised optimization typically relies on reconstruction loss, which may be a poor proxy for downstream utility. Exhaustive hyperparameter optimization (HPO) is computationally expensive, leading researchers to frequently rely on suboptimal default configurations. To democratize access to large-scale unsupervised HPO research, we introduce $\textbf{BBOmix}$, the first open-source tabular benchmark for unsupervised representation learning on real-world biological data. Our benchmark includes 105,000 evaluations across four AE architectures and seven multi-omics modalities from the TCGA and SCHC datasets. We quantify the correlation between reconstruction loss and downstream task performance and provide an extensive evaluation of state-of-the-art single-fidelity, multi-fidelity, and transfer learning HPO methods, establishing a rigorous baseline for future research in unsupervised biological representation learning.

AI.tldr: Introduces BBOmix, the first open-source tabular benchmark for unsupervised hyperparameter optimization of Autoencoders on real-world biological multi-omics data, with 105,000 evaluations across four architectures and seven datasets.
AI.motivation: Autoencoders are widely used for dimensionality reduction on high-dimensional omics data but are sensitive to hyperparameters. Unsupervised optimization relies on reconstruction loss (often a poor proxy for downstream utility), and exhaustive HPO is computationally expensive, leading to reliance on suboptimal defaults.
AI.method: Created BBOmix benchmark with 105,000 evaluations across 4 AE architectures and 7 multi-omics modalities from TCGA and SCHC datasets. Evaluated correlation between reconstruction loss and downstream task performance. Tested single-fidelity, multi-fidelity, and transfer learning HPO methods.
AI.result: Established a rigorous baseline for unsupervised HPO in biological representation learning, quantifying the weak correlation between reconstruction loss and downstream utility, and benchmarking multiple HPO methods.
AI.conclusion: BBOmix democratizes access to large-scale unsupervised HPO research in biology, providing a standardized benchmark to develop better optimization strategies beyond reconstruction-loss-based tuning.

title: STRIDE: Training Data Attribution via Sparse Recovery from Subset Perturbations
summary: Training Data Attribution (TDA) seeks to trace a model's predictions back to its training data. The gold standard for TDA relies on causal interventions, observing how a model changes when data is added or removed, but repeated retraining is computationally challenging for Large Language Models (LLMs). Consequently, most approaches approximate this effect in the parameter space using gradients. However, tracking gradients across billions of parameters is not only prohibitively expensive but relies on local approximations. In this work, we propose a shift: rather than estimating parameter changes, we model the functional effect of training data in the activation space. We introduce STRIDE (Steering-based Training Data Influence Decomposition), a framework that formulates TDA as a sparse recovery problem in the spirit of compressive sensing. STRIDE learns lightweight "steering operators" that mimic the behavioral shift caused by training on data subsets. By measuring how these operators perturb test predictions, we recover individual training example influences via sparse linear decomposition. STRIDE achieves state-of-the-art for LLM pre-training attribution while being an order of magnitude ($13\times$) faster than previous art. We further validate its practical utility through downstream applications including data selection, data contamination, and qualitative analysis.

AI.tldr: STRIDE proposes a new approach to Training Data Attribution for LLMs by modeling training data influence in activation space using sparse recovery, achieving SOTA results while being 13x faster than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing TDA methods for LLMs are computationally prohibitive due to reliance on gradient tracking across billions of parameters and local approximations.
AI.method: STRIDE shifts from parameter space to activation space, formulating TDA as a sparse recovery problem. It learns lightweight "steering operators" that mimic behavioral shifts from training subsets, then recovers individual example influences via sparse linear decomposition.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art for LLM pre-training attribution with 13x speed improvement over previous methods.
AI.conclusion: STRIDE provides a practical, scalable solution for TDA in LLMs, validated through downstream applications like data selection, contamination analysis, and qualitative analysis.
